**Why it matters.** A first-line/replacement recommendation is a guideline-level comparative claim requiring direct (or networked) comparison against the incumbent therapy plus an appraisal of benefits, harms, and certainty. Inferring superiority over drugs from versus-control trials is a non sequitur and exceeds the stated review question; as written it is an unsupported clinical claim that could misdirect prescribing and is the single most serious problem in the manuscript (likely outright rejection at a clinical journal).

**Suggested action.** Remove the "replace pharmacotherapy / first-line" recommendation from both the Abstract and Discussion. Bound conclusions to what the data support (e.g., "dCBT-I reduced insomnia severity relative to the controls used in the included trials"), explicitly qualified by certainty of evidence, and state that no inference about its place relative to pharmacotherapy is possible without head-to-head comparative data and a formal certainty assessment. If a pharmacotherapy comparison is intended, make it a pre-specified PICO question restricted to head-to-head trials reporting comparative effect estimates and adverse events.

*Consensus: raised by all four reviewers (r1–r4) as critical.*

items.section_ref: Methods & Results — risk of bias / certainty (PRISMA-12, PRISMA-24)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** No risk-of-bias (RoB) assessment is reported at the individual-study (PRISMA-12) or across-study (PRISMA-24) level. The Methods name no appraisal tool or process, and the Results/Discussion report no per-study judgements, no reporting/publication-bias assessment, and no certainty-of-evidence (GRADE) rating.

**Why it matters.** RoB appraisal is the defining feature that distinguishes a systematic review from a narrative summary; without it the "dCBT-I is effective" conclusion cannot be weighted by study quality and small/biased trials are treated as equivalent to rigorous ones. This is especially critical here because the primary outcome (insomnia severity) is self-reported and dCBT-I-versus-waitlist trials are typically unblinded — a combination prone to performance/detection bias that plausibly inflates apparent benefit. The omission undermines every downstream claim, including the strong clinical recommendation built on top of it.

**Suggested action.** Pre-specify and apply a validated tool (Cochrane RoB 2 for RCTs), assessed in duplicate; report per-domain and overall judgements for each trial (traffic-light/summary figure); assess publication/reporting bias where a synthesis supports it; and rate certainty per outcome with GRADE, carrying these judgements explicitly into the synthesis and the strength of conclusions.

*Consensus: raised by all four reviewers; r2/r3/r4 rated critical and r1 major. Consolidated as critical given it is a non-optional, defining SR element.*

**Problem.** The search covered only PubMed. There is no second database (Embase, PsycINFO, CENTRAL/Cochrane), no trial registry (ClinicalTrials.gov, ISRCTN), no grey literature or reference-list/forward-citation searching, and no full search string, search dates, or language/other limits.

**Why it matters.** Single-database searching is a recognized source of retrieval/selection bias and misses a substantial share of eligible trials — many insomnia and psychological-intervention trials are indexed only in PsycINFO/Embase, not PubMed. PRISMA-7 requires a reproducible search of at least one database with dates and limits. As reported, the claim of having captured the evidence base (and locating only "14 trials") is neither credible nor reproducible.

**Suggested action.** Search at least MEDLINE/PubMed plus Embase, PsycINFO, and CENTRAL, together with a trial registry and backward/forward-citation and grey-literature sources (ideally with information-specialist input). Report the full search string for at least one database verbatim, with the dates of coverage, the date last run, and any limits and their justification, supplied as a supplement.

*Consensus: all four reviewers, major.*

**Problem.** The selection process is described only as "Two reviewers screened titles." Screening appears limited to titles, with no abstract or full-text eligibility stage, no statement that screening was independent and in duplicate at each stage, and no conflict-resolution rule. The data-extraction process (who extracted, in duplicate or not, which fields) is also absent.

**Why it matters.** Title-only screening systematically discards eligible studies whose titles are uninformative, biasing the included set, and provides no basis for documenting full-text exclusions with reasons. Omitting the duplicate/full-text process and the extraction method undermines reliability and reproducibility — these are core methodological steps a clinical journal expects.

**Suggested action.** Describe a two-stage cascade (title/abstract, then independent full-text eligibility assessment) performed in duplicate with a stated conflict-resolution rule, recording full-text exclusion reasons for the flow diagram. Add a data-extraction section specifying the items collected (population, intervention, comparator, outcomes/instruments, timepoints, effect estimates) and whether extraction was done in duplicate.

*Consensus: all four reviewers, major.*

**Problem.** No PRISMA flow diagram or narrative of study selection is provided. The Results jump to "Fourteen trials were included" with no counts of records identified, deduplicated, screened, sought for retrieval, full-text assessed, or excluded with reasons.

**Why it matters.** The flow diagram is a required PRISMA 2020 element and the standard evidence that selection was complete and unbiased. Without it the review is not reproducible, the included set is unverifiable, and readers cannot reconstruct how 14 trials were reached or what was excluded and why.

**Suggested action.** Add a PRISMA 2020 flow diagram with numeric counts at each stage and full-text exclusion reasons, and reconcile the numbers in the Results narrative.

*Consensus: all four reviewers, major (r2 categorised as mechanical; consolidated structural).*

**Problem.** No a priori protocol or prospective registration (e.g., PROSPERO) is reported, the Abstract omits any registration statement, adherence to PRISMA 2020 is not stated, and any deviations from a plan are therefore undocumented.

**Why it matters.** Prospective registration guards against post hoc changes to eligibility, outcomes, and analyses and against selective outcome reporting; its absence raises the concern that decisions were outcome-driven — directly relevant given the strong directional conclusion. Registration is increasingly a submission requirement, and PRISMA-2 expects it to appear in the abstract.

**Suggested action.** Register or deposit the protocol (PROSPERO) and report the registration number in both the Abstract and Methods; state adherence to PRISMA 2020. If registration was not prospective, say so explicitly and disclose any deviations from the original plan.

*Consensus: dedicated items from r1 and r2; folded into other items by r3 (abstract) and r4 (eligibility). Major.*

**Problem.** Synthesis is purely narrative ("summarised narratively"; "Effect sizes varied across studies"). There is no pre-specified outcome/effect metric, no per-study effect estimates with confidence intervals, no measure of heterogeneity/consistency (I²/τ²), no exploration of why effects varied, no plan for handling different outcome instruments or control conditions, and no justification for a narrative-only (vs meta-analytic) approach.

**Why it matters.** PRISMA-14 requires the synthesis method and, where applicable, measures of consistency. Stating that effect sizes "varied" without quantifying that variation gives readers no way to judge direction, magnitude, precision, or consistency, and a bare narrative masks important differences. Concluding uniformly that dCBT-I is "effective" while acknowledging that effects "varied" is internally inconsistent.

**Suggested action.** Pre-specify the primary outcome and effect metric (e.g., SMD on insomnia severity / ISI). If trials are sufficiently comparable, meta-analyse and report I² with subgroup/sensitivity analyses for heterogeneity; if not, follow a structured synthesis-without-meta-analysis method (SWiM), justify why pooling was not done, and report standardized effect direction/magnitude with CIs, grouped by comparator type and outcome measure.

*Consensus: all four reviewers, major.*

**Problem.** The Abstract is unstructured and omits most required elements: eligibility criteria, the comparator, number of databases/sources and records screened/selected, study-selection and synthesis methods, numerical results (participants, effect estimates, uncertainty), limitations/certainty, and registration. It also carries the unsupported pharmacotherapy-replacement claim.

**Why it matters.** Most clinicians, screeners, and database users act only on the abstract; an incomplete one that leads with an overreaching recommendation propagates the manuscript's core problems to its widest audience, misrepresents the review, and fails PRISMA-2 (PRISMA-for-abstracts).

**Suggested action.** Rewrite as a structured PRISMA-for-abstracts summary: objective (PICO), data sources and search dates, eligibility criteria, number of studies and participants, RoB and synthesis methods, principal quantitative results with measures of uncertainty and certainty, limitations, registration number, and a conclusion bounded by the comparator-limited evidence.

*Consensus: all four reviewers, major (r2 categorised as mechanical; consolidated structural).*

**Problem.** The objective — "We systematically reviewed the effectiveness of dCBT-I in adults" — is not an explicit, answerable PICO question; the comparator(s) and the specific outcomes/timepoints that define "effectiveness" are unstated.

**Why it matters.** PRISMA-4 requires an answerable PICO objective that anchors eligibility, synthesis, and interpretation. The vague objective lets the review drift toward the unsupported pharmacotherapy/first-line claim because no comparator or outcome was ever pinned down.

**Suggested action.** Restate as a precise PICO question, e.g., "In adults with chronic insomnia (P), does dCBT-I (I) versus [defined comparators] (C) reduce insomnia severity and improve [secondary outcomes] (O) at [timepoints]?" Align eligibility and synthesis to this question.

*Consensus: all four reviewers; r3 rated minor, others major. Consolidated as major.*

**Problem.** Eligibility criteria are incomplete: Methods require only a "randomised design," an "adult population," and reporting an insomnia-severity outcome. The comparator is undefined; the intervention ("digital CBT") is not operationalised (app vs web vs therapist-guided; which CBT-I components); outcome instruments and timepoints are unspecified ("insomnia severity outcomes" left undefined); and report characteristics (language, publication status, date range) are absent.

**Why it matters.** Vague eligibility makes inclusion non-reproducible and invites inconsistent, selection-biased decisions. The undefined "control" is later treated as a single entity even though waitlist, treatment-as-usual, and active controls yield very different effect estimates — which directly affects the headline conclusion.

**Suggested action.** Specify full PICOS eligibility: an operational dCBT-I definition with minimum CBT-I components, the eligible comparator(s) and how heterogeneous comparators are handled, required outcome measures (e.g., ISI) and timepoints, study designs, and report-level limits (language, dates, publication status) with rationale.

*Consensus: all four reviewers, major (r1 combined this with the PRISMA-4 objective item).*

**Problem.** Results report effects "favouring dCBT-I over control" without specifying the comparator. dCBT-I trials commonly use waitlist or no-treatment controls, which produce larger apparent effects than active or attention controls.

**Why it matters.** Pooling or summarizing across heterogeneous comparators conflates regression-to-the-mean and expectancy effects with treatment benefit, and a waitlist-dominated evidence base cannot support a claim of superiority to (or replacement of) active pharmacotherapy. This is the analytic link between the undefined comparator and the over-strong conclusion.

**Suggested action.** Extract and report the comparator for each trial, present results stratified by comparator type (waitlist/usual care vs active control), and temper the conclusions to reflect the comparator mix.

*Consensus: raised explicitly by r1; the comparator-definition aspect is shared with r2/r3/r4. Kept distinct for its analytic stratification point. Major.*

items.section_ref: Results (characteristics of included studies & effect estimates)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** The Results contain no characteristics-of-included-studies table (author/year, sample size, population, dCBT-I content/delivery, comparator, outcome instrument, follow-up) and no per-study effect estimates with confidence intervals; the section is essentially three general sentences ("most reported reductions … favouring dCBT-I," "effect sizes varied").

**Why it matters.** Without per-study characteristics and effect estimates, readers cannot assess applicability, magnitude, precision, or consistency, nor verify the conclusion. "Digital CBT" spans heterogeneous products (fully automated apps vs therapist-guided programmes), so a broad-adoption recommendation is uninterpretable without this characterisation.

**Suggested action.** Add a characteristics-of-included-studies table and report each study's effect estimate with a 95% CI for the primary outcome (e.g., ISI), stratified/labelled by comparator type; summarise the range and direction of effects.

*Consensus: dedicated items from r2, r3, r4 (r4 combined with synthesis). Distinct from the synthesis-method item (data reporting vs method). Major.*

**Problem.** The Discussion has no limitations section and no certainty-of-evidence assessment; it moves directly from "effective across the included trials" to broad recommendations (e.g., "Health systems should adopt dCBT-I broadly") without acknowledging the single-database search, absent RoB appraisal, unquantified heterogeneity, reliance on subjective self-reported outcomes, or unknown durability/applicability.

**Why it matters.** PRISMA expects discussion of the limitations of both the evidence and the review process. Omitting them while issuing strong clinical/policy recommendations overstates confidence and risks unjustified conclusions; the strength of recommendations must be calibrated to the strength of the evidence.

**Suggested action.** Add a limitations subsection covering search breadth, risk of bias, heterogeneity, outcome subjectivity, follow-up duration, generalisability, and the absence of a certainty rating; rate certainty per outcome (GRADE); and align the strength of every recommendation to certainty, softening or removing broad-adoption statements the evidence cannot support.

*Consensus: dedicated items from r1 and r4; certainty also raised by r2/r3 within the RoB item. Major.*

**Problem.** A competing-interests statement is present, but there is no funding statement for the review and no description of the role of any funder (PRISMA-27).

**Why it matters.** Funding disclosure (including an explicit "none") and the funder's role are standard transparency requirements that let readers judge potential influence on conduct and conclusions; the omission is a completeness gap even if no funding was received.

**Suggested action.** Add an explicit funding statement naming any source and describing its role in design, conduct, analysis, or reporting (or stating that the review received no specific funding). Consider also summarising the funding sources of the included trials in the characteristics table, since several dCBT-I trials in this field are industry/app-developer sponsored, which bears on interpreting effect sizes.

*Consensus: all four reviewers, minor (r3 categorised as structural; consolidated mechanical).*

**Problem.** Effect reporting relies on vague vote-counting language — "most reported reductions," "effect sizes varied," "improved insomnia severity across studies" — with no numbers, direction-of-effect detail, or uncertainty.

**Why it matters.** Imprecise wording obscures how large, how consistent, and how certain the effects are, and "across studies" overstates uniformity given the acknowledged variation.

**Suggested action.** Replace qualitative phrasing with specific estimates (per-study or pooled mean differences/SMDs on the ISI with 95% CIs), state how many trials favoured dCBT-I versus showed no difference, and describe heterogeneity explicitly rather than with "varied."

*Consensus: raised by r4. Closely related to the synthesis-method item but kept as a distinct wording-level fix. Minor.*

**Problem.** The reference standard (dilated fundus examination) was applied selectively: to *all* participants the classifier flagged as referable, but to only "a sample" of those it cleared. Sensitivity and specificity were then computed against this incompletely verified sample. This is partial verification bias, and because verification depended on the index-test result, also differential verification bias.

**Why it matters.** When who gets the reference standard depends on the index-test outcome, the resulting accuracy estimates are not valid. False negatives concentrate in the large cleared group (278 of 410) that was mostly *not* verified, so true sensitivity is typically overestimated and specificity distorted. The headline 0.89 / 0.91 cannot be interpreted as unbiased diagnostic accuracy.

**Suggested action.** Report exactly how many of the 278 cleared participants received the reference standard and how that sample was selected (random vs convenience). Either (a) verify all participants — or a prespecified random subset drawn independently of the classifier result — and report accuracy on that complete set, or (b) apply an explicit verification-bias correction (e.g., inverse-probability weighting) with stated sampling fractions and assumptions. Present the full cross-tabulation of verified counts.

**Problem.** The manuscript concludes the system is "accurate and ready for deployment in primary care," that "smartphone AI can replace specialist screening," and that "wider rollout should proceed." These are drawn from a single-center study with a biased verification design (see verification-bias finding), no external/prospective validation, no confidence intervals, and a population that is not the intended deployment setting.

**Why it matters.** Replacement-of-specialist and deployment-readiness claims require representative sampling, complete verification, reported predictive values at the target prevalence, and ideally external validation plus clinical-impact data. Stating deployment readiness on the present evidence risks premature adoption of an unproven screening pathway.

**Suggested action.** Restrict conclusions to what the data support: a preliminary, single-center estimate of accuracy that requires confirmation. Remove "ready for deployment," "can replace specialist screening," and "rollout should proceed." If deployment is discussed, tie it explicitly to the confirmatory steps still needed (complete verification, primary-care population, external validation).

**Problem.** Only summary sensitivity and specificity are reported. There is no cross-tabulation of index test vs reference standard (true/false positives and negatives), no count of verified participants, and no disease prevalence in the verified sample.

**Why it matters.** Without the underlying counts, readers cannot check the estimates, recompute measures, or judge how verification was handled. A 2x2 table is the minimum required to interpret a diagnostic-accuracy study (STARD item 23).

**Suggested action.** Provide the full 2x2 contingency table for verified participants (TP, FP, FN, TN), the number verified in each index-test arm, and the prevalence of referable DR among the verified.

**Problem.** Sensitivity (0.89) and specificity (0.91) are reported as point estimates with no measure of precision.

**Why it matters.** With ~410 participants and only a subset verified, the confidence intervals around these estimates may be wide; point estimates alone overstate certainty (STARD item 24).

**Suggested action.** Report 95% confidence intervals for sensitivity, specificity, and any derived measures, in both the abstract and Results.

**Problem.** 410 participants were imaged and 132 flagged referable, but the manuscript does not report how many images were ungradable/uninterpretable or how such cases were handled.

**Why it matters.** Smartphone fundus photographs are frequently ungradable, and the ungradable rate plus the rule for classifying ungradable images (e.g., auto-referral vs exclusion) materially changes real-world screening performance. Silently excluding ungradable images inflates apparent accuracy.

**Suggested action.** Report the ungradable/uninterpretable image rate and the prespecified handling rule (ideally counting ungradable as "refer"), and include these counts in the participant-flow accounting.

**Problem.** It is not stated whether the ophthalmologist performing the reference standard was masked to the classifier's result and to the clinical information.

**Why it matters.** If the reference reader knew the classifier output, reference grading could be influenced, biasing accuracy estimates (STARD items on blinding). This is especially relevant here because the reference exam was triggered largely by the classifier flag.

**Suggested action.** State explicitly whether reference-standard interpretation was performed without knowledge of the index-test result, and describe the grader's qualifications and grading protocol (e.g., ICDR severity scale, definition of "referable").

**Problem.** Participants were recruited from an endocrinology clinic, yet the stated use case is primary-care screening. The disease spectrum and prevalence in an endocrinology population are likely higher and more severe than in a general primary-care screening population.

**Why it matters.** Spectrum/selection bias can inflate sensitivity and limits transportability of the estimates to the intended deployment setting — directly undermining the primary-care deployment claim.

**Suggested action.** Acknowledge the selected population as a limitation, report the referable-DR prevalence observed, describe consecutive-recruitment completeness, and frame validation in the intended primary-care screening population as required future work.

**Problem.** The AI system is described only as an "embedded deep-learning classifier." There is no system name/version, operating threshold, or statement that the evaluation cohort was independent of the data used to develop the model.

**Why it matters.** Without a fixed, prespecified decision threshold and confirmation of train/test independence, results may not be reproducible and could be optimistic (e.g., threshold tuned on the same data). Readers cannot reproduce or compare the system.

**Suggested action.** Specify the system and version, the prespecified referral threshold, and confirm that test participants were not part of model development/training. Note whether the threshold was locked before this evaluation.

**Problem.** Only sensitivity and specificity are reported; positive and negative predictive values and the pre-test prevalence are not.

**Why it matters.** Screening and referral decisions depend on PPV/NPV at the target prevalence, not on sensitivity/specificity alone. Of 132 flagged, the number truly positive (and thus the false-referral burden on specialists) is not stated.

**Suggested action.** Report the verified prevalence and PPV/NPV with 95% CIs, and explicitly note their dependence on prevalence in the intended screening setting.

**Problem.** No sample-size or precision justification is given for the 410 participants.

**Why it matters.** Without a prespecified target, it is unclear whether the study was designed to estimate sensitivity/specificity with adequate precision, particularly given the small verified subset of cleared participants.

**Suggested action.** Provide a sample-size/precision rationale (e.g., expected prevalence, target CI width for sensitivity), and state whether it was prespecified.

**Problem.** Diabetic retinopathy is graded per eye, but the analysis appears to be per person ("410 participants ... 132 flagged"). The unit of analysis and how discordant eyes were combined are not defined.

**Why it matters.** Mixing eyes and persons without accounting for within-person correlation can bias estimates and their CIs, and ambiguity prevents correct interpretation.

**Suggested action.** State the unit of analysis (eye vs participant), the rule for assigning a participant-level result from two eyes, and how clustering was handled in the CIs.

**Problem.** There is no participant-flow diagram tracing imaged → gradable → index-test result → reference-standard verification → final analysis.

**Why it matters.** Given selective verification and unreported ungradable images, a flow diagram is essential for readers to see where participants were lost and which counts feed the 2x2 (STARD item 19).

**Suggested action.** Add a STARD-style flow diagram with counts at each step, including ungradable images and the verified/unverified split of cleared participants.

**Problem.** The abstract is unstructured and omits study design, sample size, the verification approach, and confidence intervals, while ending with an unsupported deployment claim.

**Why it matters.** Many readers act on the abstract alone; omitting design and precision while asserting readiness misrepresents the evidence.

**Suggested action.** Use a structured abstract (Objective/Methods/Results/Conclusions) stating design, setting, N, reference standard and how verification was done, sensitivity/specificity with 95% CIs, and a conclusion matched to the (corrected) findings.

**Problem.** The authors declare no conflicts of interest, but the study evaluates a specific embedded AI device whose developer/manufacturer is not identified, and the funder's role is not described.

**Why it matters.** A relationship to the device developer is a material potential conflict for a diagnostic-accuracy evaluation; readers cannot assess independence without disclosure.

**Suggested action.** Identify the device/algorithm manufacturer, disclose any financial, employment, or material (e.g., free hardware/software) relationship, and state the funder's role in design, analysis, and reporting.

**Problem — partial/differential verification bias (raised by all four reviewers; the dominant validity threat).** The reference standard (dilated fundus exam) was applied to every participant the classifier flagged as referable but only to *"a sample"* of those it cleared, so reference-standard verification is conditioned on the index-test result and an unknown fraction of classifier-negatives was never verified. Sensitivity (0.89) and specificity (0.91) are then computed as if all participants had been examined.

**Why it matters.** When work-up of the reference standard depends on the index test, sensitivity and specificity are not unbiased: sensitivity is typically inflated (false negatives hidden among the unverified "cleared" participants go uncounted) and specificity is distorted. The headline 0.89/0.91 cannot be read as true accuracy, and for a screening test the safety-critical missed-disease (false-negative) rate sits precisely in the partially verified classifier-negative arm. This is the core QUADAS-2 flow-and-timing / reference-standard failure and undermines the manuscript's central numbers.

**Suggested action.** Verify all classifier-negative participants, or a *prespecified random* sample with a *known* sampling fraction, and recompute with an explicit verification-bias correction (inverse-probability-of-verification weighting / Begg–Greenes) reporting corrected sensitivity/specificity with CIs. State exactly how the cleared sample was selected, what fraction of each arm was verified, and that uncorrected estimates are biased. If neither full nor random verification is feasible, reframe the study as screen-positive yield rather than diagnostic accuracy and foreground this as the primary limitation.

**Problem — conclusions far exceed the evidence (raised by all four reviewers).** The Abstract calls the system "accurate and ready for deployment in primary care"; the Discussion states it "can replace specialist screening in primary care" and that "wider rollout should proceed." One reviewer further notes these claims are internally contradictory — the specialist exam *is* the reference standard, so the device cannot logically "replace" the very standard that defines its accuracy.

**Why it matters.** These are deployment-, replacement-, and policy-level claims drawn from a single-center accuracy study whose primary estimates are themselves biased (verification bias) and reported without uncertainty, with no comparative arm, no screening-program outcomes (detection yield, time-to-treatment), no cost/implementation or equity data, and no non-inferiority design. Diagnostic accuracy in one endocrinology cohort does not establish equivalence to specialists, clinical effectiveness, safety at scale, or generalizability to primary care. Overstated readiness claims can drive premature adoption and missed sight-threatening retinopathy — exactly the kind of claim a clinical journal flags immediately.

**Suggested action.** Remove "ready for deployment," "can replace specialist screening," and "wider rollout should proceed." Reframe as a preliminary single-center accuracy estimate, tie every conclusion to a reported bias-corrected estimate with its CI, and state the prerequisites for any rollout: external/multi-site validation in the intended primary-care population, prospective implementation/outcome evaluation, and a non-inferiority study against the specialist pathway.

**Problem — accuracy results are incompletely reported and cannot be reconstructed (raised by all four reviewers).** Results give only point estimates (0.89/0.91) and two totals (410 imaged, 132 flagged). Missing: the full 2×2 cross-tabulation (TP/FP/TN/FN), the number in each arm actually verified by the reference standard (how many of the ~278 cleared were examined and how that sample was drawn), 95% confidence intervals, the reference-standard prevalence of referable DR, predictive values (PPV/NPV), and a participant-flow diagram. The 410→132 numbers do not reconcile into interpretable groups.

**Why it matters.** Without the verified 2×2 and denominators the estimates cannot be reproduced, audited, or bias-corrected; without CIs precision is unknowable (at n=410 with 132 flagged the intervals are wide and material to the "accurate" claim); and for a screening tool PPV/NPV at the relevant prevalence — especially NPV / missed-disease rate — are the clinically decision-relevant numbers and are absent. A cross-tabulation, CIs, and a flow diagram are core STARD items.

**Suggested action.** Provide a STARD participant-flow diagram (imaged → gradable → flagged/cleared → verified by reference → analyzed) and the complete 2×2 table with raw counts, clearly labeling the denominators used for sensitivity vs. specificity under partial verification. Report 95% CIs for sensitivity, specificity, and PPV/NPV (Wilson/Clopper–Pearson, or a variance estimator accounting for the verification sampling and any eye-level clustering), the observed prevalence, and projected predictive values at plausible primary-care prevalences. Carry the CIs into the Abstract.

**Problem — ungradable / low-quality images not reported (raised by r1, r2, r3, and within r4's participant-flow item).** Images were "graded automatically as referable or non-referable," with no mention of ungradable images, image-quality failures, mydriasis status, or participants excluded after imaging; the path from 410 "imaged" to a referable/non-referable label is opaque.

**Why it matters.** Smartphone (often non-mydriatic) fundus photography commonly yields a non-trivial ungradable rate (often 10–20%) and is one of the strongest determinants of real-world screening performance. Silently dropping ungradables inflates apparent accuracy and hides a key operational limitation; in screening an ungradable result usually triggers referral and should count against the test, changing both workload and specificity.

**Suggested action.** Report the number/percentage of ungradable/poor-quality images, the gradability definition, whether mydriasis was used, the device's behavior on such images, and how they entered the analysis. Present an intention-to-screen analysis counting ungradables as referral alongside the per-gradable analysis, and describe any other post-imaging exclusions and missing reference results (ideally within the STARD flow diagram).

**Problem — spectrum/selection bias: the studied population does not match the claimed use setting (raised by all four reviewers).** Participants were "consecutive adults with diabetes attending an endocrinology clinic" — an enriched, specialist-referred population (consistent with the high ~32% flagged-referable rate) — yet the conclusions generalize to primary-care screening.

**Why it matters.** An endocrinology-clinic population differs from a primary-care screening population in diabetes type/duration, glycemic burden, and prior/known retinopathy, and therefore in DR prevalence and severity spectrum. Diagnostic accuracy is spectrum-dependent, so sensitivity/specificity — and especially PPV/NPV at primary-care prevalence — may not transfer to the lower-prevalence, milder-disease deployment setting being claimed.

**Suggested action.** Report source-population characteristics relevant to spectrum (diabetes type/duration, prior DR, glycemic control, DR severity distribution, referable-DR prevalence), acknowledge single-center spectrum limitations, and either restrict accuracy claims to comparable populations or validate in an actual primary-care screening cohort before generalizing.

**Problem — reference-standard masking not described (built-in review bias), plus a single grader of unverified reliability (raised by r2, r3, r4 as major; r1 raised the reader-reliability facet).** Because the ophthalmologist exam was *triggered by* the classifier flagging a participant as referable, the reference grader plausibly knew the index-test result; masking is not stated. The reference is a single "ophthalmologist," with no mention of multiple graders, adjudication, or inter-rater reliability.

**Why it matters.** If the reference grader is aware of the classifier output, the reference standard is no longer independent of the index test (review/incorporation bias), which can spuriously increase apparent agreement and inflate accuracy (QUADAS-2 reference-standard domain). A single unmasked grader is itself error-prone, and without a reliability metric the reference's own error is unquantified and can bias accuracy in either direction.

**Suggested action.** State explicitly whether the ophthalmologist was masked to the classifier result (and to image-level findings); use ≥2 independent graders with a prespecified adjudication rule and report inter-rater agreement (e.g., kappa). If masking was impossible given the triggered design, acknowledge the resulting non-independence as a limitation and, ideally, re-grade under masked conditions.

**Problem — the target condition "referable diabetic retinopathy" is never defined (raised by r2, r3, r4).** No grading scale, severity threshold, or criteria are given for what counts as "referable," the ophthalmologist's grading protocol is unspecified, and it is unclear whether diabetic macular edema (DME) is included.

**Why it matters.** Sensitivity/specificity are interpretable only relative to an explicitly defined target condition and reference grading rule. "Referable" thresholds differ across programs (e.g., moderate NPDR or worse, any DME, vs. more severe cutoffs), and DME can be present without visible retinopathy and may be missed on color images. Without a stated threshold the numbers are not reproducible or comparable to other DR-screening studies, and the index test and reference may not be measuring the same condition.

**Suggested action.** Define referable DR against a recognized scale (e.g., ICDR or NHS DESP) with an explicit severity cutoff, specify the ophthalmologist grading procedure, state whether DME is included and how it was ascertained, and confirm the AI and reference standard used the same definition.

**Problem — the index test is underspecified (raised by r4; device/version also flagged by r3 under STARD).** No device/app identity or version, no embedded-model version, no operating threshold for "referable," no training/derivation provenance, and no statement of whether image capture was standardized or operator-dependent.

**Why it matters.** Diagnostic-accuracy results are uninterpretable and non-reproducible without the exact index test and threshold — a different model version or cut-point yields different numbers. Readers and reviewers cannot assess applicability, operator dependence, or independence of the evaluation.

**Suggested action.** Specify the device and software/model version, the referable threshold and how it was set (prespecified vs. tuned on these data — confirm it was fixed before analysis), the capture protocol and operator training, and the algorithm's derivation/provenance.

**Problem — no sample-size or precision justification (raised by r1).** The study enrolled "consecutive adults" to a final n=410 with no prespecified target sample size, expected prevalence, or target CI width / power calculation.

**Why it matters.** Without a sample-size rationale readers cannot tell whether the study was powered to estimate sensitivity/specificity with adequate precision, particularly since sensitivity is estimated from the relatively small number of reference-confirmed diseased cases — that count is the binding constraint on the precision of the sensitivity estimate.

**Suggested action.** Add a prespecified sample-size justification stating the assumed prevalence of referable DR, the target sensitivity/specificity, and the desired precision (CI half-width), and report the actual number of reference-confirmed diseased cases.

**Problem — unit of analysis (eye vs. patient) and clustering undefined (raised by r1; the eye-vs-patient unit was also touched by r2).** It is not stated whether one or both eyes were imaged, nor whether the analysis unit is the eye or the participant, nor how both eyes were combined.

**Why it matters.** If both eyes contribute, the two eyes of a patient are correlated; analyzing eyes as independent observations understates variance and produces falsely narrow CIs, while patient-level rules (e.g., refer if either eye is positive) change the effective sensitivity/specificity. The denominator 410 (participants) vs. an eye-level count materially affects every estimate.

**Suggested action.** State the imaging protocol (eyes per participant) and the analysis unit. If eye-level, use clustered/robust variance (GEE or cluster-bootstrap) to account for within-patient correlation; if patient-level, define the rule for combining eyes.

**Problem — no diagnostic-accuracy reporting-guideline (STARD 2015) conformance (raised by r2, r3).** The study does not reference or complete a STARD 2015 checklist, and several STARD items are missing (participant flow, prespecified analysis/estimands, indeterminate/ungradable results, index-to-reference interval, CIs, device/version).

**Why it matters.** STARD conformance is expected for diagnostic-accuracy submissions to clinical journals; the omissions above directly track unmet STARD items, prevent independent risk-of-bias (QUADAS-2) assessment, and weaken reproducibility.

**Suggested action.** Complete and cite a STARD 2015 checklist, add the participant-flow diagram, and state whether the population, threshold, and primary estimands were prespecified. (This is the umbrella item; the specific gaps are itemized in the reporting, ungradable, index-to-reference interval, and index-test issues.)

**Problem — processing speed is presented as if it supports diagnostic validity (raised by r3, r4).** "The system processed each image in under five seconds" sits among the accuracy results and feeds the "ready for deployment" narrative.

**Why it matters.** Throughput speed is a device/operational characteristic, not evidence of diagnostic validity; placing it among accuracy results pads the findings and distracts from the missing 2×2 data, CIs, and ungradable rates.

**Suggested action.** Move processing time to a clearly labeled operational/feasibility note (or a device-description sentence in Methods) and explicitly decouple it from the diagnostic-accuracy and deployment conclusions.

**Problem — interval between index test and reference standard not reported (raised by r2).** The time between smartphone imaging and the ophthalmologist's dilated exam is not stated.

**Why it matters.** If the interval is long, disease status can change between the two tests (disease-progression bias); same-visit vs. later-visit verification also affects feasibility claims.

**Suggested action.** Report the time interval (and its distribution) between index imaging and reference examination, and confirm that target status was stable over that interval.

**Problem — device-manufacturer relationship and funding role inadequately disclosed (raised by r2, r3, r4).** The study evaluates a specific commercial deep-learning device, yet declarations state only "no conflicts of interest" and "a departmental grant," with no statement about the AI system's manufacturer, and the device is never named.

**Why it matters.** For a device-evaluation study, independence from the manufacturer is central to credibility; undisclosed manufacturer involvement in funding, device/algorithm provision, data access, configuration, analysis, or manuscript review is a recognized source of bias and a reporting/ethics gap that a generic no-conflict statement does not cover.

**Suggested action.** Name the device and software version; disclose the manufacturer's role (funding, device/algorithm provision, data access, analysis, manuscript review); affirm investigator control of the data and independence of the analysis; and name the funding source specifically.

**Problem — the unstructured abstract omits key elements (raised by r4).** It omits study design, setting, sample size, prevalence, and confidence intervals while leading with an interpretive conclusion.

**Why it matters.** Many readers, screeners, and databases act on the abstract alone; without design, N, setting, prevalence, and CIs they cannot gauge the strength or applicability of the 0.89/0.91 estimates, and the unsupported "ready for deployment" line dominates.

**Suggested action.** Restructure the abstract to report design (prospective, single-center), setting, number screened/verified, referable-DR prevalence, sensitivity/specificity with 95% CIs, and a measured conclusion aligned with the corrected Discussion.

**Problem — foundational Introduction claims are uncited (raised by r4).** Premises such as diabetic retinopathy being "a leading cause of preventable blindness" and that "screening coverage is low" are stated without citations.

**Why it matters.** A clinical journal expects the epidemiologic premises that motivate the study to be referenced; uncited assertions of disease burden and unmet need read as unsupported.

**Suggested action.** Add appropriate references for the blindness-burden and screening-coverage statements, ideally with a sentence quantifying the screening gap the device is meant to close.

**Problem.** The reference standard (dilated fundus examination) was applied selectively: to all participants the classifier flagged as referable, but only to "a sample" of those it cleared. Verification was therefore driven by the index-test result — the definition of partial verification (work-up) bias. The reported sensitivity (0.89) and specificity (0.91) are computed against this incomplete, index-dependent reference.

**Why it matters.** When who gets the reference standard depends on the index test, sensitivity is typically inflated and specificity distorted, because most true-negatives and false-negatives among cleared participants are never verified. The central accuracy estimates cannot be interpreted as unbiased, which undermines the entire results and conclusions section. STARD-17 (cross-tabulation) and STARD-14 (indeterminate handling) cannot be satisfied as designed.

**Suggested action.** Either (a) verify all participants — or a pre-specified random sample drawn independently of the index result — with the reference standard, or (b) apply an explicit verification-bias correction (e.g., Begg–Greenes) using the known sampling fractions, reporting those fractions. Report exactly how many cleared participants were and were not verified, and present bias-corrected sensitivity/specificity with 95% CIs. If neither is feasible, reframe the study as preliminary/enriched-sample and remove all unbiased-accuracy language.

**Problem.** The Abstract states the system is "accurate and ready for deployment," and the Discussion states "smartphone AI can replace specialist screening in primary care" and "Wider rollout should proceed." These are deployment- and policy-level claims drawn from a single-center study with verification-biased estimates, no confidence intervals, no predictive values, no ungradable-image data, and no external validation.

**Why it matters.** Recommending replacement of specialist screening on this evidence risks missed sight-threatening retinopathy. The claims also overshoot the studied setting: participants were recruited in an endocrinology clinic, not primary care, the very setting the conclusion targets.

**Suggested action.** Recast these as hypothesis-generating. State explicitly that deployment/replacement would require: unbiased accuracy with 95% CIs, predictive values at expected primary-care prevalence, a defined pathway and rate for ungradable images, prospective validation in the intended setting, and a safety/failure-mode analysis. Delete "ready for deployment," "can replace specialist screening," and "rollout should proceed."

**Problem.** No ungradable or indeterminate results are reported. Smartphone fundus imaging frequently yields ungradable images (media opacity, inadequate mydriasis, motion, small pupils). The manuscript reports 410 "imaged" and gives no count of ungradable index images or indeterminate reference results, nor how they were handled in the analysis (STARD-14).

**Why it matters.** Silently excluding ungradable images inflates apparent accuracy and hides a key real-world limitation — in deployment, ungradable images still need a clinical disposition. The "410 imaged" denominator may already exclude acquisition failures, making the accuracy figures unrepresentative.

**Suggested action.** Report the number/percentage of ungradable index images and indeterminate reference results, state how they were handled, and present a sensitivity analysis that counts ungradables as positives (refer) or otherwise includes them. Describe how ungradable images would be managed in the proposed screening pathway.

**Problem.** Accuracy is reported as bare point estimates (sensitivity 0.89, specificity 0.91) with: no 95% confidence intervals; no 2x2 cross-tabulation of index test vs reference standard (STARD-17); no disease prevalence; and no predictive values (PPV/NPV) or likelihood ratios.

**Why it matters.** Without the 2x2 table, readers cannot verify the computations or recompute metrics. Without CIs (and with only 132 flagged), the precision is unknown — a 0.89 sensitivity could carry a clinically important lower bound. For the claimed primary-care screening use, PPV/NPV at the relevant prevalence — not sensitivity/specificity alone — determine usefulness.

**Suggested action.** Provide the full 2x2 table (TP/FP/FN/TN with the actual verified counts, distinguishing verified from unverified cleared participants), the sample prevalence, and sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, and likelihood ratios each with 95% CIs and explicit denominators.

**Problem.** Participants were consecutive adults with diabetes attending an endocrinology clinic, yet the conclusions are about primary-care screening. Endocrinology-clinic populations tend to have longer-duration and more severe diabetes, and thus higher diabetic-retinopathy prevalence and more advanced disease, than a general primary-care screening population (spectrum bias).

**Why it matters.** Diagnostic accuracy — and especially predictive values — estimated in a higher-prevalence, more-severe spectrum do not transfer to a lower-prevalence primary-care setting. This directly weakens the generalizability underpinning the deployment claim.

**Suggested action.** Characterize the spectrum (diabetes type and duration, prior DR history, distribution of DR severity by reference grade) and discuss its effect on transferability. Restrict claims to the studied population, or validate prospectively in the intended primary-care setting before making screening-replacement claims.

**Problem.** The index test is not described in replicable detail (STARD-10a). Missing: the specific device/camera and AI model and software version, the classifier output and the exact threshold used to label "referable," the image-acquisition protocol (mydriasis vs non-mydriatic, number of fields/eye), and who captured the images and their training.

**Why it matters.** Smartphone-AI accuracy depends heavily on device, model version, capture protocol, and operator skill. As written, the study cannot be replicated and its applicability to other operators or model versions cannot be judged.

**Suggested action.** Specify hardware and software (including model/version), the classifier's output and the pre-specified decision threshold, the full image-capture protocol (dilation, fields, retakes), and the operator's role and training. State whether the threshold and settings were fixed and pre-specified before data collection.

**Problem.** The reference standard is underspecified (STARD-11). Missing: the number and qualifications of the examining ophthalmologist(s), the grading scale used (e.g., ICDR/ETDRS), the operational definition of "referable," the dilation protocol, and — critically — whether the reference assessor was blinded to the index-test result (and vice versa) and the time interval between the two tests (STARD-12).

**Why it matters.** Without a defined, standardized reference scale and documented blinding, the reference classification itself is unreliable and the accuracy estimates may be biased. If the ophthalmologist knew the classifier's result, reference grading is no longer independent.

**Suggested action.** Define the reference grading scale and the threshold for "referable," report the number and qualifications of graders (and reader agreement if multiple), state explicitly whether index and reference assessors were blinded to each other's results, and report the interval between index test and reference standard.

**Problem.** There is no participant-flow accounting and no STARD flow diagram. The manuscript does not report how many participants were approached/eligible/excluded, how many images were ungradable, how many classifier-negative participants were selected for verification versus not, or how many had missing reference results.

**Why it matters.** STARD requires a flow diagram, and here it is essential to quantify the magnitude of the verification bias (Finding 1) and to judge applicability. Without the verified/unverified counts, the 2x2 table and the accuracy estimates cannot be reconstructed or audited.

**Suggested action.** Add a STARD participant-flow diagram giving numbers at each stage: screened/eligible, imaged, ungradable, classifier-positive vs negative, number in each arm receiving the reference standard (and the sampling fraction for cleared participants), and any missing reference results.

**Problem.** The study design is incompletely reported: it is not stated whether data collection was planned before the index test and reference standard (prospective vs retrospective, with a pre-specified analysis) (STARD-5); the full eligibility/exclusion criteria are not given (age range, diabetes type, prior DR treatment, ocular comorbidities) (STARD-6); and the basis for the cleared-participant sampling is not described (STARD-7).

**Why it matters.** Whether the analysis was pre-specified and how participants were selected materially affect risk of bias and interpretability, and they are prerequisites for assessing the verification scheme in Finding 1.

**Suggested action.** State the design (prospective vs retrospective) and whether analyses were pre-specified, list full inclusion/exclusion criteria, and describe the pre-specified sampling scheme used to select cleared participants for verification.

**Problem.** This is an accuracy evaluation of a specific commercial-type AI device, yet the Declarations state "no conflicts of interest" and list only "a departmental grant," without naming the device/software manufacturer or describing any vendor role in study design, conduct, data access, or analysis.

**Why it matters.** Vendor involvement in an accuracy study of its own product is a recognized bias source; readers need the relationship and the independence of the analysis disclosed to weigh the findings. This is a request for transparency, not an assertion of an undisclosed conflict.

**Suggested action.** Name the evaluated device and its manufacturer/software vendor, disclose any vendor funding, equipment, data access, or role in design/analysis, and state who controlled the analysis and had access to the raw data. If the work was fully independent of the manufacturer, state that explicitly.

items.section_ref: Methods (reference standard / verification) / Results (STARD-7, STARD-13a, STARD-14, STARD-17)
items.content_md:
**Problem — partial/differential (work-up) verification bias.** The reference standard (dilated fundus examination) was applied to *all* 132 classifier-flagged ('referable') participants but only to *a sample* of the 278 the classifier cleared. Verification is therefore conditional on the index-test result, and true disease status is unknown for most cleared participants.

**Why it matters.** False negatives can only arise among cleared participants, most of whom were never examined, so FN are systematically undercounted — inflating sensitivity. Specificity is likewise distorted because TN are drawn only from the sampled cleared group while all FP (flagged, disease-free) are fully ascertained. The headline estimates of 0.89/0.91 are thus not valid, reproducible accuracy estimates as computed, and for a sight-saving screen the false-negative rate is the safety-critical metric this design specifically prevents measuring (STARD-7, STARD-13a, STARD-14, STARD-17).

**Suggested action.** Either (a) verify all participants (or a complete random subset) regardless of classifier output, or (b) keep two-phase sampling but draw the cleared sample at *random* with a documented, reported sampling fraction and compute verification-bias-corrected sensitivity/specificity using inverse-probability-of-verification weighting (Begg–Greenes), naming the method and giving 95% CIs. State explicitly how the cleared sample was selected (random vs convenience) and how many of the 278 cleared were examined. If the cleared sample was not random, the estimates cannot be salvaged and should be removed or reframed as conditional/uncorrected with that limitation stated. *(Raised by all four reviewers; unanimous critical.)*

**Problem — conclusions exceed the evidence.** The Abstract states the system is 'accurate and ready for deployment in primary care'; the Discussion states it 'can replace specialist screening in primary care' and that 'wider rollout should proceed.'

**Why it matters.** These deployment/replacement claims rest on a single-site study (n=410, one endocrinology clinic) with verification-biased accuracy, no CIs, no predictive values, no calibration, no external/multi-site validation, no comparison to current screening, and no clinical-outcome or cost data. There is also a logical flaw: a test validated *against* the specialist reference cannot be shown to *replace* that specialist — at best it triages referrals. Overstated readiness/replacement claims for a sight-threatening condition are a patient-safety concern, and because they also appear in the abstract they reach the widest audience.

**Suggested action.** Restrict conclusions to what the data support, e.g., 'in this single-centre study the classifier showed promising apparent accuracy for referable DR; verification-bias-corrected, prospective, multi-site validation with predictive values, ungradable-image handling, and ideally clinical-outcome/impact evidence is required before considering it as an adjunct to — not a replacement for — specialist screening.' Remove 'ready for deployment,' 'replace specialist screening,' and 'wider rollout should proceed.' *(All four reviewers; rated critical by r2/r3/r4 and major by r1 — escalated to critical.)*

**Problem — no 2×2 cross-tabulation and no disease prevalence.** Results give only 410 imaged and 132 flagged referable (278 cleared). TP, FP, FN, TN are never reported, the number of cleared participants who received the reference standard is not given, the prevalence of referable DR is absent, and no STARD participant-flow diagram is provided.

**Why it matters.** STARD-17 requires cross-tabulation of index results by reference results. Without it readers cannot reconstruct or independently verify 0.89/0.91, cannot see how many cleared participants were actually verified, cannot assess the verification bias above, and cannot derive prevalence or predictive values. As written the figures are unauditable.

**Suggested action.** Provide the full 2×2 table using verified participants, clearly delineating the verified subset of cleared patients and how the cleared-sample counts are weighted/scaled into the table; report the count and sampling fraction of cleared participants examined, the number with referable DR and the cohort prevalence, the ungradable count, and a STARD flow diagram. Ensure cell counts are arithmetically consistent with the reported metrics. *(All four reviewers.)*

**Problem — point estimates with no measure of uncertainty.** Sensitivity (0.89) and specificity (0.91) are reported as bare numbers with no confidence intervals, no numerators/denominators (number diseased / non-diseased), and no statement of the estimation method (STARD-13a).

**Why it matters.** With an unknown and likely small number of true referable cases (~132 flagged, a partially verified cleared denominator), the intervals — especially around sensitivity — may be wide enough to include clinically unacceptable values for a screen where missed disease causes preventable blindness. A single decimal implies a precision the data may not support, and reviewers/clinicians cannot weigh deployment risk without the interval.

**Suggested action.** Report 95% CIs for sensitivity and specificity (e.g., Wilson or Clopper–Pearson), state the numerator/denominator for each, name the estimation method (including any verification-bias correction), and pre-specify a clinically acceptable lower bound for sensitivity to judge the result against. *(All four reviewers.)*

**Problem — no predictive values for the screening decision.** Only sensitivity/specificity are reported; PPV and NPV at the cohort prevalence are absent, as is the DR prevalence/severity distribution (STARD-1 lists predictive values among accuracy measures).

**Why it matters.** Sens/spec do not tell a clinician the probability that a flagged patient truly needs referral (PPV, the referral-burden metric) or that a cleared patient is safe (NPV, the safety-critical false-reassurance metric for a screen). Predictive values are highly prevalence-dependent, so values from an endocrinology cohort will not transfer to lower-prevalence primary care; the conditional-verification design also means predictive values cannot be read off directly without correction.

**Suggested action.** Report DR prevalence/severity and PPV/NPV (and likelihood ratios) with 95% CIs after verification-bias correction, and additionally compute predictive values across a plausible range of primary-care prevalences so readers can judge transportability; or explain explicitly why predictive values cannot be estimated from this design and what additional sampling is needed. *(r1, r2, r3; also folded into r4's precision item.)*

**Problem — ungradable/indeterminate images not addressed.** The manuscript reports outcomes only for the 410 'imaged' with no ungradable rate and no statement of how indeterminate index- or reference-standard results were handled (STARD-14).

**Why it matters.** Smartphone fundus photography commonly yields a non-trivial fraction of ungradable images (undilated/small pupils, media opacity, motion, operator variability). If ungradables were silently excluded, 410 is a post-selection denominator and the reported accuracy applies only to gradable images — overstating real-world performance and screening coverage. In deployment an ungradable patient still needs disposition (typically referral).

**Suggested action.** Report the number/proportion of ungradable images, the gradability criteria, and how they were handled, and present accuracy both (a) excluding ungradable and (b) in an intention-to-screen analysis that treats ungradable as a 'refer'/positive outcome rather than dropping them. Clarify whether 410 is the number imaged/eligible or the number with gradable images. *(All four reviewers.)*

**Problem — spectrum/applicability mismatch.** Participants were consecutive adults with diabetes attending an *endocrinology clinic*, but the conclusions target *primary-care* screening — an enriched, higher-severity spectrum.

**Why it matters.** Specialty-clinic attendees typically have longer disease duration, worse glycaemic control, and higher DR prevalence/severity. Sensitivity/specificity are spectrum-dependent and predictive values prevalence-dependent, so accuracy measured here likely overstates performance in the lower-prevalence primary-care population where deployment is claimed. The eligibility basis and recruitment setting (STARD-6/7) therefore directly undermine the generalisation being made.

**Suggested action.** Report full eligibility and the sample's DR prevalence and severity distribution, explicitly flag spectrum bias as a limitation, temper or condition the primary-care claim, and ideally validate in the intended primary-care population before generalising. *(r1, r2, r3; also noted in r4's selection item.)*

**Problem — reference standard under-specified, target condition undefined, and masking not reported.** 'Dilated fundus examination by an ophthalmologist' omits the grading scale (e.g., ICDR/ETDRS), the operational definition of 'referable DR,' the number/experience of graders, adjudication of disagreements, inter-rater reliability, and — critically — whether graders were masked to the classifier result. Because the exam was *triggered by* the classifier flag, masking and verification are entangled.

**Why it matters.** An unmasked grader who knows the patient was flagged may grade more aggressively (review/incorporation bias), distorting reference labels and inflating apparent agreement. An undefined 'referable' threshold (the entire target condition) prevents reproducibility and cross-study comparison, and unreported reference-standard error is unquantified — any reference misclassification biases index-test accuracy (STARD-11).

**Suggested action.** Define 'referable DR' against a named grading scale (e.g., ICDR ≥ moderate NPDR, stating whether diabetic macular oedema is included), report the number/qualifications of graders, the adjudication procedure and inter-rater agreement, and confirm explicitly whether graders were blinded to the index-test result; if not, acknowledge the likely upward bias and redesign for blinded reference reading. *(All four reviewers; rated minor by r1 and major by r2/r3/r4 — set to major.)*

**Problem — index test not reproducible.** 'A smartphone camera with an embedded deep-learning classifier' omits the device/hardware and classifier name/version, the operating threshold defining 'referable' (and whether it was pre-specified or tuned on these data), the image-capture protocol (number of fields, pupil dilation vs non-mydriatic, lighting/adapter), and who captured the images and their training (STARD-10a).

**Why it matters.** The chosen probability threshold directly sets the sensitivity/specificity trade-off, so 0.89/0.91 are uninterpretable without it; capture conditions (mydriasis, field count, operator skill) strongly affect both gradability and accuracy. The result is currently neither reproducible nor appraisable.

**Suggested action.** Report the device and software version, the pre-specified decision threshold and how it was chosen, the acquisition protocol and number of fields, dilation status, and operator qualifications/training. If the threshold was selected post hoc on this dataset, flag the resulting optimism and validate on a separate sample. *(All four reviewers; rated minor by r1 and major by r2/r3/r4 — set to major.)*

**Problem — no sample-size or precision justification.** There is no a-priori calculation, and the number of participants with referable DR (which drives the precision of sensitivity) is never stated.

**Why it matters.** A study of 410 can still estimate sensitivity poorly if only, say, 30–60 true cases are present; the sensitivity CI then spans clinically meaningful differences. Without the diseased-case count and a target precision, the reader cannot tell whether the study was adequately powered to bound sensitivity below an unacceptable miss rate.

**Suggested action.** Justify the sample size against a target CI width or a minimum acceptable sensitivity, report the number of diseased and non-diseased participants, and discuss whether the achieved precision on sensitivity is sufficient for a screening decision. *(Raised as a standalone item by r1; precision concern echoed by r3.)*

**Problem — design timing and pre-specification unstated.** It is not stated whether data collection was planned before the index test and reference standard were performed (prospective vs retrospective), nor whether the analysis plan and the referable threshold were pre-specified (STARD-5).

**Why it matters.** This distinguishes a confirmatory accuracy study from an exploratory/post-hoc one and bears directly on the credibility of the operating threshold and reported metrics; post-hoc threshold or analysis choices increase the risk of optimistic bias.

**Suggested action.** State whether the study was prospective, whether the analysis plan and referable threshold were pre-specified, the number of sites, and provide a protocol/registration reference if one exists. *(r2, r3; also in r4's selection item.)*

**Problem — eligibility criteria incomplete.** Eligibility is given only as 'consecutive adults with diabetes,' with no explicit inclusion/exclusion criteria (diabetes type, age range, prior DR diagnosis or treatment such as photocoagulation, media opacity, prior ocular surgery) and no recruitment dates (STARD-6).

**Why it matters.** Previously treated or already-diagnosed eyes alter retinal appearance and the meaning of 'referable,' and unstated exclusions change the spectrum and the interpretation of accuracy.

**Suggested action.** Provide full inclusion and exclusion criteria, recruitment dates and setting, the number of sites, and state how previously diagnosed/treated DR and ineligible/excluded patients were handled. *(r2; also in r4's selection item.)*

**Problem — declarations inadequate for a commercial-device AI study.** A 'departmental grant' does not identify the funder, 'no conflicts of interest' does not address the device/algorithm provenance, the manuscript does not name the manufacturer (so independence is currently unverifiable), and there is no data/code availability statement.

**Why it matters.** Manufacturer involvement is a recognised source of optimism bias in diagnostic-AI studies; without an independence statement readers and editors cannot appraise it, and for a deployment-readiness claim about a specific product independence is material to credibility (ICMJE/STARD).

**Suggested action.** Name the device/classifier, the manufacturer, and the funding source; disclose any manufacturer role in design, data access, algorithm/threshold control, analysis, or manuscript preparation; confirm independent investigator access to the raw data; and add a data/code availability statement. *(r2, r3, r4; rated major by r4 and minor by r2/r3 — set to minor.)*

**Problem — abstract under-reports and overstates.** The abstract omits the design (single-centre diagnostic-accuracy study; two-phase/partial verification), sample size, CIs, predictive values, prevalence, and the ungradable-image issue, and states the conclusion as established fact ('accurate and ready for deployment') (STARD-1).

**Why it matters.** STARD-1 asks the abstract to identify the study as a diagnostic-accuracy study and report key estimates with uncertainty; many readers and citers act on the abstract alone, so the unqualified claim propagates the overreach to the widest audience.

**Suggested action.** Revise the abstract to state the design (prospective, single-centre, partial-verification), N, sensitivity/specificity with 95% CIs plus PPV/NPV and prevalence, note the ungradable-image rate, and replace 'accurate and ready for deployment' with an evidence-matched conclusion. *(r1, r4; this is the abstract-completeness rewrite, distinct from the substantive overreach item above.)*

**Problem.** The reference standard (dilated fundus examination) was applied *only* to participants the classifier flagged as referable, plus an unspecified "sample" of those it cleared. Verification of true disease status was therefore conditional on the index-test result, and most classifier-negative participants were never examined.

**Why it matters.** This is textbook partial and differential verification (work-up) bias (QUADAS-2 *Flow and Timing* / *Reference Standard*; STARD item 21). False negatives among the unverified cleared participants are largely unobserved, which inflates sensitivity; because the sampling fraction and weighting of cleared participants is undescribed, specificity is also distorted, and the FN/TN cells are effectively undefined as presented. The headline sensitivity 0.89 / specificity 0.91 are not unbiased estimates and are not interpretable as reported — this undermines the entire results section. (Raised by all four reviewers as critical.)

**Suggested action.** Either (a) verify a complete consecutive cohort, or (b) verify a *pre-specified random* sample of all participants drawn independently of the classifier output, with a documented sampling fraction, and apply an established partial-verification correction (e.g., Begg–Greenes / inverse-probability weighting), reporting corrected estimates with 95% CIs. State exactly how the "sample" of cleared participants was selected and how many were verified, present accuracy restricted to the fully verified subset, and quantify the likely direction and magnitude of the bias. If it cannot be remedied, reframe the numbers as exploratory and flag the bias prominently as a limitation.

**Problem.** The manuscript concludes the system is "accurate and ready for deployment" (Abstract), that "smartphone AI can replace specialist screening in primary care," and that "wider rollout should proceed" (Discussion).

**Why it matters.** These are deployment-, replacement-, and policy-level claims the design cannot support: a single-centre study, verification-biased accuracy estimates, no external/multi-site validation, no primary-care population, no comparator arm, and no safety, cost, implementation, or patient-outcome data. There is also a logical flaw — the ophthalmologist *is* the reference standard, so the data cannot show the AI can *replace* the very comparator it was validated against without equivalence- or outcome-level evidence. A 0.89 sensitivity means roughly 11% of referable cases are missed; framing this as ready to replace specialist screening overstates readiness and could justify unsafe deprioritisation of confirmatory examination, especially given the unverified cleared patients. (Raised by all four reviewers as critical.)

**Suggested action.** Restrict conclusions to what the data demonstrate — a promising internal accuracy estimate in one endocrinology-clinic cohort. Remove "ready for deployment," "replace specialist screening," and "rollout should proceed" from both Abstract and Discussion. Reframe as preliminary findings requiring bias-corrected, prospective, fully verified, multi-site validation in the intended primary-care population before any deployment recommendation.

**Problem.** Results give only the number imaged (410), the number flagged referable (132), and two summary statistics. There is no cross-tabulation of index test versus reference standard, no TP/FP/FN/TN counts, no statement of how many cleared participants were verified, and no per-arm denominators. Because only a sample of classifier-negatives was verified, the FN and TN cells are undefined as presented.

**Why it matters.** The 2x2 contingency table is the minimum evidentiary unit for a diagnostic-accuracy study (STARD item 23). Without the cell counts and explicit denominators the estimates are unverifiable and non-reproducible — readers cannot recompute 0.89/0.91, derive confidence intervals or PPV/NPV, or assess the verification scheme. (One reviewer rated this critical; consolidated as major.)

**Suggested action.** Provide the full 2x2 table with raw counts; the number and fraction of cleared participants who underwent the reference standard; the exact numerator/denominator (n) contributing to each cell and metric; and ensure every count traces back to the 410 imaged.

**Problem.** Sensitivity (0.89) and specificity (0.91) are reported as bare point estimates, with no confidence intervals and no denominators, in both the Abstract and Results.

**Why it matters.** Point estimates without precision are uninterpretable and overstate certainty. With only ~132 flagged and an unstated (and likely small) number of verified cleared participants, the intervals may be wide. STARD item 22 requires estimates of precision.

**Suggested action.** Report 95% confidence intervals (Wilson or Clopper–Pearson exact) for sensitivity, specificity, PPV, and NPV, and state the exact n contributing to each estimate, in both the Abstract and Results.

**Problem.** The observed prevalence of referable diabetic retinopathy and the predictive values (PPV/NPV) are not reported; only sensitivity and specificity are given.

**Why it matters.** For a screening test, clinical decisions depend on post-test probabilities, not sensitivity/specificity alone. PPV/NPV are what a primary-care clinician actually acts on and drive referral burden and workload. PPV is prevalence-dependent, and an endocrinology-clinic prevalence is typically higher than primary care, so utility will not transfer directly to the intended setting. (One reviewer rated this minor, another major; consolidated as major.)

**Suggested action.** Report the observed prevalence of referable DR and PPV/NPV with CIs at that prevalence, ideally also across a plausible range of primary-care prevalences, and discuss the expected referral volume.

items.section_ref: Methods / Discussion (study population vs. claimed setting)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** Participants were consecutive adults with diabetes attending an *endocrinology clinic*, yet the conclusions target *primary-care* screening and replacing specialists.

**Why it matters.** This is spectrum/setting bias. Endocrinology-clinic attendees have higher diabetic-retinopathy prevalence and more advanced disease than a general primary-care screening population, so sensitivity, specificity, and especially PPV/NPV may not transfer to the deployment target — undermining the central claim. (Raised by all four reviewers.)

**Suggested action.** Justify the recruitment setting and describe the disease spectrum/case-mix explicitly (diabetes type, duration, prior DR status, prevalence and severity of referable DR). Explicitly limit generalisation, and recommend validation in the actual primary-care screening population before any primary-care claim.

**Problem.** The index test is described only as "an embedded deep-learning classifier." There is no model name/version, developer, software version, hardware (phone and lens), training/development-data provenance, image-acquisition protocol (fields, eyes, dilation status, photographs per eye), or the operating threshold that defines "referable" — nor whether that threshold was pre-specified/locked, nor whether the 410 participants were independent of the model's training/development data.

**Why it matters.** The index test cannot be reproduced or risk-assessed. An undisclosed, post-hoc, or on-data-tuned threshold, or overlap between test participants and training/tuning data (data leakage/overfitting), would optimistically bias the reported accuracy. STARD items 10–12 require the index test to be fully specified. (Raised by all four reviewers.)

**Suggested action.** Report the device/model and software version, the developer, the hardware, the training-data source, and the acquisition protocol. State the pre-specified referable threshold and confirm it was locked before this evaluation (test set not used for tuning) and that test participants did not overlap with training/tuning data.

**Problem.** Image quality and the ungradable / failed-acquisition rate are not reported; all 410 imaged participants appear to receive a binary result, and handling of indeterminate classifier outputs is unstated.

**Why it matters.** The ungradable rate is a primary real-world performance metric for smartphone fundus photography (media opacity, small pupils, operator variability) and directly affects effective sensitivity/specificity and clinical usefulness — the failure mode that matters most in non-specialist hands. Silently excluding ungradable images inflates apparent accuracy and hides a key operational limitation; indeterminate results are an explicit STARD reporting item. (Raised by all four reviewers.)

**Suggested action.** Report the number and percentage of ungradable images and how they were handled (excluded, repeated, or counted as referable — the safe screening default), include them in the participant flow, and present a sensitivity analysis re-deriving accuracy under each handling rule, including a clinically realistic rule that treats ungradable images as referrals.
