**Problem.** It is not stated whether the ophthalmologist applying the reference standard was masked to the classifier result — and because the reference exam was *triggered* by the classifier flag, the grader plausibly knew the AI output. The reference standard also appears to be a single ophthalmologist's dilated examination, with no second reader, no adjudication, and no inter-rater reliability reported; dilated examination is itself a weaker reference than panel-graded stereoscopic or widefield photography.

**Why it matters.** Knowledge of the index result while applying the reference standard causes review bias (STARD items 13a/13b), compounding the verification problem and inflating apparent agreement. A single unmasked, unreplicated grader provides no measure of reference-standard reliability, weakening the gold standard on which all accuracy estimates depend. (Raised by all four reviewers; one split masking and single-reader concerns into two items.)

**Suggested action.** State explicitly whether reference graders were masked to the index result (and vice versa) and the time interval between index test and reference standard. Use at least two independent graders with a pre-specified adjudication rule and report inter-grader agreement (e.g., kappa). If masking was impossible given the verification design, acknowledge it as a bias in the limitations.

**Problem.** "Referable diabetic retinopathy" — the target condition — is never defined. No grading scale, severity threshold, or statement on whether diabetic macular edema / maculopathy is included is given for either the AI or the reference standard.

**Why it matters.** Accuracy is undefined and the study is not reproducible without a precise target-condition definition (STARD item 9); "referable" can span very different severity cut-points, preventing comparison with other studies. The index test and reference standard must demonstrably target the same construct, and omitting macular edema risks missing sight-threatening disease that single-field smartphone images may not capture. (Raised by three of four reviewers.)

**Suggested action.** Define referable DR against a named scale (e.g., the International Clinical Diabetic Retinopathy scale or ETDRS), state the explicit severity threshold, confirm whether diabetic macular edema is included and how it is detected/counted, confirm the classifier and the ophthalmologist used the same definition, and state how borderline/ungradable cases were classified.

**Problem.** There is no participant flow diagram, and the counts are not reconciled. The path eligible → approached → imaged → gradable → flagged/cleared → verified by reference standard → analysed is not traceable; the numbers eligible/excluded and the number verified are unknown; ungradable or excluded images are unaccounted for; the time interval between index test and reference standard is not reported; and the manuscript does not follow STARD 2015.

**Why it matters.** Without a flow diagram the selective verification and any exclusions are hidden, making risk-of-bias and applicability assessment impossible (STARD item 19). A long index-to-reference interval could allow disease progression to misclassify results. (Raised by three of four reviewers.)

**Suggested action.** Add a STARD 2015 flow diagram with counts at each stage (eligible, imaged, gradable, flagged vs cleared, verified by reference standard, analysed, excluded with reasons), complete and cite the STARD 2015 checklist, reconcile every count from eligible to analysed, and report the index-to-reference time interval.

**Problem.** The unit of analysis is unstated. Each participant has two eyes (and possibly multiple images), but the manuscript does not say whether accuracy is computed per patient, per eye, or per image, nor how the two eyes are combined into a referral decision. (Raised by one reviewer.)

**Why it matters.** Per-eye analyses that ignore within-patient correlation understate variance and produce falsely narrow confidence intervals; mixing eyes and patients confuses denominators. The choice also changes the clinical meaning, since a patient-level referral decision differs from an eye-level one.

**Suggested action.** Specify the unit of analysis and the rule for combining eyes into a patient-level decision (e.g., worse eye drives referral), and account for within-patient clustering in the variance/CI estimates.

**Problem.** There is no statement of ethics committee / IRB approval, informed consent, or prospective study registration. (Raised by one reviewer.)

**Why it matters.** Human-subjects diagnostic research requires ethical oversight and consent, and prospective registration is expected by most clinical journals; their absence is a compliance and research-integrity gap.

**Suggested action.** Add IRB/ethics approval details, the consent procedure, and a registration identifier (or state and justify why registration was not done).

**Problem.** No sample-size or target-precision justification is given for the accuracy estimates; the verified subset that actually contributes to sensitivity/specificity is much smaller and unspecified.

**Why it matters.** Sensitivity is driven by the number of confirmed true cases; with an unstated number of verified positives the estimate may be imprecise or underpowered, and it is unclear whether the study can estimate accuracy with clinically useful confidence-interval width (STARD item 18). (Raised by all four reviewers.)

**Suggested action.** Provide a sample-size/precision rationale (assumed prevalence, expected sensitivity/specificity, target CI width) or, post hoc, report the achieved precision (CI half-widths) and the number of confirmed referable cases underpinning the sensitivity estimate, acknowledging the limits this places on the conclusions.

**Problem.** The declarations state "no conflicts of interest" and support by "a departmental grant," but the study evaluates a specific embedded commercial-style AI device whose developer/manufacturer relationship, data provenance, funder role, and the authors' analytic independence are not disclosed.

**Why it matters.** Device-evaluation studies with a favorable, deployment-oriented conclusion require explicit statements on manufacturer involvement (device/algorithm provision, funding, data access, role in design/analysis). A blanket "no conflicts" plus vague funding raises undisclosed-COI concerns and undermines transparency and credibility. (One reviewer rated this major; consolidated as minor across four reviewers.)

**Suggested action.** Disclose who supplied/owns the hardware, software, and algorithm; whether the developer/manufacturer funded or participated in study design, conduct, data access, analysis, or reporting; name the funding source specifically and the sponsor's role; and state that the authors had full data access and independent control of the analysis.

**Problem.** The abstract is unstructured and omits the design, setting, participants, index test, reference standard, the number verified/analysed, and confidence intervals, while ending on an unsupported deployment claim.

**Why it matters.** Clinical-journal abstracts are frequently read standalone; missing N and precision, and an overreaching conclusion, misrepresent the strength of evidence at the point most readers stop. (Raised by three of four reviewers; the conclusion overreach itself is addressed in the conclusions item.)

**Suggested action.** Use a structured abstract (Objective / Design / Setting / Participants / Index test / Reference standard / Results with N and 95% CIs / Conclusion) and ensure the stated conclusion matches the calibrated, verification-bias-aware, single-site findings.

items.section_ref: Methods / Results (reference-standard application; STARD-12, 17; also STARD-7, 11)
items.content_md:
**Problem (raised by all four reviewers).** The reference standard (dilated fundus examination) was applied to every participant the classifier flagged as *referable* but to only an unspecified, non-random *sample* of those it *cleared* (~278 cleared participants). Verification was therefore conditional on the index-test result.

**Why it matters.** This is partial verification bias — and, because the cleared arm was only sampled, differential verification bias. False negatives among the unverified cleared participants are extrapolated from a fraction rather than measured, which typically inflates sensitivity and distorts specificity. The headline estimates (sensitivity 0.89, specificity 0.91) cannot be read as unbiased estimates of diagnostic accuracy, and the 2×2 denominators are incomplete and non-reconstructable from the text (STARD-12, STARD-17). Because every downstream conclusion rests on these two numbers, this is the foundational threat to the paper.

**Suggested action.** Either (a) apply the reference standard to all participants, or to a *random, pre-specified* sample of classifier-negative participants independent of the index result, and weight accordingly; or (b) report the exact verification (sampling) fraction in each arm — how many of the 410 imaged and 132 flagged actually received the reference standard — and apply an established verification-bias correction (e.g., Begg–Greenes / inverse-probability weighting), presenting both crude and corrected estimates with confidence intervals. State explicitly how false negatives were ascertained. If neither is feasible, reframe the study as a triage/audit of flagged cases rather than a sensitivity/specificity estimate, remove the accuracy claims, and acknowledge residual verification bias as a primary limitation.

items.section_ref: Abstract (final sentence) and Discussion ("can replace specialist screening," "wider rollout should proceed")
items.content_md:
**Problem (raised by all four reviewers).** The Abstract states the system is "accurate and ready for deployment in primary care"; the Discussion states smartphone AI "can replace specialist screening in primary care" and that "wider rollout should proceed." These are deployment/replacement/policy claims.

**Why it matters.** They are not supported by the data: the accuracy estimates are biased (verification bias, above); the study is single-site with no external/temporal/multi-site validation; there are no confidence intervals; the studied population (endocrinology clinic) differs from the proposed primary-care setting; and ungradable-image handling, referral pathways, cost/implementation, and patient-relevant outcomes are not assessed. Diagnostic accuracy is not clinical or implementation effectiveness, and "replace specialist screening" is an especially strong claim a single accuracy study cannot license. Overstated conclusions risk premature, unsafe adoption — missed referable disease in a screen-and-clear workflow can cause preventable vision loss.

**Suggested action.** Rewrite the Abstract and Discussion conclusions to match the evidence: report what was measured, in which population, with which limitations, and frame the device as showing promise that requires prospective, fully verified, multi-site validation in the intended primary-care population — and ideally downstream-outcome evidence — before any deployment decision. Delete "ready for deployment," "can replace specialist screening," and "wider rollout should proceed."

items.section_ref: Results (2×2 cross-tabulation, STARD-17; participant-flow diagram, STARD-19)
items.content_md:
**Problem (raised by all four reviewers).** No cross-tabulation of index-test results against the reference standard is provided, and there is no participant-flow diagram. The Results give only 410 imaged, 132 flagged referable, and the two summary indices; the number verified in each arm and the cell counts (TP/FP/FN/TN) are absent.

**Why it matters.** STARD-17 requires the 2×2 table and STARD requires a participant-flow diagram; without them the 0.89/0.91 cannot be reproduced or checked, the impact of the partial-verification design cannot be assessed by the reader, and prevalence, predictive values, and the indeterminate fraction cannot be reconstructed.

**Suggested action.** Add the full 2×2 table (TP/FP/FN/TN) with the actual counts used, clearly labeling how many index-negative (cleared) participants were verified and the sampling fraction applied, and show the arithmetic linking the cells to the reported sensitivity/specificity. Add a STARD-style flow diagram: imaged → gradable → index result → verified in each arm → analyzed, accounting for every participant and any exclusions.

**Problem (raised by all four reviewers).** Sensitivity (0.89) and specificity (0.91) are reported as bare point estimates: no 95% confidence intervals, no description of the estimation method, and no defined unit of analysis (per-eye vs per-patient) or handling of clustering from two eyes per participant.

**Why it matters.** STARD-13a requires the estimation methods, and precision is essential for interpretation — with ~410 imaged, ~132 flagged, an unstated number verified, and false negatives inferred from a subsample, the intervals around the estimates are likely wide and material to any deployment claim. Diabetic retinopathy is graded per eye, so treating eyes as independent understates uncertainty, while a per-patient "referable" definition must be stated explicitly (including how a patient with discordant eyes is classified).

**Suggested action.** Report 95% CIs for sensitivity, specificity (and predictive values), name the interval method (e.g., exact/Wilson binomial), describe the estimation procedure including any weighting for the verification sampling fraction, define the analysis unit, specify how discordant eyes were handled, and use methods that account for eye-level clustering.

**Problem (raised by all four reviewers).** The index test is described only as "a smartphone camera with an embedded deep-learning classifier." Missing: device/camera model (and adapter), classifier name and software/model version, training-data provenance, the operating threshold/cutoff defining "referable," image-acquisition protocol (mydriatic vs non-mydriatic, number of fields/images per eye, one vs both eyes), and who captured the images and their training/qualifications.

**Why it matters.** STARD-10a requires the index test to be described in enough detail to replicate it. AI performance is version- and threshold-dependent and degrades with acquisition conditions; the decision threshold directly sets the sensitivity/specificity trade-off, so without it the operating point is uninterpretable, the study is not reproducible, and the result cannot be attributed to a specific, regulable product.

**Suggested action.** Specify the device and classifier with version numbers, training-data provenance, the full image-capture protocol and pupil status, the number/quality criteria of images per eye and how they were combined, the operator role/training, and the exact threshold/output mapping used to call "referable." State whether the classifier was locked/pre-specified or tuned on these data.

**Problem (raised by all four reviewers).** The reference standard is given only as "dilated fundus examination by an ophthalmologist." Missing: the grading scale (e.g., ICDR/ETDRS), the operational definition of "referable" diabetic retinopathy (e.g., moderate NPDR or worse, and/or any DMO), the number and experience of graders, adjudication of disagreements, and any inter-rater reliability.

**Why it matters.** STARD-11 requires a replicable reference standard. "Referable DR" is the target condition for both the index test and the reference standard; if their definitions differ, the comparison is ill-posed. A single grader without a reliability check is a weak reference.

**Suggested action.** State the grading scale and the explicit severity threshold defining "referable," the number and qualifications of graders, the disagreement-resolution/adjudication procedure, and inter-rater agreement where multiple graders were used — and confirm the same target-condition definition was used for the index and reference tests.

**Problem (separated explicitly by r4; also raised by r1, r2, r3).** Reference-standard grading was triggered by the classifier result (flagged participants, plus a sample of cleared ones), so the ophthalmologist plausibly knew or could infer the index-test output. Whether reference graders were masked to the classifier result is not reported.

**Why it matters.** Knowledge of the index result during reference grading produces review/incorporation bias and can inflate apparent agreement — a bias layered on top of the verification bias above. STARD requires reporting whether reference assessors were blinded to the index-test result.

**Suggested action.** Conduct and report reference grading masked to the classifier output; decouple the decision to verify from the index result (verify a defined random sample regardless of classifier output); and state the masking procedure explicitly. If graders were not blinded, address this as an explicit limitation.

items.section_ref: Results / Abstract (prevalence and predictive values; STARD-1)
items.content_md:
**Problem (dedicated major item in r2, r3, r4; also noted by r1).** Neither the prevalence of referable DR in the cohort nor predictive values (PPV/NPV) are reported.

**Why it matters.** For a screening-deployment claim, PPV/NPV at the relevant prevalence are arguably more decision-relevant than sensitivity/specificity — they govern how many flagged patients truly have referable disease (referral burden) and how many cleared patients are falsely reassured (missed-disease risk). STARD-1 expects predictive values, and predictive values shift sharply with the lower prevalence expected in primary care versus an endocrinology clinic.

**Suggested action.** Report the prevalence of referable DR in the verified sample and PPV/NPV with confidence intervals, and present predictive values across a plausible range of lower prevalences for the intended primary-care setting.

**Problem (raised by all four reviewers).** Participants were consecutive adults with diabetes attending an endocrinology clinic — a referral-enriched population with likely higher prevalence and more advanced retinopathy — yet the conclusions recommend primary-care screening. The disease-severity/prevalence distribution of the sample is not reported.

**Why it matters.** Diagnostic accuracy, and especially predictive values, are spectrum- and prevalence-dependent. Estimates from a specialty-clinic sample do not transfer to a lower-prevalence, milder primary-care screening population (spectrum bias), directly undermining the applicability/deployment claim independent of the verification issue.

**Suggested action.** Report the disease-severity/prevalence distribution of the cohort, confine applicability claims to the studied setting, explicitly discuss expected spectrum/prevalence effects (including predictive values at primary-care prevalence), and ideally validate in a representative primary-care cohort before any primary-care recommendation.

**Problem (raised by all four reviewers).** There is no mention of ungradable or indeterminate results from the smartphone index test or the reference examination, no image-quality exclusions, and it is unclear whether the "410 imaged" denominator already excludes acquisition/grading failures.

**Why it matters.** Smartphone/non-mydriatic fundus imaging has a clinically significant ungradable rate, and how ungradable cases are counted (excluded vs treated as referred/positive) is one of the most important real-world determinants of a screening program's value and referral burden. Silently dropping them inflates apparent accuracy and hides a key operational limitation. STARD-14 requires this to be reported.

**Suggested action.** Report the number/percentage of ungradable images for both the index test and the reference standard and how they were handled, and present an "intention-to-screen" sensitivity analysis that counts ungradable images as screen-positive (refer) vs excluded, plus the implied referral burden if ungradable cases must be re-imaged or referred.

**Problem (raised by all four reviewers; minor in r1/r2/r3, major in r4).** Eligibility is described only as "consecutive adults with diabetes attending an endocrinology clinic." Missing: explicit inclusion/exclusion criteria (age range, diabetes type/duration, prior DR diagnosis or treatment/laser/anti-VEGF, media opacity/cataract, prior vitrectomy, prior ophthalmic care), recruitment dates and site(s), and confirmation that enrollment was truly consecutive.

**Why it matters.** STARD-6/7 require eligibility and the basis of participant identification so readers can judge selection bias and generalizability. Including previously treated or known-DR patients materially changes both prevalence and the meaning of "referable."

**Suggested action.** Provide a complete inclusion/exclusion list, the recruitment period and site(s), how consecutive enrollment was operationalized (all attendees vs clinician-referred), and how many eligible patients were missed or declined.

**Problem (raised by all four reviewers).** The manuscript does not state whether data collection was planned before the index test and reference standard were performed (prospective vs retrospective), and gives no protocol, registration, or pre-specification of the operating threshold.

**Why it matters.** STARD-5 requires this because retrospective/post-hoc designs carry higher risk of selection and verification bias and of optimistic post-hoc threshold selection; given the partial-verification concern, the timing is material to interpreting the estimates.

**Suggested action.** State the design timing explicitly, cite any trial/registry identifier or protocol, and indicate whether the operating threshold and primary analysis were pre-specified (locked) before testing.

**Problem (raised by r2; also noted by r3).** There is no a priori sample-size calculation or target-precision justification for sensitivity/specificity.

**Why it matters.** With 132 flagged and an unstated number of verified negatives, the study may be underpowered to estimate sensitivity tightly; without a precision target the reader cannot judge whether the sample supports the confidence the conclusions assert.

**Suggested action.** Provide the sample-size rationale (target CI width or expected sensitivity/specificity with margin) or, if none was pre-specified, report the achieved precision and acknowledge its absence as a limitation.

**Problem (raised by r1).** The abstract is unstructured, omits the sample size and key accuracy estimates (prevalence, confidence intervals, predictive values), and closes with a deployment claim rather than a results summary.

**Why it matters.** STARD-1 calls for identification as a diagnostic-accuracy study with the key estimates; sensitivity/specificity without N, prevalence, and predictive values can mislead clinical readers about real-world performance, especially for a screening recommendation.

**Suggested action.** Provide a structured abstract reporting N, prevalence of referable DR, sensitivity and specificity with 95% CIs, and PPV/NPV at the observed prevalence, and replace the deployment sentence with a calibrated accuracy summary. (Specific fixes to the deployment claim, confidence intervals, and predictive values are detailed in the related conclusions, precision, and predictive-value items.)

**Problem (raised by all four reviewers).** The device under evaluation is an embedded commercial-style AI product, yet the authors declare "no conflicts of interest" and describe funding only as "a departmental grant."

**Why it matters.** Undisclosed relationships with a device manufacturer (funding, device/software provision, model/data access, or involvement in design/analysis/reporting) are a recognized source of bias in diagnostic-AI studies and are required disclosures for clinical journals. Vague declarations weaken trust in an already deployment-oriented paper.

**Suggested action.** Disclose the device/algorithm manufacturer and any author/manufacturer relationship, whether hardware/software or analytical support was provided, who had access to the model and data, and the funder's specific role (or explicitly state none) in design, conduct, analysis, and the decision to publish; name the funding source rather than "a departmental grant."

**Problem.** The manuscript draws causal and prescriptive conclusions from a single observational cohort. The Abstract states "Regular physical activity reduces the risk of late-life depression and should be prescribed as first-line prevention," and the Discussion asserts "Because physical activity directly lowers depression risk, clinicians should prescribe structured exercise to prevent late-life depression."

**Why it matters.** A prospective cohort can establish association, not causation. Residual confounding and reverse causation are not excluded (see related findings), so causal verbs ("reduces," "directly lowers") and a "first-line prevention" recommendation are not supportable and could misdirect clinical practice.

**Suggested action.** Replace causal/prescriptive language with associational wording throughout (e.g., "higher baseline activity was associated with lower depression risk"). Remove the first-line-prevention recommendation, or reframe it explicitly as a hypothesis that requires confirmation in randomized trials.

**Problem.** The Cox model is specified with "activity tertile as the exposure" and no covariates. No confounders are measured or adjusted (e.g., baseline physical health and chronic-disease burden, functional/mobility limitation, cognitive status, social engagement/isolation, sex, education, BMI, smoking). The statement that "the association persisted across age strata" addresses only age.

**Why it matters.** Less active older adults are systematically sicker and more socially isolated, all of which independently raise depression risk. An unadjusted hazard ratio (HR 0.72) therefore likely overstates any protective association and cannot be interpreted as the activity effect.

**Suggested action.** Present a baseline characteristics table by activity tertile, pre-specify a confounder set with rationale, and report adjusted models alongside the crude estimate. State which variables were available and acknowledge any unmeasured confounders.

**Problem.** Reverse causation is not addressed. Subclinical depressive symptoms (below the GDS < 5 eligibility threshold) can reduce physical activity before depression is formally detected, so low baseline activity may be an early manifestation of depression rather than a cause.

**Why it matters.** Reverse causation could generate the observed association with no true preventive effect, directly undermining the central conclusion.

**Suggested action.** Add analyses to probe this, e.g., a lag/sensitivity analysis excluding events in the first 6-12 months of follow-up, and adjustment for baseline GDS as a continuous covariate. Discuss reverse causation explicitly as a limitation.

**Problem.** The effect is reported only as "hazard ratio 0.72, p < 0.05." No confidence interval is given anywhere, the middle-tertile estimate is omitted, and no test for trend across the three tertiles is reported.

**Why it matters.** A p-value without a confidence interval conveys neither precision nor effect size, and reporting only the extreme tertiles hides any dose-response pattern. This does not meet standard cohort reporting (STROBE item 16).

**Suggested action.** Report HRs with 95% confidence intervals for both upper tertiles versus the referent, the number of events and person-time per tertile, and a formal test for linear trend across tertiles.

**Problem.** Incident depression is defined as "a Geriatric Depression Scale score of 5 or higher, or a new clinical diagnosis." A GDS screening cut-off is not a diagnosis, and combining a self-report threshold with clinician diagnosis creates a heterogeneous composite outcome. The same instrument (GDS) also defines baseline exclusion, so screen-positivity drives both eligibility and the endpoint.

**Why it matters.** Screen-positive status overcounts true clinical depression and may share determinants with inactivity (e.g., poor health, fatigue), biasing the estimate. A mixed screen-plus-diagnosis endpoint is difficult to interpret and compare across studies.

**Suggested action.** Define a single primary outcome (e.g., clinical diagnosis), report the GDS-threshold outcome separately, state how many events came from each source, and add a confirmed-diagnosis sensitivity analysis.

**Problem.** 512 participants were enrolled but only 498 were analyzed, with no explanation for the 14 excluded. Loss to follow-up over the mean 4.2 years is not described, and neither censoring nor the competing risk of death (substantial in adults aged 65+) is addressed.

**Why it matters.** Differential dropout and mortality compete with the depression outcome; treating deaths as ordinary censored observations in a standard Cox model can bias incidence estimates in this age group, and unexplained exclusions raise selection-bias concerns.

**Suggested action.** Add a participant-flow diagram with numbers and reasons for exclusion and loss to follow-up, state explicitly how censoring (including death) was handled, and consider a competing-risks (subdistribution hazard) analysis.

**Problem.** The Discussion contains no limitations paragraph; it moves directly from the result to prescriptive recommendations and "support [for] population-level activity promotion."

**Why it matters.** Omitting limitations (confounding, reverse causation, self-reported and single-time-point exposure, outcome misclassification, generalizability) overstates certainty and is expected by clinical journals and STROBE.

**Suggested action.** Add a dedicated limitations paragraph covering these issues and temper the concluding claims to match the strength of the evidence.

**Problem.** Leisure-time physical activity is self-reported at a single baseline time point. The named "validated questionnaire" is not specified or cited, and activity is assumed constant over the 4.2-year follow-up.

**Why it matters.** Self-report is subject to recall and social-desirability bias, and a single baseline measure ignores change in activity over time (regression dilution). Misclassification, if differential by mood, could bias the estimate in either direction.

**Suggested action.** Name and cite the specific instrument and its validation, acknowledge exposure-measurement limitations, and use repeated measures if available or state the single-measurement assumption.

**Problem.** The Declarations report only "no competing interests." There is no ethics/IRB approval statement, no informed-consent statement, and no funding or data-availability statement.

**Why it matters.** Clinical journals require ethics approval and consent disclosures for human-subjects research; their absence can preclude peer review or publication.

**Suggested action.** Add the approving ethics committee (name and reference number), an informed-consent statement, a funding statement, and a data-availability statement.

**Problem.** The Introduction motivates the study by noting that "prospective evidence in the oldest old is limited," but the cohort enrolls community-dwelling adults aged 65 or older, not specifically the oldest old (commonly defined as 85+), and no age distribution is reported.

**Why it matters.** The framing overstates the study's novelty and scope relative to the sample actually studied.

**Suggested action.** Either restrict the framing to "older adults aged 65 and over" or report the age distribution and present the number of participants and results within an oldest-old subgroup.

**Problem.** Cox proportional-hazards models are used, but there is no statement that the proportional-hazards assumption was assessed.

**Why it matters.** Over a mean 4.2-year follow-up the hazard ratio may not be constant; an unchecked PH violation would make a single reported HR misleading.

**Suggested action.** Report how the PH assumption was evaluated (e.g., Schoenfeld residuals or log-log plots) and describe any remedial modeling if it was violated.

**Problem.** A single observational cohort is used to assert causation and a clinical directive. The Abstract states "Regular physical activity reduces the risk of late-life depression and should be prescribed as first-line prevention," and the Discussion states "Because physical activity directly lowers depression risk, clinicians should prescribe structured exercise" and "Our findings support population-level activity promotion." These are causal-mechanism claims ("reduces," "directly lowers") plus a prescribing/policy recommendation built on an essentially unadjusted association.

**Why it matters.** A prospective cohort establishes association, not causation. Residual confounding and reverse causation (see related findings) are at least as plausible as a true effect, no trial evidence is presented, and trial evidence for exercise as *prevention* of late-life depression is in fact modest and mixed. Labeling exercise "first-line prevention" overstates the evidence base and could misdirect clinical practice and guideline interpretation — a core honesty-of-reporting failure. The conclusion is also internally inconsistent with the design and the single HR shown.

**Suggested action.** Reframe all conclusions as associational (e.g., "higher baseline activity was associated with lower incident depression"). Delete "reduces," "directly lowers," "first-line prevention," "should be prescribed," and "population-level promotion." State explicitly that causality cannot be inferred and that randomized or quasi-experimental confirmation is required before any prescribing claim; confine clinical implications to a hypothesis-generating statement.

*Raised by r1, r2, r3, r4 (all critical).*

**Problem.** The model is described only as "Cox proportional-hazards models with activity tertile as the exposure," with the sole robustness statement that the association "persisted across age strata." No covariates are listed or adjusted and no crude-vs-adjusted comparison is given, so the HR 0.72 reads as a crude/unadjusted estimate. Strong prognostic confounders of both activity and depression go unmentioned: baseline chronic-disease burden/comorbidity, disability and mobility limitation, cognitive status, BMI, smoking, alcohol, socioeconomic position, social engagement/isolation, and baseline subthreshold depressive symptoms (GDS 0–4).

**Why it matters.** Leisure-time activity in older adults is heavily confounded by underlying health and social circumstances: healthier, less disabled, more connected adults are both more active and less likely to become depressed. An essentially unadjusted HR is expected to be biased away from the null and cannot be interpreted as an effect of activity, which undermines the central result.

**Suggested action.** Report a pre-specified, covariate-adjusted Cox model (ideally justified with a DAG), present crude and fully adjusted HRs side by side with 95% CIs, add a baseline-characteristics table by activity tertile, and describe how each confounder was measured. Note that ~96 events constrain model complexity (~1 covariate per 8–10 events). Age stratification alone is insufficient; adjust for measured confounders jointly and discuss residual confounding. Follow STROBE items 7 and 16(a)/(b).

*Raised by r1 (critical), r2 (critical), r3 (critical), r4 (major) → critical.*

**Problem.** Reverse causation is not addressed. Eligibility excludes only baseline GDS ≥ 5, which does not remove participants with subthreshold/prodromal depression (e.g., GDS 3–4) or early undiagnosed illness — conditions that reduce leisure-time activity well before symptoms cross that threshold. Exposure is measured once, at baseline.

**Why it matters.** If low baseline activity is an early *manifestation* of incipient depression rather than a cause, the protective HR is inflated by reverse causation — one of the best-known and most likely alternative explanations in activity–mood cohorts, especially with a short (4.2-year) follow-up and cases arising early.

**Suggested action.** Add a landmark/lag sensitivity analysis excluding events in the first 1–2 years of follow-up; adjust for baseline subthreshold symptoms as a continuous GDS score rather than only dichotomizing at 5; model time-updated activity if data allow; and discuss reverse causation explicitly as a limitation, reporting how estimates change.

*Raised by r1, r2, r3, r4 → major.*

**Problem.** The only effect estimate is "hazard ratio 0.72, p < 0.05" for the highest-vs-lowest tertile, in both Abstract and Results. There is no 95% confidence interval, no exact p-value, no estimate for the middle tertile, no formal test for trend/dose-response, no events or person-years per tertile, no absolute incidence rates, and no total person-time.

**Why it matters.** Without a 95% CI and per-category estimates, the precision and shape of the association cannot be judged and "p < 0.05" is uninformative — with 96 events across three groups the interval could be wide, could nearly cross 1.0, and be compatible with a near-null effect. Omitting the middle tertile and a trend test leaves dose–response unestablished and raises selective-reporting concern about presenting only the most extreme contrast. This is below STROBE reporting expectations (item 16(a)).

**Suggested action.** Report adjusted (and crude) HRs with 95% CIs and exact p-values for each non-reference tertile versus the lowest, a formal test for linear trend, events and person-years per tertile, absolute incidence rates (e.g., per 1,000 person-years), and total person-time. State precisely which contrast each estimate represents.

*Raised by r1, r2, r3, r4 → major.*

**Problem.** Magnitude language is inflated and the exposure is mislabeled. An HR of 0.72 is roughly a 28% relative reduction — a moderate association — yet it is described as "substantially lower" (Abstract), "markedly lower" (Results), and participants being "far less likely" to become depressed (Discussion). The Abstract also attributes the effect to "higher baseline activity," whereas the estimate is a highest- vs lowest-tertile contrast.

**Why it matters.** Intensifiers overstate a moderate estimate, and conflating a categorical tertile contrast with continuous "higher activity" misrepresents what was measured; both erode the precision and credibility of the claim, and the absence of a dose–response gradient weakens any causal argument.

**Suggested action.** Calibrate the wording to the actual estimate (e.g., "a lower hazard"), remove "substantially/markedly/far" or tie them to the quantitative effect, and consistently describe the exposure as the highest vs lowest activity tertile.

*Raised by r4 (major); also noted by r3 → major.*

**Problem.** Leisure-time activity is self-reported once at baseline, the instrument is named only as a "validated questionnaire," and the variable is collapsed into sample-specific tertiles whose cutpoints are not reported. It is then treated as fixed across the mean 4.2-year follow-up.

**Why it matters.** (1) A single baseline measure ignores activity change over follow-up, which is substantial as health declines in this age group. (2) Self-report is prone to recall and social-desirability error; non-differential misclassification/regression dilution typically attenuates, but differential error (by mood/health) is unpredictable. (3) Tertiles discard dose information and use sample-dependent cut points that do not transfer across populations. (4) An unnamed instrument cannot be appraised for validity in adults 65+, where many activity questionnaires are poorly validated.

**Suggested action.** Name and cite the instrument and its validation population (ideally in adults 65+); report exposure in interpretable absolute units (e.g., MET-min/week) and give the tertile cutpoints; analyze activity continuously to assess dose–response in addition to tertiles; use repeated/time-varying activity if available; and discuss exposure misclassification and its likely direction of bias.

*Raised by r1 (major), r2 (major), r3 (minor) → major.*

**Problem.** No sample-size or power/precision justification is given. The study analyzes 498 participants with 96 incident events across a 3-level exposure that should also accommodate multiple confounders.

**Why it matters.** Without an a priori target, the study's ability to detect or precisely estimate an effect is unknown. With ~96 events, an adjusted model risks exceeding safe events-per-variable limits (roughly 8–10 events per covariate), inviting overfitting and unstable estimates — directly bearing on whether the reported HR is trustworthy.

**Suggested action.** Add an a priori power/precision rationale; if none exists, report a post hoc precision analysis (expected CI width) and the events-per-variable in the final model, constrain model complexity or use penalized estimation accordingly, and state this limitation explicitly.

*Raised by r1 (major); reinforced within the confounding/effect-reporting items of r2 and r3 → major.*

**Problem.** Incident depression is ascertained "at annual follow-up" — i.e., the outcome is interval-censored (known only to have occurred between two yearly assessments) — yet a standard Cox proportional-hazards model treating event times as continuously observed is used. The GDS-based component is interval-censored while clinical diagnoses may be dated continuously, and how event times and censoring were assigned is not described.

**Why it matters.** Forcing interval-censored events onto exact visit dates (or assigning event times inconsistently across components) can bias both the hazard ratio and its standard error, and the choice of imputed event time (visit date vs midpoint) affects results.

**Suggested action.** Use methods appropriate for interval-censored/discrete-time data (e.g., complementary log-log discrete-time hazard models or interval-censored survival models), or justify the Cox approximation and report a sensitivity analysis under alternative event-time assignment. Clearly state how event timing and censoring were handled.

*Raised by r1 (major), r2 (minor) → major.*

**Problem.** There is no statement that the proportional-hazards assumption underlying the Cox model was assessed (e.g., Schoenfeld residuals, log-log survival plots, or a time-by-exposure interaction).

**Why it matters.** A single HR summarizing 4.2 years assumes a constant hazard ratio; if the activity effect varies over time (plausible), the reported HR is a misleading time-averaged value and inference may be distorted.

**Suggested action.** Test and report the PH assumption for the exposure and key covariates. If violated, present time-varying effects, stratify, or report effects within defined time windows.

*Raised by r1, r2, r4 → minor.*

**Problem.** 512 were enrolled but 498 "were included in the analysis"; the 14-participant difference is never explained, the analytic denominator is inconsistent between Abstract (512) and Results (498), there is no participant-flow diagram (screened/eligible/excluded/analyzed), no reporting of loss to follow-up over the mean 4.2 years, and no stated strategy for missing exposure/covariate data. The implied attrition (14 losses over 4.2 years in adults 65+) is implausibly low.

**Why it matters.** STROBE requires that all participants be accounted for at each stage. Unexplained exclusions and differential attrition over a multi-year follow-up can bias incidence estimates and the HR — especially if dropout relates to both activity level and mood — and undocumented missing-data handling undermines reproducibility of the complete-case analysis.

**Suggested action.** Provide a STROBE participant-flow diagram with reasons and counts for every exclusion between enrollment and analysis, reconcile to a single consistent denominator, report loss to follow-up and compare it by activity tertile, and specify the missing-data approach (e.g., multiple imputation) with a complete-case vs imputed sensitivity comparison.

*Raised by r1, r2, r3, r4 → major.*

**Problem.** In community-dwelling adults aged 65+ followed a mean of 4.2 years, death is a substantial competing risk — and is itself likely more common in the least-active group — yet standard Cox censoring treats death as non-informative. Death is not mentioned in the participant flow.

**Why it matters.** Differential mortality can remove low-activity participants before they can be diagnosed with depression, and censoring deaths in a standard Cox model can overestimate cumulative incidence and bias the HR (typically toward apparent protection).

**Suggested action.** Report deaths and person-time by activity tertile and analyze depression with a competing-risk approach (cause-specific or Fine–Gray subdistribution hazards), or at minimum present a sensitivity analysis treating death as a competing event and compare it with the Cox result.

*Raised by r2, r3 → major.*

**Problem.** Incident depression is a heterogeneous composite — "a Geriatric Depression Scale score of 5 or higher, OR a new clinical diagnosis," ascertained at annual follow-up — then labeled simply "depression." GDS ≥ 5 is a screening threshold with imperfect specificity, not a diagnosis of major depression, and a single positive annual screen can reflect transient symptoms. The two components have different ascertainment, the number of events from each source is not reported, and the same GDS instrument/threshold defines both eligibility and outcome.

**Why it matters.** Combining screen-positives with clinically diagnosed cases pools outcomes of unequal validity and severity, inflates the event count, and can produce differential misclassification (e.g., more-active participants may have fewer care contacts and thus fewer recorded diagnoses), biasing the HR. Using the same GDS cut-point for eligibility and outcome also raises regression-to-the-mean concerns near the threshold, and the clinical meaning of "incident depression" is left ambiguous and hard to reproduce.

**Suggested action.** Rename the outcome precisely (e.g., "incident depressive symptoms (GDS ≥ 5) or a new clinical diagnosis"); state the GDS version (e.g., GDS-15) and its sensitivity/specificity in a comparable population and justify the cutoff; report how many of the 96 events were screen-only versus clinically diagnosed; analyze the components separately and run a sensitivity analysis restricted to clinically diagnosed cases; and consider requiring confirmation (repeat positive or clinical assessment) for screen-positives.

*Raised by r1 (minor), r2 (major), r3 (major), r4 (major) → major.*

**Problem.** Participants were recruited from two primary-care registries, yet the study is framed as "community-dwelling" and used to "support population-level activity promotion." No participation rate or characterization of non-participants is given.

**Why it matters.** Primary-care registrants are not a general-population sample — they differ in comorbidity, help-seeking, and access — so external validity is limited, selection bias cannot be assessed, and the population-level claims exceed the sampling frame.

**Suggested action.** Describe the registries, catchment, eligibility-to-enrollment flow, and participation rate; compare participants with non-participants where possible; and temper generalizability claims to the sampled population. Report STROBE items 6 and 13.

*Raised by r2 → major.*

**Problem.** The Discussion contains no limitations paragraph; it moves directly from the result to a causal mechanism and a population-level policy recommendation.

**Why it matters.** For an observational study in a clinical journal, an explicit limitations section is expected and is the main guardrail against overinterpretation; its absence is a structural gap that amplifies every overclaim elsewhere.

**Suggested action.** Add a dedicated limitations paragraph covering residual confounding, reverse causation, self-reported single-baseline exposure, a screening-based composite outcome, competing risk of death, single baseline measurement, and generalizability (two primary-care registries), followed by appropriately hedged conclusions.

*Raised by r4 (major); also noted by r2 → major.*

**Problem.** Declarations contain only a competing-interests statement. There is no ethics/IRB approval, no informed-consent statement, no funding statement (with funder role), no data-availability statement, and no statement of adherence to a reporting guideline.

**Why it matters.** A prospective human-subjects cohort in a clinical journal requires ethics-approval and consent statements; funding, data-availability, and reporting-guideline disclosures are standard mandatory elements for appraisal of integrity and reproducibility.

**Suggested action.** Add ethics-committee approval (with committee/reference) and informed-consent statements, a funding statement with funder role, a data-availability statement, and confirm reporting against STROBE (include a completed checklist).

*Raised by r2, r4 → minor.*

**Problem.** The manuscript contains no citations. The Introduction asserts prior evidence ("Observational data have linked physical activity to better mood") without references, and neither the activity questionnaire nor the Geriatric Depression Scale is named (by version) or cited.

**Why it matters.** Background claims and measurement instruments must be sourced so readers can verify them and judge instrument validity; this is basic reporting mechanics for a clinical journal.

**Suggested action.** Add references for the background statements and name + cite the specific activity questionnaire and the GDS version (e.g., GDS-15) used.

*Raised by r4 → minor.*

**Problem.** The Introduction motivates the study by noting that "prospective evidence in the *oldest old* is limited," but the sample is adults aged 65 and older (mean follow-up 4.2 years). "Oldest old" conventionally denotes ages 85+.

**Why it matters.** The framing overstates the novelty/scope of the sample and creates a mismatch between the stated evidence gap and the population actually studied.

**Suggested action.** Report the baseline age distribution and either justify the "oldest old" framing with those data or reword to "community-dwelling older adults."

*Raised by r4 → minor.*

**Problem.** "The association persisted across age strata" is asserted with no supporting data — no stratum-specific hazard ratios, no confidence intervals, and no interaction test — yet it is used to bolster the overall conclusion.

**Why it matters.** A subgroup/consistency claim with no numbers cannot be verified or reproduced.

**Suggested action.** Either report the stratum-specific HRs (with 95% CIs) and an interaction p-value, or remove the sentence.

*Raised by r4 (major); also noted by r2 → major.*

**Problem.** The only model described is "Cox proportional-hazards models with activity tertile as the exposure," and the Results report a single hazard ratio (0.72) with no indication that any covariates were adjusted for. There is no confounder set, and no separate unadjusted vs. adjusted estimates.

**Why it matters.** Leisure-time activity in older adults is strongly correlated with age, sex, baseline physical health and comorbidity burden, functional and cognitive status, smoking, BMI, socioeconomic status, and social engagement — all of which independently predict depression. An unadjusted HR is almost certainly confounded, so the headline 0.72 cannot bear the causal and clinical weight the paper places on it. STROBE-12 explicitly requires describing methods used to control for confounding, and STROBE-16 requires confounder-adjusted estimates.

**Suggested action.** Pre-specify a confounder set (justified by prior literature or a DAG), and report both crude and adjusted HRs with 95% CIs for each tertile. State how covariates were selected. If adjustment was in fact performed, it must be described and reported; if it was not, the analysis needs to be redone before any inference is drawn.

**Problem.** Exposure is measured once at baseline, depression is screened annually, and mean follow-up is only 4.2 years. Reverse causation is not addressed anywhere.

**Why it matters.** Prodromal/subclinical depression reduces physical activity before it crosses the GDS threshold. With short follow-up and a baseline-only exposure, participants in the lowest activity tertile may already be in an early depressive process, which would inflate the apparent protective effect and directly undermine the claim that activity "directly lowers" risk.

**Suggested action.** Add lag/sensitivity analyses that exclude incident events in the first 1–2 years (or a landmark analysis), and report whether the association attenuates. Discuss reverse causation explicitly as a limitation. Ideally restrict to participants with a robustly depression-free baseline window.

**Problem.** The paper draws causal and prescriptive conclusions from a single observational cohort: "Regular physical activity reduces the risk of late-life depression and should be prescribed as first-line prevention" (Abstract); "Because physical activity directly lowers depression risk, clinicians should prescribe structured exercise" (Discussion).

**Why it matters.** An observational association — especially an apparently unadjusted one vulnerable to confounding and reverse causation — cannot establish that activity *directly lowers* risk, and certainly cannot license a first-line *prescription* recommendation. STROBE-1b requires a balanced abstract summary. This overreach is the manuscript's central weakness and will mislead clinical readers.

**Suggested action.** Rewrite all causal verbs to associational language (e.g., "higher baseline activity was associated with lower hazard of incident depression"). Remove the clinical prescription recommendation, or reframe it as hypothesis-generating and explicitly defer to randomized trial evidence (which exists for exercise and depression and should be cited rather than re-derived here).

**Problem.** The Discussion contains no limitations section at all.

**Why it matters.** STROBE-19 requires discussion of limitations and sources of bias. The absence is not merely a reporting gap; it is what allows the unqualified causal conclusion to stand. For this design the omissions are consequential.

**Suggested action.** Add a limitations paragraph covering, at minimum: residual confounding, reverse causation, single self-reported baseline exposure, outcome misclassification from a screening cutoff, attrition/competing risk of death, and limited generalizability from two primary-care registries. Each limitation should be tied to its likely direction of bias.

**Problem.** The effect estimate is reported only as "hazard ratio 0.72, p < 0.05." No 95% confidence interval is given, the middle tertile is never reported, no adjusted estimate is distinguished from the crude one, and "the association persisted across age strata" is stated with no supporting numbers.

**Why it matters.** STROBE-16 requires confidence intervals, and a bare "p < 0.05" conveys neither magnitude precision nor whether the estimate is borderline. Omitting the middle tertile hides whether there is any dose-response, which is central to an activity-depression argument.

**Suggested action.** Report HR with 95% CI for the middle and highest tertiles vs. the lowest reference, for both crude and adjusted models, plus a test for trend across tertiles. Provide the age-stratified estimates rather than asserting consistency.

**Problem.** 512 were followed but 498 are "included in the analysis," with the 14-person discrepancy unexplained. There is no description of loss to follow-up, censoring, deaths, or person-time, and no flow diagram.

**Why it matters.** STROBE-13 requires numbers of participants at each stage and reasons for non-inclusion; a flow diagram is encouraged. In an oldest-old cohort over a mean 4.2 years, attrition and competing mortality are substantial and can bias the estimate; readers cannot assess this from the current text.

**Suggested action.** Add a participant flow diagram with reasons for the 14 exclusions, numbers lost to follow-up and censored, deaths during follow-up, and total person-years. Address competing risk of death if relevant.

**Problem.** Incident depression is defined as "a Geriatric Depression Scale score of 5 or higher, **or** a new clinical diagnosis." These are heterogeneous criteria, and GDS ≥ 5 is a screening threshold, not a diagnostic standard.

**Why it matters.** A GDS screen-positive cutoff has imperfect specificity and over-captures non-cases, so the composite outcome likely includes substantial misclassification. Combining a screen and a clinical diagnosis without reporting how many met each makes the outcome hard to interpret and may bias the HR in an unpredictable direction.

**Suggested action.** Specify the primary outcome precisely, cite the validation supporting the GDS cutoff used, and report how many incident cases met each criterion. Add a sensitivity analysis using only the stricter clinical-diagnosis outcome.

**Problem.** The exposure is a self-reported baseline questionnaire described only as "validated," with no name, no citation, no units, and no tertile cut-points. It is measured once.

**Why it matters.** Without naming the instrument and giving cut-points (e.g., MET-min/week), the analysis is not reproducible and the exposure categories cannot be judged. Self-report at a single timepoint also introduces measurement error and feeds the reverse-causation concern above.

**Suggested action.** Name and cite the validated questionnaire, report the tertile boundaries with units, and state the recall window. Acknowledge nondifferential misclassification of a self-reported single-timepoint exposure as a limitation.

**Problem.** There is no explanation of how the study size (n = 512) was arrived at, and no power consideration for the stratified analyses.

**Why it matters.** STROBE-10 requires this. With 96 events split across tertiles and further across age strata, power for the subgroup claims is likely limited, which bears on the "persisted across age strata" statement.

**Suggested action.** State the sample-size rationale (or acknowledge a convenience/registry-determined sample) and comment on the number of events relative to the number of covariates and subgroups analyzed.

**Problem.** The Introduction states a general aim ("examined the association") but no specific, pre-specified objective or a priori hypothesis.

**Why it matters.** STROBE-3 calls for stated objectives including any pre-specified hypotheses; this also frames whether the tertile and age-stratified analyses were planned or post hoc.

**Suggested action.** Add an explicit objective and a priori hypothesis (including the planned exposure contrast and any pre-specified subgroup analyses).

**Problem.** The Declarations report competing interests but omit any funding statement.

**Why it matters.** STROBE-22 requires the funding source and the role of funders for the study and, where applicable, the original cohort.

**Suggested action.** Add a funding statement naming the source(s) and describing the funder's role, or state explicitly that the study received no funding.

**Problem.** The statistical methods do not mention whether the proportional-hazards assumption was checked, how missing exposure/covariate data were handled, or how the age-stratified analysis was performed.

**Why it matters.** STROBE-12 requires the full analytic approach, including examination of subgroups and handling of missing data; an unverified PH assumption can invalidate the reported HR.

**Suggested action.** State how the PH assumption was assessed (e.g., Schoenfeld residuals), describe the missing-data approach (complete-case vs. imputation, with counts), and pre-specify the subgroup/interaction tests underlying the age-strata claim.

**Problem.** A single observational cohort is reported with explicitly causal, prescriptive, and policy language: the Abstract states "Regular physical activity *reduces* the risk of late-life depression and *should be prescribed as first-line prevention*," and the Discussion states "physical activity *directly lowers* depression risk," "clinicians *should prescribe* structured exercise," and calls for "population-level activity promotion." The supporting result is a single HR of 0.72 that appears to be unadjusted (see confounding item).

**Why it matters.** A prospective association — even if adjusted — cannot establish that activity *directly lowers* or *reduces* risk, cannot license a first-line treatment recommendation, and cannot support a population-policy claim. Residual confounding and reverse causation remain plausible and unaddressed, and randomized exercise-for-depression-prevention trials show smaller, less consistent effects. This is the single largest mismatch between evidence and claims, flagged by all four reviewers. The abstract is read by clinical and policy audiences, yet it is imbalanced (no CI, no event counts, no limitations), propagating an over-confident message far beyond what the analysis supports (STROBE-1b balanced summary; STROBE-20/21 cautious, proportionate interpretation).

**Action.** Convert every causal/prescriptive statement to associational language (e.g., "higher baseline activity *was associated with* lower hazard of incident depression"); delete "reduces," "directly lowers," "should be prescribed," "first-line prevention," and "population-level activity promotion." State explicitly that causal and clinical-recommendation conclusions require randomized evidence. Make the abstract balanced: report the adjusted HR with 95% CI, the outcome definition and number of events, frame the finding as an association, and add a limitations clause (residual confounding, possible reverse causation). Reserve any preventive implication for adequately powered trials.

**Problem.** The Methods describe only "Cox proportional-hazards models with activity tertile as the exposure." No confounders are named, no adjustment strategy is described, and the Results report a single HR (0.72) with no indication of whether it is adjusted — so the headline estimate appears *crude/unadjusted*, yet the Discussion interprets it as a *direct* effect.

**Why it matters.** In community-dwelling adults 65+, leisure-time activity is densely confounded by baseline physical health and function/disability, chronic disease burden and medications, cognition, BMI, smoking, pain, socioeconomic position, social engagement, and baseline subthreshold depressive symptoms — all independently linked to incident depression and often consequences of incipient frailty/illness. A crude HR cannot distinguish an effect of activity from confounding by underlying illness, so the central result is uninterpretable and cannot support any of the paper's conclusions. STROBE-7 requires confounders to be defined, STROBE-12 the methods used to control confounding, and STROBE-16 *both* unadjusted and confounder-adjusted estimates.

**Action.** Pre-specify a clinically/DAG-justified confounder set (e.g., age, sex, education/SES, comorbidity index, functional/disability status, cognition, BMI, smoking, social engagement, baseline GDS); describe how each was measured and selected (a priori, not data-driven). Report crude and adjusted HRs with 95% CIs side by side and state which variables entered the model. If the HR is in fact already adjusted, state this and give the covariate list. Budget covariates against the available events (see study-size item).

**Problem.** The only effect estimate reported is "hazard ratio 0.72, p < 0.05" (highest vs lowest tertile), with no 95% confidence interval, only a thresholded p-value, no estimate for the middle tertile, no events/person-time per tertile, and no test for linear trend.

**Why it matters.** STROBE-16 requires estimates reported *with their precision (95% CI)*. A point estimate plus "p < 0.05" hides the magnitude of uncertainty — the CI could plausibly run from a trivial 0.95 to a large 0.55, which would change interpretation entirely — and "p < 0.05" is uninformative (0.049 and 0.001 are very different). Omitting the middle tertile and a trend test hides the dose-response gradient expected of a graded biological effect; with only 96 events split across three tertiles the estimate may be imprecise, yet a strong recommendation is built on it.

**Action.** Report HRs with 95% CIs and exact p-values for both the middle and highest tertiles versus the referent, for crude and adjusted models; provide a table of incident cases, person-years, and incidence rates per tertile; and present a formal test for linear trend across the ordinal exposure.

**Problem.** Reverse causation is not addressed. Low leisure-time activity is a well-recognized *prodromal* feature of depression; excluding participants with baseline GDS ≥ 5 removes screen-positive cases but not subthreshold/prodromal symptoms (e.g., GDS 3–4) that already depress activity months to years before the threshold is crossed. No lag or sensitivity analysis is reported.

**Why it matters.** If incipient depression reduces activity, the association is at least partly the outcome causing the exposure, biasing the HR away from the null and directly undermining the directional/causal claim. This is the leading alternative explanation for HR 0.72 and the first issue a methodologically literate reviewer will raise; early incident cases (first 1–2 years) are especially suspect.

**Action.** Add lag/sensitivity analyses that exclude incident events in the first 1–2 years of follow-up (and/or model time-varying activity), and consider adjusting for baseline subthreshold GDS as a continuous score rather than only a binary cutoff. Report whether the association persists, and discuss reverse causation explicitly as a limitation.

**Problem.** Two linked problems with the outcome and its timing. (1) Incident depression conflates a Geriatric Depression Scale score ≥ 5 (a screening threshold for depressive *symptoms*, and the mirror image of the GDS < 5 entry criterion) with a "new clinical diagnosis," combined into a single heterogeneous endpoint; the GDS version/cutoff, what constituted a clinical diagnosis and who adjudicated it, and how the two routes were reconciled are unspecified. (2) Ascertainment occurs at *annual* follow-up visits, so event times are interval-censored, yet a standard Cox model treats event times as known and continuous and the assigned event date is undefined.

**Why it matters.** A one-time GDS ≥ 5 reflects probable symptomatology (somatic/cognitive items overlap with physical illness and dementia in older adults), not DSM/ICD disorder; pooling a screen with clinical diagnoses mixes constructs and can inflate or misclassify events, biasing the HR if misclassification is differential by activity/health status. Interval censoring mis-specifies the likelihood and can bias hazard ratios and standard errors. Both affect the 96-case count and the estimate. STROBE-7 (clear outcome definition), STROBE-12 (analysis methods).

**Action.** Define the primary outcome precisely (GDS version/cutoff; criteria and adjudicator for clinical diagnosis); report screen-defined and clinically diagnosed outcomes separately as well as combined, and add a sensitivity analysis using a stricter/confirmed outcome. State how event dates were assigned and use methods appropriate for interval-censored data (interval-censored survival models or discrete-time/pooled logistic models with yearly intervals).

**Problem.** Over a mean 4.2 years in adults aged 65+, death is a substantial *competing risk* for incident depression, yet the analysis is a standard Cox model that simply censors at death. The number of deaths is not reported.

**Why it matters.** Standard Cox censoring assumes non-informative censoring and estimates the cause-specific hazard. Because physically inactive, less-healthy older adults die at higher rates (and cannot then develop observable depression), censoring at death is informative and the cumulative-incidence interpretation underlying the "activity prevents depression" message is biased; differential mortality can manufacture an apparent protective association. This is a subtle but consequential pitfall specific to oldest-old cohorts.

**Action.** Report deaths and losses to follow-up by activity group; present a competing-risks analysis (Fine–Gray subdistribution hazard and/or cause-specific hazards with cumulative incidence functions) and reconcile it with the Cox estimate, or justify the cause-specific hazard framing and interpret it accordingly.

items.section_ref: Abstract vs Results (participant flow) & Methods; STROBE-13, -12 (missing data)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** The Abstract reports 512 community-dwelling adults, but the Results analyze 498 "included in the analysis," and the 14-participant discrepancy is never explained. There is no accounting of deaths, loss to follow-up, or missing-data handling over the 4.2-year follow-up, and no participant-flow diagram. Complete-case analysis is implied but never stated.

**Why it matters.** This is an internal Abstract–Results inconsistency and an unexplained loss that can signal selection bias. Differential attrition is a major bias source in older cohorts: participants who become frail, cognitively impaired, or depressed drop out more and are also less active, so informative dropout can manufacture or exaggerate a protective association. Self-reported activity, GDS, and covariates all generate missingness; if missingness or dropout relates to health/activity, complete-case estimates are biased. STROBE-13 requires numbers at each stage and a flow diagram; STROBE-12 requires how missing data were addressed.

**Action.** Provide a participant-flow diagram and text reconciling 512 → 498 (eligible → enrolled → excluded with reasons → analyzed), with deaths, losses to follow-up, total person-time, and how the 96 incident cases and censored participants were handled. Quantify missingness per variable, state the missing-data method and its assumptions, and add a sensitivity analysis (e.g., multiple imputation) assessing the impact of attrition.

**Problem.** Leisure-time activity was self-reported once at baseline using an unnamed, uncited "validated questionnaire," with no metric/units and no tertile cut-points reported, then collapsed into tertiles; activity is not updated over the 4.2-year follow-up, and only the highest-vs-lowest contrast is modeled.

**Why it matters.** A single baseline self-report is subject to recall and social-desirability bias and to regression dilution; activity often changes during follow-up (sometimes because of incipient illness — linking to the reverse-causation concern), so baseline-only status misclassifies long-term exposure. Categorizing a continuous exposure into tertiles discards dose information and induces residual confounding within categories. Without the instrument named/cited and the tertile boundaries reported in interpretable units (e.g., MET-min/week), the exposure is neither reproducible nor comparable to other cohorts and guideline thresholds. STROBE-8 (data sources/measurement), STROBE-11 (handling of quantitative variables).

**Action.** Name and cite the questionnaire and its validation population; report the activity metric/units and the numeric tertile cut-points; if repeat measures exist, use time-updated or continuous activity (e.g., splines) rather than baseline tertiles; and acknowledge single-measurement exposure misclassification as a limitation.

**Problem.** The Discussion contains no limitations paragraph whatsoever.

**Why it matters.** STROBE-19 requires a discussion of limitations and the direction and magnitude of potential bias. Omitting limitations while asserting causal, prescriptive conclusions makes the Discussion one-sided and inflates apparent certainty, compounding the causal overreach noted elsewhere.

**Action.** Add a dedicated limitations paragraph covering, at minimum: residual/unmeasured confounding, reverse causation, single baseline self-reported exposure, outcome misclassification from a screening cutoff, competing risk of death, loss to follow-up/attrition, absence of a confidence interval, and limited generalizability from two primary-care registries — naming the likely direction of each bias and tempering the conclusions accordingly.

**Problem.** There is no sample-size or power justification (STROBE-10, "explain how the study size was arrived at"), and only 96 incident events occurred.

**Why it matters.** With 96 events, the events-per-variable budget for a confounder-adjusted Cox model is tight (roughly <10 covariates at the conventional EPV≈10), which constrains how much confounding can actually be controlled and risks overfitting/unstable estimates — directly limiting the confounder adjustment recommended above. Without a power statement, readers cannot judge whether a null in subgroups reflects no effect or insufficient power.

**Action.** State how the sample size/cohort was determined (a priori power/precision calculation or available cohort), report the number of events, and justify the number of covariates in the adjusted model against the event count (EPV); acknowledge any power limitation.

**Problem.** A Cox proportional-hazards model — central to the validity of the reported HR — is used, but the proportional-hazards assumption is never mentioned or tested.

**Why it matters.** Over 4+ years in older adults a constant hazard ratio for activity is not guaranteed; as activity-related risk profiles diverge, PH may be violated, in which case a single HR is a hard-to-interpret time-average that can mask early/late differences. Checking modeling assumptions falls under STROBE-12.

**Action.** Assess PH (e.g., scaled Schoenfeld residuals/global test or log–log plots) and report the result; if violated, present time-stratified estimates or include a time interaction.

**Problem.** The claim that "the association persisted across age strata" is stated with no stratum-specific estimates, no event counts, no interaction test, and no indication that the subgroup analysis was pre-specified.

**Why it matters.** Unreported, post-hoc subgroup analyses invite multiplicity and selective reporting; a qualitative "persisted" cannot be evaluated without numbers or a formal interaction test, and underpowered strata can spuriously appear consistent. Effect modification by age/frailty is plausible and clinically relevant in the oldest old. STROBE-17.

**Action.** Report each stratum's HR with 95% CI and event counts plus a formal interaction (effect-modification) p-value, state whether the analysis was pre-specified, and account for the number of subgroup comparisons — or state that the study was underpowered for subgroup analysis and remove the claim.

**Problem.** Participants were recruited from two primary-care registries and required to be "able to complete questionnaires" (excluding cognitively impaired or frail individuals), yet the Discussion calls for population-level activity promotion.

**Why it matters.** A primary-care, questionnaire-capable sample may not represent the general older population, so a population-level policy claim overstates external validity. STROBE-6 requires the source population and selection to be described, and STROBE-21 requires generalizability to be discussed.

**Action.** Describe the source population and the effect of the questionnaire-ability criterion on the analytic sample, and temper generalizability claims to the population actually sampled rather than the general older population (this connects to removing the population-policy language flagged in the interpretation item).

**Problem.** The Introduction states a general aim ("We examined the association...") but no pre-specified, directional hypothesis or specific objectives. STROBE-3 is only partially met.

**Why it matters.** Pre-specified hypotheses distinguish confirmatory from exploratory analysis and guard against selective reporting and post-hoc interpretation.

**Action.** State a specific, directional hypothesis and the pre-specified primary analysis (exposure contrast, outcome definition, and confounder set).

**Problem.** STROBE-22 (funding source and role of funders) is not reported; the Declarations state only "no competing interests."

**Why it matters.** Funding source and funder role are required reporting elements and bear on potential bias — particularly for a result with policy implications. Their absence is a straightforward reporting gap that reviewers and editors will flag.

**Action.** Add a funding statement naming the source(s) and the funder's role in design, analysis, interpretation, and the decision to publish — or state explicitly that the study received no specific funding.

**Problem.** The Cox proportional-hazards model is fit "with activity tertile as the exposure" and names no covariates; Results report a single hazard ratio (0.72) with no adjustment set. Strong common causes of both low activity and incident depression in older adults are unaccounted for: age, sex, education/socioeconomic status, baseline physical function/disability, chronic-disease/comorbidity burden, cognitive status, BMI, smoking, social engagement/isolation, and baseline subthreshold depressive symptoms.

**Why it matters.** Physically active older adults differ systematically from inactive ones (healthier, more functional, more socially connected). An unadjusted HR almost certainly overstates any independent effect of activity (confounding by health status), yet the manuscript builds a causal, prescription-level conclusion on it. This is the central threat to validity.

**Suggested action.** Pre-specify a confounder set (justified by a DAG or prior literature) and report both crude and fully adjusted HRs with 95% CIs side by side. State which covariates were included, how they were measured and selected, and how missing covariate data were handled. With only ~96 events, constrain the number of covariates by an events-per-variable rule (~10) and state how this was managed. If key confounders were unmeasured or the data cannot support adjustment, say so and remove the causal framing.

_Sources: r1, r2, r3, r4._

**Problem.** The manuscript converts an observational association into causation and a clinical mandate: "Regular physical activity reduces the risk of late-life depression and should be prescribed as first-line prevention" (Abstract) and "Because physical activity directly lowers depression risk, clinicians should prescribe structured exercise" (Discussion).

**Why it matters.** A single-exposure, apparently unadjusted prospective cohort cannot establish that activity *directly lowers* risk and cannot license a "first-line prevention" recommendation; confounding and reverse causation are at least as plausible. For a clinical journal an overstated causal prescription is potentially harmful, would draw an immediate reviewer objection, and could misdirect prescribing.

**Suggested action.** Reframe every conclusion as associational (e.g., "higher baseline activity was associated with lower depression risk").
