Remove causal verbs ("reduces," "directly lowers," "prevent") and the "first-line prevention"/"clinicians should prescribe" claims, or condition any clinical implication explicitly on confirmatory randomized-trial evidence. State plainly that the observational design precludes causal inference, and make the Abstract conclusion match the strength of the data.

_Sources: r1, r2, r3, r4._

**Problem.** Reverse causation is not addressed. Eligibility required only GDS < 5, which does not exclude prodromal or subthreshold depression; early or declining mood can itself reduce leisure-time activity. Exposure was measured once at baseline, near the time when undetected early depression could already be depressing activity.

**Why it matters.** Reverse causation and lead-time bias are among the most common explanations for an apparent activity benefit in late-life depression cohorts. The protective HR may reflect prodromal disease rather than protection.

**Suggested action.** Add lag/landmark sensitivity analyses excluding incident events in the first 1–2 years of follow-up, adjust for baseline GDS as a continuous covariate, and consider time-updated activity if available. Report whether the association persists, and discuss reverse causation explicitly as a limitation.

_Sources: r1, r2, r3, r4._

**Problem.** Incident depression is defined as "a Geriatric Depression Scale score of 5 or higher, OR a new clinical diagnosis." The GDS is a screening instrument, not a diagnostic standard; the same threshold defines both baseline exclusion (GDS<5) and the outcome (GDS≥5), so a single crossing of a screening threshold counts as a new disease event, and the composite mixes screen-positive status with clinician diagnosis (constructs of differing validity). The GDS version (e.g., GDS-15 vs GDS-30) is not stated.

**Why it matters.** A low screening threshold inflates and misclassifies events (transient symptom fluctuation counted as incident depression), and the shared cut-off invites regression-to-threshold artifacts. Differential detection by activity level (more-active people may have more clinical contact) could manufacture or mask an association. Heterogeneous outcome definitions also reduce comparability with other studies.

**Suggested action.** State the GDS version and justify the cut-off (cite diagnostic performance in this population). Report how many events came from screening versus clinical diagnosis, run sensitivity analyses restricted to clinically confirmed cases and at higher/diagnostic thresholds, and acknowledge outcome misclassification.

_Sources: r1, r2, r3, r4._

**Problem.** Effect estimates are reported only as "hazard ratio 0.72, p < 0.05." There is no 95% confidence interval, no exact p-value, no estimate for the middle tertile, no test for linear trend/dose-response across tertiles, and no event counts or person-years per tertile. Only the highest-versus-lowest contrast is given.

**Why it matters.** A point estimate paired with a thresholded p-value hides precision — the effect could plausibly be near-null — and "p < 0.05" alone is uninformative. A dose-response (trend) test is central to the activity–depression argument; reporting only the extreme contrast prevents assessment of a graded relationship and invites selective-reporting concerns. Confidence intervals are a STROBE reporting requirement for cohort studies.

**Suggested action.** Report the HR with 95% CI and exact p-value for each non-reference tertile, add a p-for-trend across tertiles, and report the number of events, person-years, and incidence rates per tertile. Consider modeling activity continuously (e.g., restricted cubic splines) to characterize the shape of the association. Replace "p < 0.05" with exact p-values throughout.

_Sources: r1, r2, r3, r4 (incorporates r4's separate dose-response item)._

**Problem.** There is no Limitations section. The Discussion moves directly from results to a population-level prescription with no acknowledgement of confounding, reverse causation, single self-reported exposure, outcome misclassification, attrition/competing risk, or generalizability.

**Why it matters.** For an observational study in a clinical journal an explicit, balanced limitations discussion is mandatory (STROBE); its absence is what currently licenses the overstated conclusions and is a likely rejection trigger.

**Suggested action.** Add a dedicated limitations paragraph covering residual/unmeasured confounding, reverse causation/lead-time, single baseline self-reported exposure, GDS-based outcome misclassification, interval-censored follow-up, attrition and the competing risk of death, the 14 excluded participants, and the limited two-registry setting. Calibrate the conclusions accordingly before stating any clinical implication.

_Sources: r1, r2, r3, r4._

**Problem.** Participant flow and attrition are not reported. 512 were enrolled but 498 analyzed, and the 14-participant gap is unexplained; there is no flow diagram, no description of loss to follow-up over the mean 4.2-year follow-up, no missing exposure/covariate-data handling, no statement of how censoring was handled, and no comparison of completers versus those lost. The 512-vs-498 discrepancy also reads as an internal inconsistency.

**Why it matters.** Differential attrition is a major source of bias in cohort studies, and readers cannot assess it without these numbers; participant flow and censoring handling are core STROBE items that bear directly on the validity of the HR.

**Suggested action.** Provide a STROBE participant-flow diagram (recruited → eligible → analyzed) with reasons and counts for the 14 exclusions, report the number and timing of losses to follow-up, state how censoring and missing data were handled (e.g., complete-case vs multiple imputation), and compare completers with those lost.

_Sources: r1, r2, r3, r4._

**Problem.** No baseline characteristics table (Table 1) is presented, overall or by activity tertile (age distribution, sex, comorbidity, function, baseline GDS, etc.); the numeric activity-tertile cut-points are not given; and the setting is described only as "two primary-care registries" with no country, demographics, or recruitment period.

**Why it matters.** Without Table 1 and setting detail, readers cannot judge comparability of the activity groups, the plausibility of confounding, what activity level each tertile represents, or generalizability — all central to interpreting an observational association. The Introduction invokes "the oldest old," yet no age summary is provided.

**Suggested action.** Add a Table 1 of baseline characteristics by activity tertile (with appropriate summary statistics and the numeric tertile cut-off values), and expand the setting description (location, recruitment dates, eligibility flow, participation rate).

_Sources: r1, r2, r3, r4._

**Problem.** In adults aged 65+ followed for a mean of 4.2 years, death is a substantial competing risk, but the analysis appears to use a standard Cox model that treats deaths as ordinary censoring, and the number of deaths during follow-up is not reported.

**Why it matters.** Less active participants are likely to have higher mortality and may die before depression can be ascertained; ignoring the competing risk of death can distort a cause-specific estimate and bias the HR (potentially toward an apparent protective effect), and treating deaths as non-informative censoring overestimates cumulative incidence.

**Suggested action.** Report the number of deaths during follow-up and add a competing-risks sensitivity analysis (e.g., Fine-Gray subdistribution hazards and/or cause-specific models) alongside the primary Cox model.

_Sources: r2, r4._

**Problem.** The recommendation does not match the measured exposure. The exposure was *self-reported habitual leisure-time physical activity* categorized into tertiles, but the Discussion recommends that "clinicians should prescribe structured exercise" — a different construct. The study contains no intervention and no data on structured exercise.

**Why it matters.** Generalizing from observed habitual activity to a prescribed structured-exercise program overshoots the evidence; the study cannot support an intervention recommendation it never tested.

**Suggested action.** Align the conclusion with the measured exposure (e.g., "higher habitual leisure activity was associated with lower depression risk"), and frame any exercise-prescription discussion as a question for future randomized trials rather than a supported recommendation.

_Sources: r1._

**Problem.** Declarations report only "no competing interests." There is no statement of ethics/IRB approval, no informed-consent statement, no funding source, and no data-availability statement for what is prospective human-subjects research.

**Why it matters.** Clinical journals require documented ethics approval and consent; funding and data-availability statements are now standard. Their absence is a compliance and research-integrity gap that journals will flag before review, and a desk-reject risk.

**Suggested action.** Add explicit statements of ethics committee/IRB approval (committee name and protocol/reference number), the informed-consent procedure, funding sources (or "none"), and a data-availability statement.

_Sources: r1, r2, r3._

**Problem.** Exposure was a single baseline self-report from an unnamed "validated questionnaire," with no citation, no description of the activity metric/units (e.g., MET-minutes), no numeric tertile cut-points, and no time-varying measurement over the 4.2-year follow-up. Tertiles are sample-specific.

**Why it matters.** Single self-reported activity is subject to recall and social-desirability bias and regression dilution, and a single baseline value cannot capture activity change over the years most relevant to depression onset. Without the instrument name, citation, scoring, units, and tertile boundaries the analysis is not reproducible and the "validated" claim cannot be checked; sample-specific tertiles limit cross-study comparability and discard dose-response information.

**Suggested action.** Name and cite the instrument (ideally validated in older adults), report its scoring, units, and the numeric tertile boundaries (e.g., MET-min/week), and acknowledge single-measurement misclassification. If repeat measures exist, use a time-varying exposure analysis as a sensitivity check.

_Sources: r1, r2, r3, r4 (raised as minor by r1, major by r2/r3/r4)._

**Problem.** The proportional-hazards assumption is asserted by model choice but never tested. Relatedly, outcomes were ascertained at annual follow-up, making event times interval-censored rather than continuously observed as a standard Cox model assumes. The statistical software/version, handling of tied event times, and any sample-size/power justification are also unstated.

**Why it matters.** If the activity–depression hazard is non-proportional (plausible if an effect attenuates over time), a single summary HR is misleading; treating annually assessed, interval-censored events as exact times can bias estimates and standard errors. Software, tie handling, and power are standard reproducibility details.

**Suggested action.** Report a formal PH check (e.g., Schoenfeld residuals, log-log plots) and include time-interaction terms if violated. Consider a discrete-time or interval-censored survival model given annual assessment, name the software/version and tie-handling method, and add a brief power/precision consideration.

_Sources: r1, r2, r3, r4._

**Problem.** External validity is overstated: the sample came from only "two primary-care registries" (with unreported demographics), yet the conclusions invoke "population-level activity promotion" and prescribing as "first-line prevention." Participants able to complete questionnaires further select for healthier, higher-functioning older adults.

**Why it matters.** Two primary-care registries are not a population sample, and the questionnaire-completion requirement limits generalizability to the frail and oldest-old populations the Introduction motivates; population-level extrapolation is unsupported.

**Suggested action.** Describe the source population and any selection (e.g., the questionnaire-completion requirement), assess how representative the registries are, temper population-level/clinical-prescription language, and scale the conclusion back to the studied primary-care population, stating where findings may not generalize.

_Sources: r1, r4._

**Problem.** The Abstract is a single narrative paragraph rather than a structured Background/Methods/Results/Conclusions abstract, which is a submission requirement at most clinical journals.

**Why it matters.** A structured abstract is typically required, and the abstract is the most-read and most-cited element, so its format and content propagate widely.

**Suggested action.** Convert to a structured abstract, report the adjusted HR with 95% CI and the number of events (96), and temper the conclusion to an associational statement that flags the need for trial confirmation. (The overstated conclusion itself is addressed under the causal-overclaiming item above.)

_Sources: r2._

**Problem.** The Introduction frames the gap as "prospective evidence in the oldest old is limited," but the cohort enrolls adults aged 65+ (not specifically the oldest old, typically 80+), and no explicit objective or hypothesis is stated.

**Why it matters.** A scope mismatch between the stated gap and the sample weakens the rationale, and STROBE asks for pre-specified objectives.

**Suggested action.** State a specific objective/hypothesis and align the rationale with the 65+ population actually studied, or restrict/justify an "oldest old" focus if that is intended.

_Sources: r3._

**Problem.** The claim that "the association persisted across age strata" is made with no stratum-specific estimates, no strata definitions, and no interaction test.

**Why it matters.** An unsupported subgroup assertion cannot be evaluated and may overstate the robustness of the finding.

**Suggested action.** Report stratum-specific HRs with 95% CIs (defining the age strata) or a formal interaction p-value, or remove the statement.

_Sources: r1, r3._

**Problem.** From a single observational cohort the manuscript states causal and clinical-directive conclusions: the Abstract says "Regular physical activity reduces the risk of late-life depression and should be prescribed as first-line prevention," and the Discussion says "Because physical activity directly lowers depression risk, clinicians should prescribe structured exercise."

**Why it matters.** A prospective cohort with a self-reported baseline exposure and a crude hazard ratio cannot establish that activity *directly lowers* depression risk, and certainly cannot justify a first-line preventive prescription. Residual confounding, reverse causation, and selection effects are all unaddressed. STROBE-1b requires a balanced abstract and STROBE-20 a cautious interpretation considering objectives, limitations, and other evidence; the current framing overstates the evidence and could misdirect clinical practice at a clinical journal.

**Suggested action.** Replace causal/prescriptive wording with associational language throughout (e.g., "higher baseline activity was associated with lower incident depression"). Delete the "first-line prevention" / "should be prescribed" / "prescribe structured exercise" recommendations, or explicitly downgrade them to hypothesis-generating statements that require randomized-trial confirmation, and add caveats that residual confounding and reverse causation cannot be excluded.

*(Raised by all four reviewers as critical.)*

**Problem.** The only model described is "Cox proportional-hazards models with activity tertile as the exposure," and the reported HR of 0.72 appears to be a crude, unadjusted estimate. No confounders are defined anywhere and no confounder-adjusted estimate is reported.

**Why it matters.** Leisure-time activity in the oldest old is strongly patterned by age, sex, education/socioeconomic status, baseline physical function/disability, chronic-disease burden, cognitive status, BMI, smoking/alcohol, social engagement, and baseline subthreshold mood — each independently associated with depression. An unadjusted HR cannot separate activity from confounding by underlying health and likely overstates the association. STROBE-7 requires confounders to be defined, STROBE-12 requires describing methods used to control confounding, and STROBE-16 requires reporting *both* unadjusted and confounder-adjusted estimates.

**Suggested action.** Pre-specify a confounder set (ideally justified with a DAG), fit adjusted models, and report crude and adjusted HRs with 95% CIs side by side for every activity tertile, naming every covariate and how it was measured and selected. State how the proportional-hazards assumption was assessed. If adjustment was in fact performed, add every covariate explicitly to Methods and Results.

*(Raised by all four reviewers as critical.)*

**Problem.** Exposure is measured only once at baseline while incident depression is captured from the first annual follow-up onward, and baseline eligibility excluded only screen-positive depression (GDS ≥ 5). Subthreshold or prodromal depressive symptoms below that cut-point can themselves reduce physical activity, so low baseline activity may be a consequence/marker of incipient depression rather than a cause.

**Why it matters.** Reverse causation would inflate the apparent protective association (biasing the HR away from the null) and directly undermines the temporal interpretation on which the causal and prescriptive conclusions rest. STROBE-9 requires addressing this source of bias.

**Suggested action.** Add a lag/landmark sensitivity analysis that excludes events in the first 1–2 years of follow-up; adjust for or stratify on the continuous baseline (subthreshold) GDS score; run a sensitivity analysis excluding participants with baseline subthreshold symptoms; and discuss reverse causation explicitly in the limitations.

*(Raised by all four reviewers.)*

**Problem.** Incident depression is defined as "a Geriatric Depression Scale score of 5 or higher, OR a new clinical diagnosis," assessed annually. GDS ≥ 5 is a screening threshold, not a diagnosis, so the composite (96 events) mixes screen-positive cases with clinician-diagnosed cases of differing severity, validity, and ascertainment; annual assessment also makes events interval-censored.

**Why it matters.** Using a screening cut-off as a disease endpoint causes outcome misclassification and likely inflates event counts, and combining two non-equivalent ascertainment routes complicates interpretation (STROBE-7 define outcomes clearly; STROBE-8 comparable measurement). Treating interval-censored, annually-detected events as exact failure times in a standard Cox model can bias timing and estimates.

**Suggested action.** Justify and cite the GDS ≥ 5 cut-point (sensitivity/specificity in this population) and report how many of the 96 events arose from screening versus clinical diagnosis. Run sensitivity analyses using each component alone (or requiring sustained rather than single-visit GDS elevation), account for interval censoring (e.g., discrete-time or interval-censored survival models) or justify the standard Cox approach, and state whether outcome assessors were blinded to baseline activity.

*(Raised by all four reviewers.)*

**Problem.** Leisure-time activity is captured by a single baseline self-report on an unnamed "validated questionnaire," collapsed into tertiles, and not re-measured during the mean 4.2-year follow-up. The instrument is not named or cited, and its metric (e.g., MET-min/week) and tertile cut-points are not reported.

**Why it matters.** STROBE-7/8 require specifying measurement methods. An unnamed self-report invites recall and social-desirability bias and (typically non-differential) misclassification toward the null; a single baseline measure cannot capture activity change over years (bias direction then unpredictable, especially with reverse causation); tertiles discard dose information; and without the instrument reference and cut-points the exposure contrast is not reproducible and its clinical meaning cannot be judged.

**Suggested action.** Name and cite the questionnaire and report its validation in older adults; report the unit and the tertile boundaries with the underlying distribution; acknowledge single-timepoint self-reported exposure as a measurement-error limitation; and incorporate repeated/time-varying exposure measures if available.

*(Raised by all four reviewers; r3 rated the naming/cut-point gap minor, r1/r4 rated the measurement issue major.)*

**Problem.** In community-dwelling adults aged 65+ followed a mean of 4.2 years, death is a substantial competing event, but the analysis uses a standard Cox model that treats deaths as ordinary censoring and never mentions competing risks or the number of deaths.

**Why it matters.** Mortality is itself strongly associated with low physical activity, so censoring deaths can bias the cause-specific association (potentially inflating an apparent protective effect) and distort the cumulative-incidence interpretation; the number of deaths is also needed to interpret the 96 events. STROBE-12 expects the analytic approach to fit the data structure.

**Suggested action.** Report the number of deaths during follow-up, state explicitly how they were handled, and add a competing-risks analysis (e.g., Fine–Gray subdistribution hazard or cause-specific hazards with cumulative incidence), or justify why competing risk is negligible here.

*(Raised by r1 and r3 as dedicated items; r2 noted it within the participant-flow item.)*

**Problem.** 512 participants were enrolled but only 498 were analyzed, with no explanation for the 14 excluded. Loss to follow-up over the mean 4.2 years, the numbers completing each annual assessment, and the handling of missing exposure/covariate/outcome data are not reported, and no flow diagram is provided.

**Why it matters.** STROBE-13 requires participant numbers at each stage with reasons for non-participation (a flow diagram is encouraged), and STROBE-12c requires describing how missing data were handled. Over 4+ years in an elderly cohort, attrition and missingness can be substantial and differential — e.g., frailer or less-active participants dropping out or dying before an event is recorded — biasing the hazard ratio invisibly to the reader.

**Suggested action.** Add a participant flow diagram (enrolled → excluded → analyzed → completed / lost / died), give reasons for the 14 exclusions, report follow-up completeness and losses (ideally by exposure tertile), state how censoring at last contact was handled, and describe the missing-data approach (complete-case vs. imputation) with the amount of missingness.

*(Raised by all four reviewers.)*

**Problem.** The Discussion contains no limitations paragraph and acknowledges no source of bias; it is uniformly positive and moves directly from results to a population-level recommendation.

**Why it matters.** STROBE-19 requires discussion of limitations, including sources of potential bias and their likely direction and magnitude. Omitting them leaves the manuscript's most serious threats — residual/unmeasured confounding, reverse causation, single self-reported baseline exposure, GDS-based outcome misclassification, loss to follow-up, the competing risk of death, and limited generalizability — entirely unaddressed, and reinforces the overstated causal conclusion.

**Suggested action.** Add a dedicated limitations paragraph naming each major bias and stating its likely direction relative to the observed protective association, and explicitly tempering the conclusions. Address generalizability given recruitment from "two primary-care registries," which may not represent the general community-dwelling oldest-old.

*(Raised by all four reviewers as major.)*

**Problem.** Effect estimates are reported only as "hazard ratio 0.72, p < 0.05," with no 95% confidence interval anywhere in the Abstract or Results and no exact p-value.

**Why it matters.** STROBE-16 requires estimates with a measure of precision. "p < 0.05" conceals the magnitude of uncertainty — an HR of 0.72 with a CI of 0.55–0.95 supports a different conclusion than 0.52–0.99 — so readers cannot weigh the strength of evidence as written.

**Suggested action.** Report the 95% CI and an exact p-value for every hazard ratio (crude and adjusted, each tertile contrast, and any stratum-specific estimate) in both the Abstract and Results.

*(Raised by all four reviewers; r1 and r3 bundled it with the dose-response and age-strata gaps below.)*

**Problem.** The claim that "the association persisted across age strata" is asserted with no stratum-specific estimates, no sample sizes, and no interaction/heterogeneity test.

**Why it matters.** STROBE-17 requires reporting which analyses (including subgroups) were performed and their actual results. A subgroup claim without data is unverifiable, is likely underpowered when 96 events are split across strata, and may imply unreported multiple testing or overstate robustness.

**Suggested action.** Report the stratum-specific HRs with 95% CIs and a formal test for effect modification (interaction term), state whether the age-stratified analysis was pre-specified, and present the estimates numerically (or in a table) — or remove the claim if it cannot be substantiated.

*(Raised by r2 as major and r4 as minor as dedicated items; r1 and r3 noted it within the effect-reporting item.)*

**Problem.** Continuous activity is reduced to tertiles, but only the highest-versus-lowest contrast (HR 0.72) is reported; the middle-tertile estimate and any test for linear trend/dose-response are absent.

**Why it matters.** STROBE-11 concerns the handling of quantitative variables and STROBE-16 the reporting of category boundaries and trend. Omitting the middle category and a trend test prevents assessment of a dose-response gradient — one of the few features that would strengthen an observational causal argument — and selectively reporting only the extreme contrast can overstate the effect.

**Suggested action.** Report HRs with 95% CIs for all tertiles versus the reference (with cut-points), add a test for trend, and consider modeling activity continuously or with splines to characterize the dose-response shape.

*(Raised by r2 and r4 as minor dedicated items; r1 and r3 folded it into the effect-reporting item.)*

**Problem.** There is no justification for the study size; 512 enrolled / 498 analyzed with 96 events is presented without any power or precision rationale.

**Why it matters.** STROBE-10 requires explaining how the study size was arrived at. The point is also analytically consequential: with only 96 events, events-per-variable constraints sharply limit how many confounders an adjusted Cox model can reliably support — directly bearing on the adjustment recommended above and on the age-strata analyses.

**Suggested action.** State whether this was a fixed/convenience/registry cohort or an a-priori-powered design, and report the events-per-variable for the planned adjusted model (target roughly ≥10), commenting on its constraint on the multivariable and subgroup analyses.

*(Raised by all four reviewers as minor.)*

**Problem.** The Introduction states a general aim ("We examined the association between leisure-time physical activity and incident depression") but specifies no pre-specified hypothesis or expected direction and does not distinguish primary from secondary analyses.

**Why it matters.** STROBE-3 calls for stating specific objectives including any pre-specified hypotheses. Without a stated hypothesis and a pre-specified primary contrast, the highest-vs-lowest-tertile emphasis and the age-strata analysis cannot be distinguished from selective or exploratory reporting, which affects how the results should be weighted.

**Suggested action.** State the specific objective and a pre-specified directional primary hypothesis (including the primary exposure contrast), label the age-stratified and other analyses as pre-specified or post hoc, and indicate whether an analysis plan or protocol exists.

*(Raised by r1, r3, and r4 as minor; not raised by r2.)*

**Problem.** The Declarations report only competing interests (none); there is no funding statement or description of any funder's role.

**Why it matters.** STROBE-22 requires reporting the funding source and the funders' role for the present study (and any original study on which the data are based) — a routine but mandatory reporting gap at most clinical journals. It is also relevant given recruitment from two primary-care registries, whose access/governance may involve sponsors.

**Suggested action.** Add a funding statement naming the source(s) and describing the funder's role in design, analysis, and reporting (including any sponsorship of the registries used), or explicitly state that the study received no funding.

*(Raised by all four reviewers as minor.)*

**Problem.** The Results report no quantitative data. There is no baseline characteristics table, no mean HbA1c values or between-group difference, no effect estimate with 95% CI, no p-value, and no measures of dispersion. The only statement is qualitative: "Mean HbA1c reduction was greater in the chatbot arm." Secondary outcomes ("also favoured the intervention") are likewise reported without numbers.

**Why it matters.** For a randomized trial, the magnitude and uncertainty of the treatment effect are the core deliverable. Without an effect size, readers cannot judge whether the difference is statistically credible or clinically meaningful, and the efficacy claim in the Abstract/Discussion is unverifiable.

**Suggested action.** Report (1) a baseline characteristics table by arm, (2) the primary outcome as an adjusted between-group difference in HbA1c change with 95% CI and p-value from the linear mixed model, with per-arm means/SDs, and (3) secondary outcomes with the same statistics. Explicitly state whether the HbA1c difference meets a pre-specified clinically meaningful threshold (e.g., ~0.3-0.5% absolute).

**Problem.** No prospective trial registration is reported (no registry name, registration ID, or date). ICMJE and JMIR require prospective registration for randomized trials.

**Why it matters.** Unregistered RCTs are generally not publishable as trials and raise concerns about outcome-switching; without a registered protocol, reviewers cannot confirm that the reported primary outcome (HbA1c at 8 weeks) and secondary outcomes were pre-specified.

**Suggested action.** Add the registry, registration number, and date in the Methods and Declarations. Confirm that the reported primary and secondary outcomes match the registered/protocol outcomes, and disclose any deviations.

**Problem.** The conclusions overstate the evidence. The Abstract states "LLM-assisted counseling is effective for glycaemic control," and the Discussion asserts the findings "show that LLM counseling is effective and should be adopted in routine diabetes care." These are definitive efficacy and practice-change claims drawn from a single-centre, unblinded, 8-week trial with no reported effect size.

**Why it matters.** Causal/definitive language unsupported by the design and (currently) by any reported statistics will mislead clinicians and is a predictable reviewer rejection point. The recommendation for routine adoption is not warranted by one small, short, single-site study.

**Suggested action.** Reframe conclusions as preliminary/exploratory, tie them to the actual measured effect and its uncertainty, explicitly acknowledge the single-centre/unblinded/short-follow-up limits in the conclusion, and remove the "should be adopted in routine diabetes care" recommendation (replace with a call for larger, longer, multi-centre confirmation).

**Problem.** There is no a priori sample size or power calculation. It is unclear whether the 220 randomized participants were chosen to detect a pre-specified HbA1c difference.

**Why it matters.** Sample-size justification (CONSORT item 7a) is required for interpretability; without it, readers cannot tell whether a non-significant secondary result reflects no effect or underpowering, or whether the trial was sized post hoc.

**Suggested action.** State the pre-specified target effect size, alpha, desired power, assumed SD of HbA1c change, and anticipated dropout, and show how these yield the target N.

**Problem.** The intervention is not described in reproducible detail. The manuscript does not specify the LLM (model and version), the prompting/guardrail design, how counseling content was sourced and validated for clinical accuracy, the intended usage dose/frequency, what (if any) human clinician oversight existed, or actual engagement/usage data. The control ("standard printed diabetes education materials") is also undescribed.

**Why it matters.** Readers cannot reproduce or critically appraise what was actually tested, and digital-health reporting expectations (CONSORT-EHEALTH) require this level of detail for an LLM-based intervention.

**Suggested action.** Add a structured intervention description following CONSORT-EHEALTH: model/version, prompt and safety/guardrail design, content validation process, intended and observed usage/engagement metrics, and degree of human oversight. Describe the comparator content equivalently.

**Problem.** The primary outcome is HbA1c change assessed at 8 weeks. HbA1c reflects average glycaemia over roughly the preceding 2-3 months (red-cell lifespan ~120 days), so an 8-week endpoint may not fully capture treatment-induced change and can underestimate or distort the effect.

**Why it matters.** The validity and timing of the primary outcome are central to the efficacy claim; reviewers will question whether 8 weeks is an adequate window for HbA1c.

**Suggested action.** Justify the 8-week timepoint explicitly, ensure baseline-to-8-week change is interpreted in light of HbA1c kinetics, and ideally add or contextualize a >=12-week measurement. At minimum, flag the short window as a limitation that directly affects interpretation of the primary outcome.

**Problem.** No safety or harms data are reported. There is no adverse-event/serious-adverse-event monitoring, no hypoglycaemia or medication-change safety reporting, and no audit of chatbot outputs for inaccurate or unsafe medical advice (a known LLM hallucination risk).

**Why it matters.** An LLM delivering counseling to patients on glucose-lowering therapy poses direct clinical-safety risks; omitting harms reporting is a major gap for a clinical trial and for a digital-health intervention.

**Suggested action.** Report harms per CONSORT-Harms: AE/SAE counts by arm, hypoglycaemia events, and any unsafe-advice incidents. Describe the prospective process used to review/validate chatbot messages for clinical safety and how unsafe outputs were prevented or handled.

**Problem.** The comparison is confounded by intervention type rather than content. The intervention arm received an interactive chatbot while the control arm received static printed materials, so any benefit may reflect greater attention, engagement, and contact time rather than LLM counseling specifically.

**Why it matters.** Without an attention/engagement-matched comparator, the trial cannot isolate the contribution of the LLM, which directly weakens the central efficacy claim.

**Suggested action.** Acknowledge this attention-control confound explicitly, temper the causal attribution to "LLM counseling," justify the choice of comparator, and note an attention-matched (e.g., non-LLM interactive) control as needed in future work.

**Problem.** Participant flow and attrition handling are incomplete. The text reports 220 randomized and 198 completed (~10% loss) but gives no per-arm flow, no reasons for dropout, and no CONSORT flow diagram. "Intention-to-treat" is asserted, but the analysis population and the missing-data assumptions of the linear mixed model are not specified.

**Why it matters.** Differential or unexplained attrition can bias the estimate; CONSORT requires a flow diagram and clear definition of the analyzed population.

**Suggested action.** Add a CONSORT flow diagram with per-arm numbers randomized, analyzed, and lost (with reasons). Define the ITT population precisely and state the missing-data assumption underlying the mixed model (e.g., MAR), ideally with a sensitivity analysis.

**Problem.** The favourable secondary outcomes (medication adherence and treatment satisfaction) are likely self-reported, and participants were unblinded, so these results are susceptible to response/expectation bias. The measurement instruments are not named, and no adjustment for multiple comparisons is mentioned.

**Why it matters.** Subjective outcomes under unblinding are prone to inflated apparent benefit, and untreated multiplicity across primary and multiple secondary outcomes increases the false-positive risk.

**Suggested action.** Name the validated instruments used (and report objective adherence measures if available), discuss the specific impact of unblinding on self-reported outcomes, and state how multiple comparisons were handled (or pre-specify these as exploratory).

**Problem.** The data-availability statement ("available from the corresponding author on reasonable request") is weak relative to JMIR open-data norms, and the funding statement does not describe the funder's role; there is no mention of sharing the chatbot/prompt artifacts or analysis code.

**Why it matters.** Transparency and reproducibility expectations, particularly for digital-health trials, favour deposited data and disclosed funder involvement.

**Suggested action.** Where possible, deposit de-identified data (and analysis code / chatbot configuration) in a repository with a DOI, or justify any access restriction. State the funder's role (or lack thereof) in design, conduct, analysis, and reporting.

_Consensus: raised by all 4 reviewers (r1, r2, r3, r4)._

**Problem:** The Results report only the *direction* of effects ("Mean HbA1c reduction was greater in the chatbot arm," "HbA1c improved," adherence and satisfaction "favoured the intervention"). No point estimates, per-arm baseline and follow-up means (SD), between-arm difference, confidence intervals, p-values, or per-arm analyzed n are given for the primary outcome or any secondary, and no clinically meaningful difference threshold is defined.

**Why it matters:** For a randomized trial the magnitude and precision of the primary effect are the core deliverable. Without an effect estimate and its uncertainty the central efficacy claim is unverifiable, unfalsifiable by the reader, and compatible with a clinically negligible difference. Readers cannot judge whether the HbA1c effect reaches the commonly cited MCID (~0.3–0.5%) or is statistically supported (CONSORT item 17; JMIR reporting requirements).

**Suggested action:** Report, for the primary and each secondary outcome: per-arm baseline and 8-week values (mean ± SD or median/IQR), the model-based adjusted between-arm difference with 95% CI and p-value, and the number analyzed per arm. Name the units/scales for adherence and satisfaction. Pre-specify a clinically meaningful HbA1c difference and interpret the observed effect against it. Lead the abstract Results with the numeric primary estimate and its CI rather than a directional statement.

_Consensus: raised by all 4 reviewers (r1, r2, r3, r4). (r1 categorized this as a rewrite/language issue; r2–r4 as an evidence-calibration issue.)_

**Problem:** The conclusions are definitive, causal, and prescriptive: the Abstract states "LLM-assisted counseling is effective for glycaemic control" and the Discussion states the findings "show that LLM counseling is effective and should be adopted in routine diabetes care." These rest on a single-centre, unblinded, 8-week trial with no reported effect size, CI, or p-value, and contradict the paper's own stated limitations.

**Why it matters:** The conclusion is not commensurate with the evidence or its uncertainty. "Show that… effective" and "should be adopted in routine care" assert established efficacy and an implementation recommendation the design cannot support (single site, no active/attention-matched comparator, short follow-up, no precision reported), risking premature dissemination of an unvalidated clinical tool and misinforming practice. This is the central interpretive flaw of the paper.

**Suggested action:** Reframe as preliminary/hypothesis-generating and tie the claim explicitly to the reported effect estimate and its CI, e.g., "In this 8-week single-centre trial, LLM-assisted counseling was associated with a [X%, 95% CI] greater HbA1c reduction than printed materials, warranting confirmation." Remove the routine-adoption recommendation pending replication in a multi-centre, longer-duration, attention-controlled, registered trial with blinded outcome assessment. Keep the abstract Conclusions consistent with this wording.

_Consensus: raised by all 4 reviewers (r1, r2, r3, r4). (r1 rated this critical; r2–r4 rated/treated it as major.)_

**Problem:** No a priori sample-size or power justification is provided for the n=220 enrolled. The target/minimal clinically important HbA1c difference, assumed SD and its source, two-sided alpha, target power, allocation ratio inputs, and dropout allowance are all absent. The n appears arbitrary.

**Why it matters:** Without prespecified power, a "positive" finding cannot be distinguished from an underpowered chance result or an overpowered trivial one, and the trial's ability to detect a clinically meaningful HbA1c difference is unknown. This is foundational for interpreting any RCT and is required by CONSORT (item 7a).

**Suggested action:** Report the a priori calculation: the MCID for HbA1c (commonly ~0.3–0.5%), the SD source, two-sided alpha, target power, assumed attrition, and resulting required N, and confirm whether enrollment met it. If the calculation was not prespecified, state this explicitly and report the minimum detectable difference for the achieved sample (n≈198 analyzed).

_Consensus: raised by all 4 reviewers (r1, r2, r3, r4). (r3 categorized this as evidence; r1, r2, r4 as structural/design.)_

**Problem:** The intervention is an interactive, on-demand LLM chatbot; the control receives static printed diabetes education materials. The arms differ not only in LLM-generated content but in modality, interactivity, attention, contact time, and engagement frequency.

**Why it matters:** Combined with the unblinded design, any observed benefit is confounded by engagement/attention (a Hawthorne-type/performance-bias effect) and cannot be attributed to the LLM specifically — which is the manuscript's central claim. The confound particularly inflates subjective outcomes. This is a design-level threat to internal validity, not merely a reporting gap.

**Suggested action:** Acknowledge the unmatched comparator prominently as a key limitation and reframe the estimand as "interactive LLM chatbot vs. usual printed education" rather than an isolated LLM effect. Where possible report contact-time/engagement metrics by arm. For confirmatory work, recommend an attention/engagement-matched digital comparator (e.g., a non-LLM interactive app or scripted human/automated messaging delivering equivalent contact).

_Consensus: raised by all 4 reviewers (r1, r2, r3, r4). (r1 categorized as structural; r2, r4 as evidence; r3 within its sample-size/analysis item.)_

**Problem:** 22 of 220 randomized (10%) did not complete follow-up. An intention-to-treat analysis with a linear mixed model is asserted, but Results report only completers ("198 completed") and never state the analysis denominator/N per arm. Attrition by arm and reasons, the missing-data mechanism/assumption (e.g., MAR), how the mixed model handled missing 8-week data, and any sensitivity analysis are all absent. Reporting only the 198 completers is on its face a completer analysis and contradicts the stated ITT principle.

**Why it matters:** Differential or informative dropout biases the treatment effect; the MAR assumption underlying a direct-likelihood LMM is untestable from observed data and must be stated and stress-tested. Calling the analysis ITT while implicitly analyzing completers conflates two different estimands and undermines the bias protection ITT is meant to provide (CONSORT items 13–14).

**Suggested action:** State the analysis population explicitly (N per arm analyzed) and confirm whether all 220 randomized were included; if only completers were analyzed, relabel accordingly and justify. Provide a CONSORT participant-flow diagram with per-arm dropout counts and reasons, state and justify the missing-data assumption, and add a sensitivity analysis robust to MNAR (e.g., pattern-mixture/tipping-point or multiple imputation).

_Consensus: raised by all 4 reviewers (r1, r2, r3, r4). (r1 emphasized multiplicity, categorized structural; r2–r4 emphasized instruments/unblinding, categorized evidence.)_

**Problem:** Two related deficiencies in the secondary outcomes (medication adherence, treatment satisfaction): (a) no measurement instrument or data source is named (validated adherence scale vs. pharmacy refills/pill counts vs. self-report; which satisfaction scale), and (b) three outcomes are tested with no described multiplicity control, while the Discussion leans on the secondaries ("also favoured the intervention") as corroborating efficacy. Both outcomes are subjective/self-reported and the trial is unblinded.

**Why it matters:** Unnamed instruments make the secondaries unreproducible and uninterpretable. Subjective, self-reported outcomes in an unblinded trial — where participants know they received a novel chatbot — are highly susceptible to expectation/performance/detection bias systematically favouring the intervention, so "favours intervention" is expected and weakly informative. Testing multiple outcomes without adjustment inflates the family-wise type I error rate; secondaries are exploratory, not confirmatory.

**Suggested action:** Name and cite the validated instruments and reporting period (and add an objective adherence measure such as pharmacy refills/pill counts where feasible), report quantitative between-arm results with CIs, designate a single primary outcome with a prespecified multiplicity strategy (hierarchical testing or alpha adjustment), explicitly label secondary outcomes as exploratory, and discuss their susceptibility to bias given unblinding rather than drawing efficacy conclusions from them.

_Consensus: raised by all 4 reviewers (r1, r2, r3, r4)._

**Problem:** The primary outcome, change in HbA1c, is assessed at 8 weeks. HbA1c reflects average glycaemia over roughly the preceding 8–12 weeks (driven by the ~120-day erythrocyte lifespan) and typically requires at least 12 weeks to fully equilibrate to a change in glycaemia. No justification for the 8-week timing is given.

**Why it matters:** At 8 weeks the HbA1c response to a behavioural intervention is biologically attenuated and partial, so the measured difference may understate, overstate, or unreliably represent the true effect, biasing interpretation of magnitude. It also cannot speak to durability — the relevant clinical question for a self-management tool — and complicates comparison with the diabetes literature, where ≥12-week (commonly 24-week) HbA1c endpoints are standard.

**Suggested action:** Justify the 8-week endpoint explicitly against HbA1c kinetics, acknowledge the incomplete-equilibration limitation in Limitations, or report a ≥12-week endpoint. If only 8-week data exist, temper all efficacy language accordingly and consider a complementary faster-responding glycaemic measure (fasting/self-monitored glucose, fructosamine, or CGM-derived time-in-range) to triangulate.

_Consensus: raised by r1, r2, r4._

**Problem:** The Methods state only that participants were "randomized 1:1" with no description of random-sequence generation (e.g., computer-generated, block size), allocation concealment mechanism, stratification, or the separation of roles among those who generated, enrolled, and assigned participants.

**Why it matters:** Unconcealed or improperly generated allocation is a principal source of selection bias and can manufacture or distort between-arm differences; without these details the internal validity of the randomization cannot be assessed (CONSORT items 8–10).

**Suggested action:** Describe the sequence-generation method (including any block size and stratification factors), the concealment mechanism (e.g., central/automated allocation), and the separation of roles among those who generated the sequence, enrolled participants, and assigned them to arms.

_Consensus: raised by r1, r2, r3, r4 (r4 within its CONSORT-reporting item)._

**Problem:** Eligibility is only "adults with type 2 diabetes and a smartphone," enrolled in primary care, with no baseline HbA1c entry threshold, no medication-stability or other inclusion/exclusion criteria. There is no baseline characteristics table (age, sex, baseline HbA1c, diabetes duration, baseline therapy) and no evidence the arms were balanced after randomization.

**Why it matters:** Without a minimum baseline HbA1c, participants near target leave little room to improve (floor effect) and regression to the mean can masquerade as a treatment effect; baseline HbA1c is a strong predictor of change. Without a baseline-by-arm table, readers cannot judge whether randomization succeeded, whether residual confounding or baseline imbalance influenced the comparison, or the generalizability of the population.

**Suggested action:** State full eligibility criteria (including a baseline HbA1c range and medication-regimen stability), add a CONSORT baseline characteristics table by arm (including baseline HbA1c and glucose-lowering therapy), and confirm the primary analysis adjusts for baseline HbA1c (ANCOVA-style) to avoid regression-to-the-mean artifacts; quantify balance on key prognostic variables.

_Consensus: raised by r1, r2, r4 (r4 within its CONSORT-reporting item). (r1 rated minor and categorized evidence; r2 rated major/structural.)_

**Problem:** No prospective trial-registration identifier (e.g., ClinicalTrials.gov / WHO registry) and no reference to a published protocol or statistical analysis plan are provided, despite this being a randomized trial submitted to a CONSORT/CONSORT-EHEALTH journal (ICMJE and JMIR require prospective registration).

**Why it matters:** Prespecification (registered primary outcome, timing, and analysis plan) is the principal safeguard against outcome switching and selective reporting. Its absence prevents verification that the HbA1c primary outcome and 8-week timing were prespecified rather than selected post hoc, directly weakening the statistical claims — particularly given the reliance on favourable secondary outcomes.

**Suggested action:** Provide the registry name, trial ID, and registration date; cite the protocol/SAP; and confirm the reported primary and secondary outcomes and analysis match the prespecified ones (or transparently flag and justify any deviations). If unregistered, state this explicitly as a limitation.

_Consensus: raised by r2, r3, r4. (r3 rated this critical on patient-safety grounds; r2 and r4 rated major.)_

**Problem:** There is no safety-monitoring plan and no harms reporting, despite the intervention being an LLM that delivers diabetes counseling directly to patients on glucose-lowering therapy. No adverse events, escalation pathway, human-oversight mechanism, or guardrails governing what the model was permitted to advise are described.

**Why it matters:** Patient safety is the central risk of deploying a generative model in diabetes self-management: LLMs can produce inaccurate, hallucinated, or unsafe guidance affecting hypoglycaemia risk, medication/dosing, dietary advice, or sick-day rules. Harms surveillance for both arms is required for clinical-trial reporting (CONSORT item 19 / Harms), and its absence undercuts both the validity and the ethics of any "effective and should be adopted" claim.

**Suggested action:** Add a Harms/Safety section reporting prespecified adverse events of interest (notably severe hypoglycaemia and any harm from chatbot advice) by arm, how events were collected and adjudicated, the model's clinical guardrails (restricted topics, disclaimers, clinician escalation/hand-off), and any instances of unsafe or hallucinated outputs.

_Consensus: raised by r2, r3, r4. (r4 rated this critical; r2 and r3 rated major.)_

**Problem:** The intervention is described only as "an LLM counseling chatbot." Missing: the underlying model and version/provider, whether it was version-locked for the trial duration, who built/operated it, system prompt/role and guardrail design, whether outputs were grounded/retrieval-restricted to vetted clinical content, counseling content scope, language, intended usage dose/frequency, personalization, how engagement was tracked, and any actual usage/fidelity data.

**Why it matters:** Without these details the trial is not reproducible, the active ingredient and its dose cannot be separated from the effect, engagement (the presumed mechanism) is unmeasured, and reviewers cannot assess the risk of hallucinated or guideline-discordant advice. Model behaviour is version-dependent, so an unlocked model could change mid-trial and yield non-comparable results (TIDieR; JMIR CONSORT-EHEALTH).

**Suggested action:** Add an intervention subsection (or supplement) specifying the model/version and provider, whether it was fixed for the trial, the prompting/grounding strategy and any retrieval over approved diabetes-education content, the counseling scope, the usage protocol (recommended frequency, sessions/messages per participant) and actual engagement/fidelity metrics, and human-in-the-loop/escalation pathways. Include example interactions or the system prompt as a supplement so the intervention can be reproduced and dose-response assessed.

_Raised by r3 only._

**Problem:** Changes in concomitant glucose-lowering medication are not reported or controlled. HbA1c is strongly influenced by pharmacotherapy, and a chatbot that promotes adherence or prompts care-seeking could trigger differential medication intensification between arms.

**Why it matters:** If the chatbot arm had more medication titration or better adherence to existing therapy, the HbA1c difference may reflect pharmacological changes rather than the counseling per se, confounding interpretation of the "efficacy of counseling." This is a core internal-validity issue specific to glycaemic endpoints.

**Suggested action:** Report baseline and on-study glucose-lowering medication use and any treatment intensification by arm, discuss whether between-arm differences in medication changes could explain the HbA1c effect, and, if feasible, present an analysis adjusting for or stratifying by medication change.

_Raised by r4 only._

**Problem:** The structured abstract is non-informative: Background gives no specific objective, Methods omits N and setting, Results contains zero data ("HbA1c improved in the chatbot arm"), and Conclusions overstate ("is effective"). It does not match the data or the recommended content for a trial abstract.

**Why it matters:** The abstract is the most-read section and is screened against journal/CONSORT-for-abstracts expectations. Without the objective, design, N, primary effect estimate with CI, and a calibrated conclusion, the abstract cannot stand alone and signals the same overclaiming present in the body.

**Suggested action:** Rewrite to a CONSORT-for-abstracts structure: a specific objective; design (randomized, single-centre, unblinded), setting, and randomized N per arm; the primary HbA1c between-group estimate with 95% CI/p and key secondary results; and a conclusion limited to what an 8-week single-centre trial supports. _(Overlaps with the Results-reporting and Conclusions-overreach items above, but concerns the abstract's standalone structure.)_

_Raised by r1 only._

**Problem:** The analysis is described only as a "linear mixed model." The fixed effects (arm, time, arm×time, baseline HbA1c), random-effects structure, covariance/correlation structure, the estimand/contrast used for the primary result, and the software/version are not specified.

**Why it matters:** Different LMM specifications can yield materially different estimates and inference. Without the specification the result is not reproducible and the estimand is ambiguous (e.g., 8-week contrast vs. average over time).

**Suggested action:** Fully specify the model: outcome, fixed effects including the arm×time interaction and baseline adjustment, random-effects structure, covariance structure, the specific contrast reported as the primary effect, and the software/version.

_Raised by r2 (other reviewers note single-centre status only within the conclusions-overreach item)._

**Problem:** The trial is single-centre and requires smartphone ownership, restricting the sample to digitally enabled primary-care patients. The Limitations note single-centre/short follow-up/unblinding, but the efficacy and "should be adopted" conclusions are not tempered to match this restricted population.

**Why it matters:** A smartphone-required, single-site sample limits external validity and likely selects patients with greater digital literacy and engagement, overstating applicability to the broader type-2-diabetes population (selection bias affecting generalizability).

**Suggested action:** Align the conclusions with the restricted sample, describe the recruitment setting and the smartphone-access selection effect, and explicitly bound the generalizability claim.

_Raised by r4 only._

**Problem:** The title asserts "Efficacy of LLM-assisted counseling," which, paired with the body's "effective," claims established efficacy that the single-centre, unblinded, 8-week design does not establish.

**Why it matters:** Titles set reader expectations and are frequently cited in isolation; an efficacy claim stronger than the evidence contributes to overinterpretation.

**Suggested action:** Use a more measured, design-anchored title, e.g., "LLM-assisted counseling and glycaemic control in type 2 diabetes: a single-centre randomized trial," optionally noting the 8-week endpoint. Reserve "efficacy" for confirmatory evidence.

_Raised by r4 only. (The missing trial-registration ID is addressed in the registration/prespecification item above; the remaining declaration mechanics are collected here.)_

**Problem:** Reporting-mechanics gaps in the declarations: the IRB approval names no committee or approval number; funding is "an institutional research grant" with no grant identifier; the competing-interests statement does not address any relationship to the LLM/model provider despite the intervention being an LLM chatbot; and data availability is the weak "on reasonable request."

**Why it matters:** These are standard transparency and integrity items checked at submission. Omitting an LLM-provider conflict disclosure is particularly relevant here, and vague funding/ethics statements are commonly flagged in editorial screening.

**Suggested action:** Add the IRB name and approval number, a specific grant/award number (or state none), and an explicit statement of any financial/non-financial relationship with the model provider or chatbot developer. Strengthen data availability with a repository/DOI or a defined access process where feasible.

**Problem.** The Discussion states "these findings show that LLM counseling is effective and should be adopted in routine diabetes care," and the Abstract concludes "LLM-assisted counseling is effective for glycaemic control." These are unqualified causal-efficacy and policy-adoption claims drawn from a single-centre, unblinded, 8-week trial whose own Limitations section flags unblinding, short follow-up, and single-site conduct.

**Why it matters.** The conclusions are not supported by the evidence presented and directly contradict the stated limitations. No effect sizes, confidence intervals, or safety data are given to justify "effective," and a recommendation for routine-care adoption far exceeds what one short pilot-scale RCT can support. Overstated conclusions are a common cause of editorial rejection and mislead clinical readers.

**Suggested action.** Rewrite to a result-bounded, hypothesis-generating statement, e.g., "In this single-centre 8-week trial, an LLM counseling chatbot was associated with a greater short-term reduction in HbA1c; larger, longer, multi-site trials with blinded outcome assessment and safety monitoring are needed before clinical adoption." Remove the adoption recommendation.

**Problem.** The Results report direction only ("Mean HbA1c reduction was greater in the chatbot arm"; adherence and satisfaction "favoured the intervention") with no numbers: no per-arm means, no between-group difference, no 95% CI, no p-value, and no per-arm denominators.

**Why it matters.** CONSORT-17a requires, for each outcome, the results in each group and the estimated effect size with its precision (95% CI). Without magnitude and uncertainty, readers cannot judge clinical relevance (e.g., whether the HbA1c difference exceeds the ~0.3-0.5% generally considered clinically meaningful) or even whether the difference is statistically supported. As written, the efficacy claim is unfalsifiable.

**Suggested action.** Report the adjusted between-arm difference in HbA1c change with 95% CI and p-value from the mixed model, per-arm baseline and follow-up means (SD), per-arm analysed N, and the same for each secondary outcome. Add a primary results table.

**Problem.** The Methods state analyses "followed an intention-to-treat principle using a linear mixed model," but the Results analyse outcomes among the 198 who "completed follow-up" out of 220 randomized — i.e., a completers (per-protocol) population, not ITT.

**Why it matters.** This is an internal inconsistency that undermines the validity claim. True ITT analyses all randomized participants in their assigned arm; excluding the 22 non-completers (10%) reintroduces the attrition bias the mixed model is meant to mitigate, and the missingness may be differential by arm. Reviewers will read this as either a misuse of the term ITT or unreported exclusions.

**Suggested action.** Clarify the analysis population: if a mixed model under missing-at-random was used on all 220 randomized, state that explicitly and report N=220 as analysed; if only completers were analysed, do not call it ITT. Report attrition by arm with reasons, and add a sensitivity analysis (e.g., multiple imputation or tipping-point) for missing HbA1c.

**Problem.** No sample size determination or power calculation is reported; the figure of 220 randomized appears only in Results with no justification.

**Why it matters.** CONSORT-7a requires how the sample size was determined. Without a pre-specified target difference, assumed SD, power, and alpha, the trial could be underpowered (inflating false-negative risk on secondary outcomes) or the 220 could be a convenience sample, which weakens any efficacy inference and prevents replication.

**Suggested action.** Add a sample size section stating the minimally important HbA1c difference targeted, the assumed SD, two-sided alpha, target power, anticipated attrition, and the resulting required N, with the citation/source for the assumptions. If the study was a pilot/feasibility trial, label it as such and frame outcomes accordingly.

**Problem.** There is no reporting of adverse events or of any analysis of AI performance errors. For an LLM that delivers counseling to patients with a chronic disease, this is a safety-critical omission: LLMs can produce incorrect, fabricated, or unsafe self-management or medication advice.

**Why it matters.** CONSORT-AI-19 requires describing any analysis of performance errors and how errors were identified, or an explicit statement that none was planned/done; standard CONSORT also requires harms reporting. A diabetes counseling chatbot could plausibly give unsafe advice (e.g., on hypoglycaemia, dosing, or sick-day rules). Claiming the intervention is "effective" and should be "adopted" without any safety/error analysis is not defensible.

**Suggested action.** Report whether AI outputs were monitored/audited for errors and unsafe content, how such events were identified and adjudicated, the rate observed, and any escalation/guardrail mechanism. Report adverse events (including hypoglycaemia) by arm. If no error analysis was done, state so explicitly and treat it as a major limitation.

**Problem.** The AI intervention is described only as "an LLM counseling chatbot." None of the CONSORT-AI intervention items are addressed: the model and version (5i), how input data were acquired/selected (5ii), how poor-quality or missing input was handled (5iii), the nature and required expertise of human-AI interaction (5iv), the output produced (5v), and how outputs fed into patient decision-making (5vi). Input-data-level eligibility (4a) and how the AI was integrated into the care setting, onsite/offsite (4b), are also absent.

**Why it matters.** These items are the core of CONSORT-AI and are essential for reproducibility, risk assessment, and interpretation of who/what the chatbot actually did. Without the model identity and version, the study cannot be replicated and results cannot be attributed to a stable system (LLM behaviour changes across versions).

**Suggested action.** Add an AI-intervention subsection specifying the underlying model and exact version/date, system prompt/guardrail strategy, what patient inputs it received and how, handling of ambiguous/low-quality input, the form of its outputs (free-text advice, structured tips, etc.), how outputs were surfaced to patients and whether a clinician reviewed them, and how the chatbot was delivered/integrated (app, frequency, on/offsite).

**Problem.** Neither arm is described in enough detail to replicate. For the chatbot arm there is no dose/exposure (sessions, frequency, duration, scripted vs free conversation, topics, who initiated contact). The comparator is only "standard printed diabetes education materials," with no content, source, or contact schedule.

**Why it matters.** CONSORT-5 requires sufficient intervention detail to allow replication. The exposure 'dose' is also needed to interpret the effect and to separate the LLM's contribution from contact time.

**Suggested action.** Specify the chatbot regimen (intended frequency/duration of use over the 8 weeks, prompting/reminders, topics covered) and actual engagement metrics, and fully describe the printed-materials comparator (content, source guideline, when given, any follow-up contact).

**Problem.** The comparator is a passive printed booklet while the intervention is an interactive, on-demand conversational agent. The two arms differ not only in LLM content but in interactivity, engagement, and contact/attention time. There is no attention- or dose-matched control.

**Why it matters.** Any HbA1c and satisfaction difference may be driven by attention/engagement rather than the LLM counseling per se, confounding the causal interpretation that "LLM counseling is effective." This is a design-level threat to internal validity that the analysis cannot fix.

**Suggested action.** Acknowledge the attention confound explicitly and temper causal language; ideally describe (or in future work use) an attention-matched comparator such as a non-LLM scripted chatbot or human-delivered equivalent-contact education, so the LLM component is isolated.

**Problem.** Eligibility is stated only as "adults with type 2 diabetes and a smartphone." There are no exclusion criteria and no entry HbA1c threshold, age range, diabetes duration, treatment regimen, or comorbidity/exclusion specification.

**Why it matters.** CONSORT-4a requires complete eligibility criteria; they determine generalisability and the room-to-improve on HbA1c (a population already near target has little headroom, biasing the effect). The absence of an entry HbA1c criterion also makes the primary-outcome interpretation ambiguous.

**Suggested action.** State full inclusion and exclusion criteria, including baseline HbA1c eligibility window, age range, diabetes duration, background therapy, language/digital-literacy requirements, and any safety exclusions (e.g., severe comorbidity).

**Problem.** The method used to generate the random allocation sequence (CONSORT-8a) and the mechanism used to conceal and implement allocation (CONSORT-9) are not described. Only "randomized 1:1" is stated.

**Why it matters.** Sequence generation and allocation concealment are primary determinants of selection bias; trials without reported concealment show inflated effect estimates. In primary-care recruitment, lack of concealment is a real risk.

**Suggested action.** Report how the sequence was generated (e.g., computer-generated, any stratification/blocking) and the concealment mechanism (e.g., central/electronic randomization, sealed opaque envelopes), and who enrolled and assigned participants.

**Problem.** The manuscript reports only that participants were unblinded. It does not state whether outcome assessors or analysts were blinded. Two of the three outcomes — medication adherence and treatment satisfaction — are self-reported and subjective.

**Why it matters.** CONSORT-11a requires stating who was blinded and how. Unblinded participants reporting their own satisfaction in the arm they know is the 'new technology' is a strong source of expectation/response bias, so the secondary results favouring the intervention may be inflated. HbA1c is objective and partly mitigates this for the primary outcome, but only if assessment was blinded.

**Suggested action.** State whether HbA1c was measured by a blinded central laboratory and whether outcome assessors/statisticians were blinded. Explicitly flag the subjective secondary outcomes as at high risk of detection bias and de-emphasise them accordingly.

**Problem.** No CONSORT flow diagram is provided. The text gives only 220 randomized and 198 completed; it does not report numbers per arm at randomization, receiving the intended intervention, lost to follow-up (with reasons), and analysed.

**Why it matters.** CONSORT-13a requires the flow of participants through each stage. The 22 non-completers (10%) and their distribution and reasons by arm are needed to assess attrition bias — especially important given the ITT-vs-completers discrepancy noted above.

**Suggested action.** Add a CONSORT flow diagram and report, per arm, numbers randomized, receiving allocated intervention, discontinuing/lost (with reasons), and included in the primary analysis.

**Problem.** The primary outcome (HbA1c change at 8 weeks) is named, but the secondary outcomes "medication adherence" and "treatment satisfaction" are not operationally defined: no instrument, scale, scoring, or measurement timing is given. It is also unclear whether outcomes were pre-specified.

**Why it matters.** CONSORT-6a requires completely defined, pre-specified outcome measures. Undefined instruments prevent interpretation and replication and raise concern about outcome selection/reporting bias.

**Suggested action.** Specify the validated instruments used (e.g., a named adherence measure and a named satisfaction scale), their scoring and direction, the assessment timepoints, and confirm pre-specification by reference to the registered protocol/SAP.

**Problem.** The primary endpoint is change in HbA1c at 8 weeks. HbA1c reflects average glycaemia over roughly the preceding 8-12 weeks (red-cell lifespan ~120 days), so at 8 weeks the marker has only partially turned over and may understate or unstably estimate the true glycaemic change.

**Why it matters.** An 8-week HbA1c endpoint is biologically premature; the chosen timepoint can bias the primary effect estimate and limits the clinical interpretability of "improved glycaemic control." This also compounds the "short follow-up" limitation the authors already acknowledge.

**Suggested action.** Justify the 8-week timepoint, ideally support it with a complementary shorter-latency glycaemic measure (e.g., fasting glucose or CGM-derived time-in-range) over the same window, and add a longer endpoint (e.g., 12-24 weeks) in interpretation/future work. Soften claims to reflect the short, partial-turnover window.

**Problem.** The title "Efficacy of LLM-assisted counseling in type-2 diabetes" does not identify the study as a randomized controlled trial (CONSORT-1a) and, although it says "LLM," the title/abstract do not explicitly flag an AI/ML intervention or name the model type (CONSORT-AI-1a). "Efficacy" also pre-judges the result.

**Why it matters.** Title/abstract labelling drives database indexing, journal screening, and reader classification; CONSORT and CONSORT-AI require both the trial design and the AI nature/model type to be signalled. "Efficacy" in the title overstates a single short trial's reach.

**Suggested action.** Revise to identify the design and the AI model, e.g., "An AI (LLM)-based counseling chatbot for glycaemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes: a randomized controlled trial," naming the specific model class/version, and replace "efficacy" with neutral phrasing.

**Problem.** No trial registration number or registry name is reported (CONSORT-23), and there is no statement of where the full trial protocol can be accessed (CONSORT-24).

**Why it matters.** ICMJE and JMIR require prospective registration of clinical trials as a condition of publication; absence (or only retrospective registration) is a frequent rejection trigger and prevents readers from checking outcome pre-specification — directly relevant to the outcome-definition and ITT concerns above.

**Suggested action.** Add the trial registry name and registration number with the registration date (and state whether registration was prospective), and provide a link/citation to the full protocol and statistical analysis plan.

**Problem.** The manuscript does not state whether or how the AI intervention and/or its code can be accessed, including any access/re-use restrictions (CONSORT-AI-25). The data-availability statement is the minimal "available from the corresponding author on reasonable request."

**Why it matters.** For an AI trial, reproducibility depends on access to the model/prompts/code, not just the dataset; "on reasonable request" is increasingly viewed as weak and is discouraged by many journals. Without model/code-access information the system cannot be evaluated or re-used.

**Suggested action.** Add a statement on access to the model/version, system prompts, and any code (or explain proprietary restrictions), and strengthen the data-availability statement toward a repository deposit with a DOI or a clear, time-bound access procedure.

**Problem.** Results report only the direction of effect ("HbA1c reduction was greater in the chatbot arm"; secondary outcomes "favoured the intervention"), and the Abstract says only "HbA1c improved in the chatbot arm." There are no point estimates, no 95% confidence intervals, no p-values, no per-arm means (SD), and no n analysed — for the primary HbA1c outcome or either secondary outcome (CONSORT-17a).

**Why it matters.** Direction-only reporting is uninterpretable, unverifiable, and not independently assessable: readers cannot judge magnitude, precision, or clinical relevance, so the central claim that the intervention is "effective" cannot be supported or reproduced. This is the single largest reporting gap in the manuscript.

**Suggested action.** For the primary outcome, report per-arm baseline and 8-week HbA1c means (SD) and n, plus the adjusted between-group mean difference with 95% CI and p-value from the linear mixed model. Provide the same effect-size + 95% CI (and p-value) for medication adherence and treatment satisfaction. Add an auditable results table reconciled against the Abstract and Discussion.

_Consensus: all four reviewers (critical)._

items.section_ref: Methods / Results — Harms (AI performance errors & safety; CONSORT-AI-19)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** There is no analysis — or even a stated monitoring plan — for LLM performance errors, unsafe or hallucinated outputs, or harms (e.g., incorrect medication/insulin/dietary guidance, failure to recognise hypoglycaemia or hyperglycaemic crisis/DKA), as required by CONSORT-AI-19. No adverse events are reported by arm, and there is no CONSORT harms reporting at all.

**Why it matters.** An LLM delivering largely unsupervised self-management counseling to people with type 2 diabetes can produce clinically harmful advice. A pre-specified error/harms analysis is the quantitative safety component of an AI trial and a prerequisite to any efficacy or adoption claim, especially in a vulnerable chronic-disease population. Reporting glycaemic benefit while remaining silent on whether the model ever gave unsafe advice is not acceptable.

**Suggested action.** Add a harms/safety section stating whether outputs were monitored/audited and how errors and unsafe responses were identified and adjudicated; the rate and severity of identified errors/harms; any human-in-the-loop safeguard and clinical-escalation pathway; and adverse events (including hypoglycaemia) by arm. If no such analysis was planned or done, state this explicitly and substantially temper the conclusions.

_Consensus: all four reviewers (critical for r2 and r3)._

**Problem.** The Abstract concludes "LLM-assisted counseling is effective for glycaemic control" and the Discussion states the findings "show that LLM counseling is effective and should be adopted in routine diabetes care" — definitive, practice-changing causal claims drawn from a single-centre, unblinded, 8-week trial reported without any effect size and with an engagement-confounded comparator. These claims also contradict the manuscript's own Limitations.

**Why it matters.** The strength of the claim is not calibrated to the strength of the evidence. "Effective" implies a confirmed causal effect; "should be adopted in routine care" is a guideline-level recommendation that no single pilot-scale trial can support. Premature translation of this kind misleads clinicians and harms the credibility of digital-health evidence.

**Suggested action.** Downgrade to hedged, evidence-proportionate language tied to the quantified between-group estimate and its 95% CI, restricted to the studied population and 8-week horizon, e.g. "In this single-centre pilot RCT, an LLM counseling chatbot was associated with a greater short-term HbA1c reduction; confirmation in larger, longer, blinded, multi-centre trials with an active digital comparator is required before any change to routine care." Remove the adoption recommendation.

_Consensus: all four reviewers (critical for r3 and r4; major for r1 and r2)._

items.section_ref: Methods / Results — Participant flow, ITT population & missing data (CONSORT-13a)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** Of 220 randomized, only 198 "completed follow-up" (22 missing, ~10%), yet an intention-to-treat (ITT) analysis is claimed — internally inconsistent with a completer count.
