There is no CONSORT flow diagram; no per-arm numbers randomized/treated/lost/analysed; no reasons for dropout by arm; and the missing-data mechanism, the mixed model's missing-at-random (MAR) assumption, and any sensitivity analysis are not stated.

**Why it matters.** True ITT requires all 220 randomized in the primary model. In an unblinded trial, differential attrition can bias the estimate, and a linear mixed model recovers ITT-consistent estimates only under the untestable MAR assumption. Pooled totals hide arm-level imbalance, and the ITT-vs-completer ambiguity prevents appraisal of attrition bias and of the denominators behind every reported effect.

**Suggested action.** Add a CONSORT flow diagram and text giving, per arm, the numbers randomized, receiving the allocated intervention, lost to follow-up (with reasons), and analysed. Define the ITT population and confirm whether all 220 contribute to the primary model. State the missing-data assumption (MAR) and add a pre-specified sensitivity analysis (e.g., multiple imputation and a tipping-point / pattern-mixture analysis).

_Consensus: all four reviewers (r1 rated the ITT/missing-data validity component critical)._

items.section_ref: Methods — Interventions (delivery, dose, input/output handling, human–AI interaction; CONSORT-5, CONSORT-AI-4b, 5ii–5vi)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** The intervention is described in essentially one clause ("an LLM counseling chatbot"). The following are missing: delivery platform and how the AI was integrated into the trial/care setting (app/SMS/web; onsite vs home use; AI-4b); session dose, frequency, and duration; what input data the model consumed and how poor-quality/unavailable inputs were handled (5ii–5iii); the output type and how outputs fed into self-management/clinical decisions (5v–5vi); the human–AI interaction model, degree of clinician oversight/escalation, and required user expertise (5iv). The control ("standard printed diabetes education materials") and its contact intensity are also underspecified.

**Why it matters.** For an AI trial the intervention specification is the science. Without it the study cannot be replicated, risk-of-bias and co-intervention/attention imbalance cannot be appraised, and readers cannot judge whether benefit derives from the LLM or from added contact time.

**Suggested action.** Add an Interventions subsection covering delivery platform and integration setting, exposure/dose schedule, input-data handling, output type and intended use, human–AI interaction and clinician oversight/escalation pathway, and required user skill. Describe the control materials and their contact intensity so contact time can be compared across arms.

_Consensus: r1, r2, r3, r4 (critical for r4)._

items.section_ref: Title / Abstract / Methods — Model identity & version (CONSORT-AI-1a, 5i)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** The manuscript never names the model, vendor, or version of the LLM, nor its system prompt/configuration or generation settings; the intervention is referred to only as "an LLM counseling chatbot" (CONSORT-AI-1a, 5i).

**Why it matters.** Commercial LLMs are updated and drift over time. Without the exact model identity, version, and configuration, the intervention cannot be reproduced and the results cannot be interpreted, compared, or replicated — a core reproducibility failure for an AI trial. The model/version is also needed to appraise the safety profile.

**Suggested action.** State the specific base model, provider, and version/release date; any fine-tuning, system prompt, retrieval grounding, and key generation settings (e.g., temperature); the calendar/deployment window during which the model was in use; and whether the model was frozen for the trial duration.

_Consensus: all four reviewers._

items.section_ref: Methods — Randomisation (sequence generation & allocation concealment; CONSORT-8a, 9)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** Only "randomized 1:1" is stated. The method of sequence generation (CONSORT-8a) and the allocation-concealment mechanism (CONSORT-9) are not described, nor is any stratification/blocking, nor who enrolled and assigned participants.

**Why it matters.** Sequence generation and concealment are the core defences against selection bias and are empirically associated with inflated treatment effects; their omission leaves baseline comparability and randomization-based inference unverifiable.

**Suggested action.** Describe how the sequence was generated (e.g., computer-generated, with block size and any stratification such as baseline HbA1c or site/clinician), the concealment mechanism (e.g., central/online allocation or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes), and who generated the sequence and enrolled/assigned participants.

_Consensus: all four reviewers._

items.section_ref: Methods / Results — Blinding of assessors & analysts; subjective secondary outcomes (CONSORT-11a)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** Participants are acknowledged as unblinded, but the report does not state whether the HbA1c outcome assessors and the statistician were blinded (CONSORT-11a). Two of three outcomes — medication adherence and treatment satisfaction — appear self-reported and subjective.

**Why it matters.** HbA1c is a laboratory measure that can be assessor- and analyst-blinded even when participants are not, materially reducing ascertainment bias. Unblinded subjective/self-report outcomes are at high risk of expectation/response bias that systematically favours the novel interactive arm, making the secondary findings the most bias-prone in the trial (satisfaction with a new chatbot vs a paper handout is almost guaranteed to favour the chatbot).

**Suggested action.** State explicitly who was and was not blinded (participants, providers, outcome assessors, analysts). Prefer objective adherence measures (e.g., pharmacy refill/MPR, pill counts) over self-report, name the validated satisfaction instrument, and explicitly flag treatment satisfaction and self-reported adherence as high-risk-of-bias outcomes, tempering the corresponding claims.

_Consensus: all four reviewers._

**Problem.** No sample-size justification or power analysis is reported. The trial enrolled 220 participants but states no target HbA1c difference (MCID), assumed SD, alpha, target power, allocation ratio, or attrition allowance; the N appears asserted rather than derived (CONSORT-7a).

**Why it matters.** Without an a priori effect size and power, the trial's ability to detect a clinically meaningful HbA1c difference is unknown: a direction-only "positive" result could be underpowered or the N chosen post hoc, and any null secondary result is uninterpretable.

**Suggested action.** Report the pre-specified primary-outcome MCID for HbA1c (commonly 0.3–0.5%), the assumed SD, two-sided alpha, target power, 1:1 allocation, and assumed attrition, and show how these yield the planned N. Reconcile the planned N with the 220 randomized and 198 analysed. If no a priori calculation was performed, state this explicitly.

_Consensus: all four reviewers (critical for r1)._

**Problem.** No trial registration number or registry name appears anywhere, and there is no statement of where the full protocol / statistical analysis plan (SAP) can be accessed (CONSORT-23, 24). Outcomes are described as "pre-specified" but this cannot be verified.

**Why it matters.** Prospective registration (ICMJE; JMIR Diabetes) and an accessible protocol/SAP are the principal safeguards against selective outcome reporting and post hoc analytic choices — especially relevant given the unusual 8-week endpoint and unquantified secondary outcomes — and their absence is frequently grounds for editorial rejection.

**Suggested action.** Provide the registry name, registration ID, and registration date (ideally prospective, before enrolment); confirm whether registration was prospective; and give a link to the full protocol and SAP so the primary outcome, timepoint, and analysis model can be confirmed as pre-specified. If registered retrospectively or unregistered, disclose and justify.

_Consensus: all four reviewers (critical for r2)._

items.section_ref: Methods — Interventions / Discussion (attention/engagement confound; active comparator)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** An interactive, on-demand LLM chatbot is compared against static printed education materials, so the arms differ in engagement, attention, contact time, and digital interactivity — not only in LLM-generated content. There is no attention/engagement-matched comparator.

**Why it matters.** Any improvement in HbA1c, adherence, or satisfaction could reflect a generic attention/engagement ("digital placebo"/Hawthorne) effect rather than the LLM specifically. With a paper comparator the trial cannot attribute the effect to the LLM, which is the actual research question — directly undercutting the causal interpretation.

**Suggested action.** Acknowledge this as a primary limitation and temper attribution to the LLM. Report contact time and platform usage/engagement by arm, and adjust for engagement if such data exist. For future work, recommend an active digital comparator (e.g., a non-LLM scripted/rule-based chatbot or app with matched contact) to isolate the LLM-attributable effect.

_Consensus: r2, r3 (also noted within r4's intervention finding)._

items.section_ref: Methods — Outcomes (primary endpoint timing: HbA1c at 8 weeks; CONSORT-6a)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** The primary endpoint is change in HbA1c assessed at 8 weeks.

**Why it matters.** HbA1c reflects average glycaemia over roughly the preceding 8–12 weeks (erythrocyte lifespan ~120 days) and equilibrates to a new steady state only by about 12 weeks, so an 8-week window captures only a partial, still-equilibrating, attenuated response. The measurement interval is biologically mismatched to the biomarker, likely biasing the estimate toward the null and limiting interpretability; diabetes trials conventionally assess HbA1c at ≥12 weeks (commonly 3–6 months).

**Suggested action.** Justify the 8-week timing explicitly, ideally report HbA1c at ≥12 weeks as the primary timepoint (or reframe the 8-week value as interim/preliminary), and acknowledge the equilibration lag when interpreting effect magnitude. Consider a complementary measure responsive over 8 weeks (e.g., fasting glucose, fructosamine, or CGM-derived time-in-range).

_Consensus: r1, r2, r3._

items.section_ref: Results / Methods — Baseline characteristics table & baseline-HbA1c adjustment
items.content_md:
**Problem.** No baseline characteristics table is provided; baseline HbA1c and key prognostic covariates by arm are not reported, and it is unclear whether the linear mixed model adjusts for baseline HbA1c.

**Why it matters.** A "reduction in HbA1c" is uninterpretable without the starting value and arm comparability. Baseline HbA1c is the dominant prognostic covariate, so an unadjusted change analysis is less efficient and vulnerable to regression-to-the-mean and chance baseline imbalance that could fully explain the observed difference. Baseline-adjusted (ANCOVA-type) modelling is the recommended primary analysis for change-from-baseline outcomes.

**Suggested action.** Add a baseline characteristics table by arm (HbA1c, age, sex, diabetes duration, baseline therapy/regimen, and adherence/satisfaction baselines), and confirm the primary model adjusts for baseline HbA1c and any stratification factors; report the adjusted between-arm difference.

_Consensus: r1, r3._

items.section_ref: Methods — Participants (eligibility, baseline-HbA1c threshold, input-data criteria; CONSORT-4a, CONSORT-AI-4a)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** Eligibility is described only as "adults with type 2 diabetes and a smartphone" — no exclusion criteria, no baseline-HbA1c entry threshold, no age range, no language/literacy/device requirements, and no input-data-level criteria for the AI (CONSORT-4a; CONSORT-AI-4a).

**Why it matters.** Eligibility defines the analysis population and bounds generalizability. A baseline-HbA1c floor is particularly important for a glycaemic-change endpoint (room to improve; regression to the mean if poorly controlled patients enrolled). For a text-based LLM, language and digital/health-literacy requirements are effectively input-data inclusion criteria that shape both efficacy and generalizability.

**Suggested action.** Specify full inclusion/exclusion criteria, including any baseline-HbA1c range, age bounds, diabetes type/duration, comorbidity/medication constraints, language/digital-literacy and smartphone-competency requirements, and the input-data-level criteria the LLM intervention required.

_Consensus: r2, r3, r4._

items.section_ref: Methods / Results — Multiplicity (primary + two secondary outcomes)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** Three outcomes are tested — HbA1c (primary), medication adherence, and treatment satisfaction (secondary) — with no stated multiplicity control, yet the conclusion explicitly leans on the secondary outcomes ("also favoured the intervention").

**Why it matters.** Multiple unadjusted comparisons inflate the family-wise type I error. Secondary outcomes are typically not individually powered and should be treated as hypothesis-generating, not as confirmatory support for effectiveness.

**Suggested action.** Pre-specify the single primary outcome and a multiplicity strategy (e.g., hierarchical testing or alpha allocation across secondaries), or explicitly label secondary analyses as exploratory, and confine confirmatory effectiveness claims to the primary HbA1c outcome.

_Consensus: r1._

items.section_ref: Methods / Results — Concomitant medication changes & co-interventions by arm
items.content_md:
**Problem.** The trial does not report whether glucose-lowering medication changes (or other co-interventions) during follow-up were tracked or balanced between arms.

**Why it matters.** An 8-week HbA1c difference could be driven by differential medication titration or co-interventions rather than the chatbot itself — especially since a counseling intervention may prompt clinic contact and treatment intensification — confounding the efficacy estimate.

**Suggested action.** Report medication changes and other co-interventions by arm during follow-up, adjust for or describe their balance, and discuss residual confounding in the Discussion.

_Consensus: r2._

items.section_ref: Results / Discussion — Clinical significance / minimal clinically important difference (MCID)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** "Improved" / "greater reduction" is asserted without reference to a minimal clinically important difference (MCID); no magnitude is given against any clinical benchmark.

**Why it matters.** Statistical direction is not clinical relevance. An HbA1c difference of ~0.1–0.2% would be clinically trivial even if statistically detectable, whereas ~0.3–0.5% is typically the threshold for meaningful glycaemic benefit. Without anchoring to an MCID the reader cannot judge whether the intervention is clinically worthwhile.

**Suggested action.** Pre-specify and report the between-arm HbA1c difference against a stated MCID (e.g., 0.3–0.5%), and interpret secondary outcomes against validated thresholds rather than direction alone.

_Consensus: r3._

**Problem.** Outcomes are incompletely operationalized. "Change in HbA1c" does not specify the baseline reference, units, assay/standardisation, or analysis metric (absolute change vs ANCOVA on follow-up value). Medication adherence and treatment satisfaction are named but give no instrument, scale, score range/direction, validation, or assessment timing (CONSORT-6a).

**Why it matters.** Undefined measures cannot be appraised for validity or compared across studies; "favoured the intervention" is uninterpretable without knowing what was measured and how; and analytic flexibility in an undefined metric can bias results toward significance.

**Suggested action.** Define each outcome precisely — HbA1c metric/units/assay and baseline reference; the named validated adherence instrument (e.g., self-report scale vs pharmacy MPR) and satisfaction instrument with score range and timing; and the exact statistic/model used — and state which were pre-specified primary vs secondary.

_Consensus: r1, r4._

items.section_ref: Abstract / Results / Discussion — Within-arm vs between-arm framing & ITT population consistency
items.content_md:
**Problem.** The framing shifts between within-arm and between-arm without resolution: the Abstract reports a within-arm change ("HbA1c improved in the chatbot arm"), Results reports a between-arm comparison ("reduction was greater in the chatbot arm"), and the Discussion asserts efficacy. The Methods also claim an ITT analysis while Results foreground the 198 completers, leaving the analysis population ambiguous.

**Why it matters.** A within-arm improvement alone does not establish efficacy; only a between-group contrast does. Abstract, Results, and Discussion must refer to the same between-group, ITT estimate; the current mismatch reads as the conclusion outrunning the analysis.

**Suggested action.** State the between-group difference consistently across Abstract, Results, and Discussion, confirm it is the ITT estimate, and reserve "effective"/"improved" for the between-arm contrast (reporting any within-arm change separately if desired).

_Consensus: r4._

items.section_ref: Title / Abstract — Trial & model identification (CONSORT-1a, CONSORT-AI-1a)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** The title ("Efficacy of LLM-assisted counseling in type-2 diabetes") does not identify the study as a randomized controlled trial (CONSORT-1a), does not specify the AI model class/architecture (CONSORT-AI-1a), and leads with "Efficacy," asserting a confirmed effect before any estimate is shown. The abstract is also unstructured around the design/model.

**Why it matters.** Indexing, database retrieval, and rapid reader appraisal depend on the title flagging the design and the AI nature; CONSORT-AI specifically asks that AI involvement and model type be signalled. "Efficacy" also pre-commits the reader to a conclusion the data must earn.

**Suggested action.** Revise to, e.g., "A [model]-based large-language-model counseling chatbot vs printed education for glycaemic control in type 2 diabetes: a randomized controlled trial," and mirror the "randomized" and model descriptors in the Abstract's first line.

_Consensus: r2, r3, r4 (minor for r2/r3; r4 weighted higher)._

items.section_ref: Declarations — AI model/code access & developer competing interests (CONSORT-AI-25; CONSORT-24)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** There is no statement of whether/how the AI intervention or its code can be accessed, including re-use restrictions (CONSORT-AI-25), nor where the protocol resides (CONSORT-24). The competing-interests declaration is "none," but the relationship between the authors and the chatbot's developers is not addressed.

**Why it matters.** Model/code and protocol access are needed for reproducibility and scrutiny — particularly for a proprietary LLM whose behaviour may change between versions — and any author role in developing or owning the chatbot is a material competing interest that must be disclosed even absent a financial conflict.

**Suggested action.** Add an AI-access statement specifying whether the model/prompts/code are available, under what terms, and any restrictions (state explicitly if proprietary/unavailable); link the protocol; and explicitly address any author role in developing or owning the chatbot in the competing-interests declaration. (Protocol availability also reinforces the trial-registration finding.)

_Consensus: r3, r4._

**Problem.** The smartphone and text-based LLM-interaction requirement is noted only implicitly; the generalizability discussion covers single-centre status but not the digital/literacy divide.

**Why it matters.** Older, lower-income, and lower-literacy patients carry a high type 2 diabetes burden but are least able to use a text LLM chatbot, so the benefit may not transfer to the populations with greatest need — relevant to JMIR Diabetes readers and to equity-of-access claims.

**Suggested action.** Add the smartphone/literacy/language requirement and digital-divide implications to the Limitations and temper generalizability accordingly.

_Consensus: r3._

**Problem:** The Results and Abstract report no quantitative data — only direction of effect ("Mean HbA1c reduction was greater in the chatbot arm"; secondary outcomes "favoured the intervention"; abstract "HbA1c improved"). There are no per-arm analyzed Ns, baseline/follow-up HbA1c values (mean ± SD), within-arm change, between-group adjusted difference, 95% CI, p-value, or any effect estimate from the stated linear mixed model, for the primary or any secondary outcome.

**Why it matters:** Without point estimates and precision, the central efficacy claim is unverifiable, non-reproducible, and cannot be meta-analyzed. CONSORT item 17a requires, for each outcome, the results in each group and the estimated effect size with its precision (e.g., adjusted mean difference with 95% CI). The manuscript currently asserts a conclusion the reader cannot check.

**Suggested action:** Report, for each arm: n analyzed, baseline and 8-week HbA1c (mean ± SD), within-arm change, and the model-based adjusted between-group difference with 95% CI and p-value. Provide the same level of detail (with named instruments) for medication adherence and treatment satisfaction, add a results table, and mirror the key primary-outcome estimate in the abstract.

_Raised independently by r1, r2, r3, r4._

**Problem:** The conclusions overreach the data. The Abstract states "LLM-assisted counseling is effective for glycaemic control in type 2 diabetes," and the Discussion asserts the findings "show that LLM counseling is effective and should be adopted in routine diabetes care" — a definitive efficacy-and-implementation claim drawn from a single-centre, unblinded, 8-week trial with no reported effect size or statistical inference, and inconsistent with the manuscript's own stated limitations.

**Why it matters:** Causal and policy-level claims ("effective," "should be adopted") are not licensed by the design or the (unreported) data and could drive premature clinical adoption of an AI tool. Reviewers and JMIR editors will treat this as the central credibility problem, and it undercuts the credibility of any genuine signal.

**Suggested action:** Recalibrate to evidence-proportional, hypothesis-generating language tied to the actual effect estimate (e.g., "In this single-centre pilot RCT, an LLM counseling chatbot was associated with a greater short-term reduction in HbA1c than printed education"), explicitly bounded by the single-centre, short-term, unblinded design. Delete the routine-adoption recommendation and replace it with a call for larger, longer, multi-centre, registered, actively controlled confirmatory trials before any practice change.

_Raised independently by r1, r2, r3, r4 (rated critical by r2/r3/r4, major by r1)._

**Problem:** No adverse events, harms, safety-monitoring procedures, clinical oversight, or guardrails against inaccurate/unsafe chatbot advice (e.g., hallucinated dosing, hypoglycaemia or medication-change guidance) are reported anywhere in the manuscript.

**Why it matters:** An LLM delivering diabetes self-management counseling can produce confident but incorrect or harmful advice. Harms reporting is a core CONSORT item (item 19) and a CONSORT-EHEALTH expectation, and for a patient-facing clinical chatbot the absence of any safety account is both a patient-safety/ethics gap and a barrier to benefit–risk assessment. JMIR Diabetes reviewers will treat this as a gating concern.

**Suggested action:** Add a safety subsection describing how unsafe or erroneous outputs were prevented, detected, and escalated (e.g., content constraints, clinician review / human-in-the-loop, escalation pathway to a clinician, output auditing), and how adverse events were defined, monitored, and adjudicated. Report adverse events by arm (including hypoglycaemia events and chatbot errors) — or explicitly state and justify that no safety monitoring was performed and flag it as a limitation.

_Raised independently by r1, r2, r3, r4 (rated critical by r1/r3/r4, major by r2)._

**Problem:** No prospective trial registration is reported (no registry name, trial identifier, or registration date), and there is no protocol/SAP reference.

**Why it matters:** ICMJE, CONSORT (item 23), and JMIR require prospective registration of randomized trials; its absence raises concern about outcome switching and selective reporting and is commonly grounds for desk rejection or a barrier to publication.

**Suggested action:** Add the registry name, trial identifier, and registration date, and confirm registration preceded enrollment. Cite the protocol/SAP and reconcile the reported primary/secondary outcomes with those registered. If the trial was registered retrospectively or is unregistered, disclose and justify this explicitly.

_Raised independently by r1, r2, r3, r4 (rated critical by r2/r4, major by r1/r3; consolidated as critical)._

**Problem:** The intervention is described only as "an LLM counseling chatbot." Unspecified: model/vendor and version (with access date), prompt/system-instruction or conversation design, counseling content scope, clinical guardrails, intended dose/frequency and access duration, human/clinician oversight, delivery format and any patient tailoring, and observed engagement/usage. The control ("standard printed diabetes education materials") is also thinly specified.

**Why it matters:** For a JMIR AI/eHealth trial the digital intervention is the core scientific contribution; without CONSORT-AI / CONSORT-EHEALTH / TIDieR-level detail the study is not reproducible and reviewers cannot appraise the safety or appropriateness of the model's advice.

**Suggested action:** Provide a TIDieR / CONSORT-EHEALTH-style intervention subsection: model name/version and access date; prompt/protocol design and safety filtering/guardrails; counseling topics; who supervised and how unsafe outputs were handled; prescribed and observed usage/engagement metrics (sessions, messages, active days), ideally by arm; and whether outputs were clinician-reviewed. Describe the printed control materials concretely so the contrast is interpretable.

_Raised independently by r1, r2, r3, r4 (rated major by r1/r3/r4, critical by r2; consolidated as major)._

**Problem:** The primary outcome, change in HbA1c, is assessed at 8 weeks. HbA1c reflects average glycaemia over roughly the preceding 8–12 weeks (erythrocyte lifespan ~120 days), so an 8-week endpoint captures only a partial, still-equilibrating signal and falls short of the conventional ~3-month window.

**Why it matters:** A short window can attenuate or unreliably estimate the true effect and gives no information on durability, weakening both the validity of the measured change and any inference of durable glycaemic benefit — and thus the headline claim.

**Suggested action:** Justify the 8-week timing physiologically and, if feasible, report a ≥12-week (ideally ≥6-month) HbA1c endpoint. At minimum, upgrade the generic "follow-up was short" limitation to a specific statement of why 8 weeks may underestimate or misestimate the effect, and consider a complementary shorter-latency measure (fasting glucose or CGM time-in-range) if available.

_Raised independently by r1, r2, r3, r4._

**Problem:** No a priori sample-size or power calculation is provided for the 220 enrolled (198 analyzed) participants; the basis for the target N and the 1:1 ratio is unstated.

**Why it matters:** Without a pre-specified target effect, assumed SD, alpha, power, and attrition allowance, readers cannot judge whether the trial was adequately powered for the primary HbA1c endpoint, and a non-significant or borderline result cannot be distinguished from underpowering. This is a required CONSORT item (7a).

**Suggested action:** Report the sample-size calculation with all assumptions — the minimal clinically important HbA1c difference, assumed SD, two-sided alpha, target power, and dropout allowance — and reconcile it with the 220 enrolled / 198 analyzed. If this is a pilot/feasibility study, state that and reframe the outcomes accordingly.

_Raised independently by r1, r2, r3, r4._

**Problem:** The randomization process is not described — only "randomized 1:1." Missing: random-sequence generation method, any stratification/blocking, allocation-concealment mechanism, who enrolled and assigned participants, and the blinding status of outcome assessors and statisticians. Only participant unblinding is noted (as a limitation).

**Why it matters:** Allocation concealment and assessor/analyst blinding are primary determinants of internal validity (CONSORT items 8–11) and the leading sources of selection and detection bias. Because HbA1c is laboratory-measured, it could plausibly be assessed blind even with unblinded participants, so the unblinding limitation may overstate detection-bias risk if assessors/lab were in fact masked.

**Suggested action:** Describe the sequence-generation method, allocation-concealment mechanism, any stratification, and the separation of roles (who generated the sequence, enrolled participants, and assigned arms). State whether outcome assessors, the HbA1c laboratory, and statisticians were blinded, and note explicitly if the assay was blinded.

_Raised independently by r1, r2, r3, r4._

items.section_ref: Methods / Results (participant flow, ITT & missing data)
items.content_md:
**Problem:** Of 220 randomized, 198 completed (~10% loss), but attrition is not broken down by arm, reasons for the 22 dropouts are not given, the analyzed N per arm is unstated, and there is no CONSORT flow diagram. An intention-to-treat analysis with a linear mixed model is asserted, yet only completers appear to be described and the missing-data approach is unspecified — so the analysis may be a completer/per-protocol analysis mislabeled as ITT.

**Why it matters:** Differential or informative attrition biases between-arm comparisons; true ITT analyzes all randomized participants, and an ITT/mixed-model claim is only credible when the analyzed denominators and missingness mechanism are specified. CONSORT requires per-arm flow including losses and exclusions with reasons (items 13a/13b).

**Suggested action:** Add a CONSORT flow diagram (randomized → allocated → lost with reasons → analyzed, by arm); confirm the analyzed N per arm and that all randomized participants contribute; compare baseline characteristics of completers vs. dropouts; state the assumed missing-data mechanism (e.g., MAR) and how the mixed model handled it; and add a sensitivity analysis under alternative missingness assumptions. Reconcile the "198 completed" statement with the analyzed N.

_Raised independently by r1, r2, r3, r4._

**Problem:** The interactive, on-demand LLM chatbot is compared against static "standard printed diabetes education materials," a passive, no-contact control. The arms differ not only in LLM content but in interactivity, attention, contact time, and digital engagement, with no attention-matched or active digital comparator.

**Why it matters:** Any observed benefit may reflect greater attention, engagement, or contact time rather than LLM-specific counseling — a classic digital-intervention confound, amplified by the unblinded design — which threatens the internal validity of the title's and conclusion's attribution of the effect to the LLM itself.

**Suggested action:** Acknowledge the active-vs-passive imbalance prominently as a key limitation; report between-arm contact time/engagement where available; frame conclusions as "LLM chatbot vs usual/printed education" rather than a test of LLM-specific efficacy; and recommend an attention-matched or non-LLM interactive comparator for confirmatory work.

_Raised independently by r1, r3, r4._

**Problem:** The structured abstract is uninformative for a randomized trial: it omits trial registration, the randomized/analyzed sample size and 1:1 ratio, the single-centre design, the specific LLM used, the primary-outcome effect estimate with 95% CI and p-value, the 8-week outcome timing, and key secondary results, while stating an unqualified "effective" conclusion.

**Why it matters:** Readers and screeners appraise the result from the abstract alone; CONSORT-for-Abstracts (and CONSORT-AI abstract items) require key design and quantitative results, and the current conclusion is stronger than the data shown.

**Suggested action:** Rewrite the structured abstract to include design (RCT, 1:1, single centre), n randomized/analyzed, the named intervention vs. control, the 8-week primary endpoint with adjusted between-arm difference and 95% CI and p-value, key secondary results, the registration identifier, and a calibrated conclusion aligned with the actual effect estimate.

_Raised independently by r1, r2._

**Problem:** Eligibility is described only as "adults with type 2 diabetes and a smartphone," with no baseline glycaemic threshold or exclusion criteria, and there is no baseline characteristics table by arm (no demographics, diabetes duration, baseline HbA1c, or medication use).

**Why it matters:** Without complete inclusion/exclusion criteria and a Table 1 by arm, the success of randomization and between-arm comparability cannot be assessed and generalizability is unclear. Because the primary outcome is HbA1c change with no eligibility HbA1c threshold, possible floor effects (participants already well controlled) cannot be ruled out. CONSORT items 6a and 15.

**Suggested action:** Specify the complete eligibility criteria (including any baseline HbA1c threshold and key exclusions) and add a baseline characteristics table by arm including baseline HbA1c, diabetes duration, and treatment/medication use.

_Raised independently by r1, r3, r4._

**Problem:** The manuscript contains no citations and no reference list. Empirical and background claims (e.g., "Conversational agents have shown promise for patient education") and the framing of LLM counseling are unreferenced.

**Why it matters:** Unsupported background and methodological claims do not meet journal standards and prevent readers from situating the work in prior evidence; references are required for publication.

**Suggested action:** Add citations for all background and methodological claims (prior chatbot/diabetes-education trials, LLM-in-health evidence, the reporting guideline followed) and include a complete reference list in the journal's format.

_Raised by r2 (single source, retained)._

**Problem:** Eligibility requires owning a smartphone, but the resulting selection effect on generalizability is not discussed.

**Why it matters:** Smartphone-gated enrollment can exclude older, lower-income, or less digitally literate patients — populations with high diabetes burden — limiting external validity and raising digital-equity concerns relevant to a digital-health journal.

**Suggested action:** Acknowledge the digital-access selection in Limitations, report participant demographics/digital-literacy where available, and discuss applicability to populations without reliable smartphone access.

_Raised independently by r1, r2._

**Problem:** The secondary outcomes (medication adherence, treatment satisfaction) are named but not operationalized — no validated instrument, scoring, or measurement timepoints are given — and it is unclear whether HbA1c was measured by a central laboratory or point-of-care.

**Why it matters:** Unspecified or non-validated measures are uninterpretable and non-reproducible and are vulnerable to measurement bias, making the "favoured the intervention" claims hard to appraise. CONSORT requires completely defined, pre-specified outcomes with how and when they were assessed.

**Suggested action:** Name and cite the validated adherence and satisfaction scales, their scoring ranges and administration timepoints, and the HbA1c assay/method; distinguish pre-specified from post hoc outcomes.

_Raised independently by r1, r2, r3, r4 (rated minor by r1/r3/r4, major by r2; consolidated as minor)._

**Problem:** The analysis is described only as "a linear mixed model" following ITT. Fixed/random effects, covariate adjustment (e.g., baseline HbA1c, site), correlation/time structure, software, and — critically — handling of multiplicity across one primary and multiple secondary outcomes are unspecified, with no statement of which comparison is primary for inference.

**Why it matters:** Under-specified models are not reproducible, and testing multiple secondary outcomes without a multiplicity plan inflates type I error, so "also favoured the intervention" may not be statistically robust.

**Suggested action:** Fully specify the model (outcome, fixed and random effects, covariates, time structure, software), state the single primary estimand/comparison, and describe multiplicity control or clearly label secondary outcomes as exploratory/hypothesis-generating.

_Raised independently by r1, r2, r3, r4 (rated minor by r1/r3/r4, major by r2; consolidated as minor)._

**Problem:** Treatment satisfaction and self-reported medication adherence are subjective outcomes assessed in an unblinded trial with an engaging intervention versus a passive control, yet the Discussion concludes the intervention "was well received" based on them.

**Why it matters:** Subjective, self-reported outcomes under unblinded, attention-imbalanced conditions are especially prone to expectation/response bias, so the "well received" and adherence differences may reflect the novelty/attention of an AI tool rather than true benefit. (This interpretation caution is distinct from, but compounded by, the comparator-confound and unspecified-blinding issues above.)

**Suggested action:** Interpret satisfaction and self-reported adherence cautiously and explicitly flag this bias in the Limitations; discuss attention/placebo effects as alternative explanations; and where possible support adherence with an objective measure (pharmacy refill/MPR or electronic monitoring).

_Raised independently by r2, r3 (rated major by r2, minor by r3; consolidated as minor)._

**Problem:** The Discussion interprets a "greater" HbA1c reduction without reference to clinical significance — no threshold for a clinically meaningful reduction is given.

**Why it matters:** A statistically larger reduction can be clinically trivial; without anchoring to a minimal clinically important difference (commonly ~0.3–0.5% HbA1c), the clinical relevance of the finding is unestablished.

**Suggested action:** Once effect sizes are reported, interpret the between-group HbA1c difference against an explicit clinically meaningful threshold and discuss whether the observed effect crosses it.

_Raised by r4 (single source, retained)._

**Problem:** Data are "available from the corresponding author on reasonable request," with no repository, analysis code, statistical analysis plan, or sharing of the chatbot configuration/prompts.

**Why it matters:** "On reasonable request" is a weak, poorly reproducible commitment below JMIR's open-science expectations; for an LLM intervention the model configuration/prompts and analysis code are essential reproducibility artifacts.

**Suggested action:** Deposit de-identified data and analysis code in a repository (or justify restrictions, with a DOI where feasible), strengthen the data-availability statement, and share or describe how to obtain the chatbot prompts/configuration and the SAP, with appropriate privacy and safety safeguards.

_Raised independently by r1, r2._

**Problem.** No outcome is quantified anywhere. The Results give only direction — "mean HbA1c reduction was greater in the chatbot arm," adherence and satisfaction "favoured the intervention" — with no per-arm baseline/follow-up values, no between-group effect estimate, no 95% CI, no measure of variability, no p-values, and no per-arm analysed n.

**Why it matters.** CONSORT-17a requires, for each primary and secondary outcome, the result in each group and the estimated effect size with its 95% CI. Without magnitudes and precision, readers cannot judge whether the HbA1c difference reaches a minimal clinically important difference (~0.3–0.5% HbA1c) or is statistically reliable, so every downstream efficacy claim is unverifiable. At JMIR this is a desk-reject-level deficiency.

**Action.** Add a primary results table: baseline and 8-week HbA1c per arm (mean ± SD, n analysed); the adjusted between-arm difference from the linear mixed model with 95% CI and p; and the same triplet for medication adherence and treatment satisfaction. State the model covariates and the estimand.

*(Flagged independently by all four reviewers.)*

**Problem.** The Discussion states "these findings show that LLM counseling is effective and should be adopted in routine diabetes care," and the Abstract concludes "LLM-assisted counseling is effective for glycaemic control." These are definitive causal and practice-change claims drawn from a single-centre, unblinded, 8-week trial that reports no effect size and that contradicts the manuscript's own stated limitations.

**Why it matters.** The strength of the conclusion and the routine-adoption recommendation outrun the evidence (surrogate endpoint, short horizon, no CIs, no replication, unblinding bias). CONSORT abstract/Discussion conventions require conclusions consistent with the results and balancing benefits and harms; over-claiming of this kind is a common cause of reviewer pushback and desk rejection.

**Action.** Recalibrate to evidence-consistent, hypothesis-generating language tied to the actual point estimates and 95% CIs (e.g., "the chatbot arm showed a greater short-term reduction in HbA1c; longer, blinded, multi-centre trials with formal safety evaluation are needed before routine adoption"). Remove the unconditional adoption recommendation and bound every efficacy statement by the stated limitations. Align the Abstract conclusion accordingly.

*(Flagged by all four reviewers; one rated it critical.)*

**Problem.** The intervention — the entire object of study — is described only as "an LLM counseling chatbot," and the control only as "standard printed diabetes education materials." Missing: which model/vendor and exact version, and whether the model was frozen or silently updated during the 8 weeks (CONSORT-AI-5i); the system prompt / counseling content and any fine-tuning/RAG/guardrail layers; how input data were acquired and selected (5ii); how poor-quality or unavailable input was handled (5iii); the degree of human–AI interaction, clinician oversight, and user expertise required (5iv); the output type delivered to patients (5v) and how outputs were intended to influence self-management decisions (5vi); how the chatbot was integrated into the primary-care workflow, onsite vs offsite (4b); and the dose/frequency/duration of use. The control's content and contact time are also undefined.

**Why it matters.** CONSORT-5 requires enough detail to replicate, and the CONSORT-AI extension exists precisely because an unversioned, unspecified "LLM chatbot" cannot be reproduced, audited, or implemented — LLM behaviour changes across versions and over time. As written, no other group could rebuild the intervention and a reader cannot assess what was actually tested or its validity/safety.

**Action.** Add a structured intervention subsection (or TIDieR-style table/supplement) specifying the model name and version with deployment date range and whether it was frozen; the prompt/guardrail design and counseling logic; content sources and clinician oversight; input acquisition, selection, and fallback handling; the human–AI interaction model and required user skill; the output format and intended behaviour-change pathway; usage intensity/dose; and integration into the clinic. Specify the exact control materials and their contact time, ideally with example dialogues in a supplement.

*(Flagged by all four reviewers; three rated it critical.)*

**Problem.** There is no analysis of AI performance errors, no safety monitoring, and no adverse-event reporting, nor any statement that such analysis was or was not planned. The manuscript does not say whether chatbot outputs were reviewed for inaccurate or unsafe counseling, how often errors occurred, or how they were handled.

**Why it matters.** CONSORT-AI-19 requires describing how performance errors were identified and analysed (or an explicit statement that none was planned/done), and CONSORT item 19 requires all-cause harms per arm. An LLM counseling adults on diabetes self-management can produce incorrect or unsafe advice (e.g., on medication/insulin titration, diet, or hypoglycaemia management); recommending routine adoption with no safety data is not defensible and is a safety-critical gap, not optional.

**Action.** Report whether outputs were monitored/clinician-reviewed for accuracy and safety, the rate and nature of erroneous or unsafe responses and how they were detected and escalated, any guardrails/human oversight (including red-flag/crisis handling), and adverse events (including hypoglycaemia) by arm. If no error analysis was performed, state this explicitly, justify it, and reflect it in the limitations.

*(Flagged by all four reviewers; three rated it critical.)*

**Problem.** No sample-size or power calculation is reported; n=220 randomized appears without justification.

**Why it matters.** CONSORT-7a. Without a pre-specified target effect, assumed SD, alpha, power, and attrition allowance, readers cannot tell whether the trial was adequately powered for the HbA1c endpoint. A positive result may be underpowered or overinterpreted, and non-significant secondary findings may reflect underpowering rather than no effect.

**Action.** State the target between-arm HbA1c difference (e.g., 0.5%), the assumed SD and its source, two-sided alpha, power, anticipated attrition, and the resulting target N, and reconcile this with the 220 enrolled. If the sample size was not formally calculated, say so and frame the study as a pilot/feasibility trial.

*(Flagged by all four reviewers.)*

**Problem.** The Methods state only "randomized 1:1." The parallel-group design is not stated explicitly (3a), and neither the method of generating the allocation sequence (8a: e.g., computer random-number generator, block sizes, stratification) nor the allocation-concealment mechanism (9: e.g., central allocation or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes) is described.

**Why it matters.** Sequence generation and concealment are core internal-validity safeguards; inadequate concealment is among the strongest documented sources of selection bias in RCTs. "Randomized 1:1" alone does not establish that allocation was unbiased and concealed, and risk-of-bias assessment (and meta-analytic inclusion) cannot be completed without it.

**Action.** State the parallel-group design and allocation ratio; describe how the sequence was generated (with any blocking/stratification) and who generated it; describe how allocation was concealed until assignment; and identify who enrolled and assigned participants. Consider adding a baseline characteristics table to demonstrate balance.

*(Flagged by all four reviewers.)*

**Problem.** Blinding is mentioned only in Limitations (participants "unblinded"); the Methods do not state whether outcome assessors, data analysts, or the HbA1c laboratory were blinded. Two secondary outcomes — treatment satisfaction and self-reported medication adherence — are subjective and were collected from unblinded participants in an engaging-chatbot arm versus printed leaflets.

**Why it matters.** CONSORT-11a. With unblinded participants, blinded outcome assessment and analysis are the main remaining safeguards against performance/detection bias, particularly for the subjective secondary outcomes, where unblinding could fully account for the favourable result. Laboratory HbA1c is relatively protected, but the lab's blinding should still be confirmed.

**Action.** State the blinding status of participants, providers, outcome assessors, and analysts and why; confirm HbA1c was measured by a blinded central laboratory; flag the subjective secondary outcomes as at high risk of bias, discuss the likely direction of bias, and temper claims about them.

*(Flagged by r2, r3, r4; also folded into r1's randomisation item.)*

**Problem.** Eligibility is only "adults with type 2 diabetes and a smartphone," enrolled "in primary care." No exclusion criteria, age bounds, diabetes-duration or treatment requirements, language/literacy or digital-literacy requirements, recruitment dates, and — critically — no baseline-HbA1c entry threshold or input-data-level criteria are specified.

**Why it matters.** CONSORT-4a requires full eligibility criteria, and CONSORT-AI-4a expects inclusion/exclusion at the level of the AI input data (e.g., supported languages/message formats). Glycaemic-improvement trials typically enrol above an HbA1c floor (e.g., >7–7.5%) because well-controlled patients have little room to improve; without it, both effect interpretation and generalizability are unclear, as is who the chatbot can safely serve.

**Action.** State full inclusion/exclusion criteria including any baseline HbA1c range, diabetes duration, comorbidity/medication constraints, age, language/digital-literacy requirements, the recruitment setting and dates, and any AI input-data-level criteria (e.g., supported languages, smartphone OS/app requirements).

*(Flagged by all four reviewers.)*

**Problem.** Only aggregate counts are given ("220 randomized, 198 completed"; ~10% attrition), with no CONSORT flow diagram, no numbers screened/excluded, no per-arm randomized/received-intervention/analysed counts, and no reasons for the 22 losses.

**Why it matters.** CONSORT-13a. The ~10% attrition could be differential between arms (plausible for an app-based, unblinded intervention); aggregate numbers hide differential dropout and uptake that could explain the result and bias the ITT estimate.

**Action.** Add a CONSORT flow diagram with per-arm numbers at each stage (randomized → received → completed → analysed) and documented reasons for exclusion and loss to follow-up by arm. Also report intervention adherence/engagement (e.g., the proportion who used the chatbot as intended).

*(Flagged by all four reviewers.)*

items.section_ref: Results / Methods — Analysis population & missing data (ITT vs completers)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** The Methods state an intention-to-treat analysis, yet the Results report only the 198 of 220 who "completed follow-up" (a completer set). The handling of the 22 participants with missing 8-week data, the analysed n per arm, the linear mixed model's missing-data assumption (MAR), and any sensitivity analyses are not described.

**Why it matters.** A true ITT analysis includes all randomized participants; a completer-only result contradicts the stated principle and can bias the 8-week HbA1c estimate if dropout is differential. The robustness of the primary conclusion depends on the unstated, untested missingness assumption.

**Action.** Reconcile the analysis population: confirm whether all 220 randomized were included (a mixed model can use all available data under MAR), report n analysed per arm, state the missing-data assumption, and present a sensitivity analysis for the primary outcome (e.g., pattern-mixture or tipping-point) to show the conclusion is not driven by the MAR assumption.

*(Raised distinctly by r3 and r4; also folded into r1/r2's flow items.)*

items.section_ref: Methods / Results — Primary outcome timing (8-week HbA1c)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** The primary outcome is change in HbA1c assessed at 8 weeks. HbA1c reflects average glycaemia over roughly the preceding ~8–12 weeks because erythrocyte lifespan is ~120 days, so an 8-week value has not fully equilibrated and substantially carries pre-intervention glycaemia.

**Why it matters.** An 8-week endpoint captures an incomplete, attenuated, lagged signal; conventional diabetes RCTs use a ≥12-week (commonly 12–24-week) primary endpoint. This threatens the construct validity of the primary outcome and may understate or misestimate the glycaemic effect, directly undercutting a glycaemic-efficacy conclusion drawn at 8 weeks.

**Action.** Justify the 8-week timing explicitly and acknowledge the HbA1c lag as a substantive limitation; report a ≥12-week HbA1c endpoint if available; and/or report a faster-responding complementary measure suited to the window (e.g., fructosamine or CGM-derived time-in-range) to support the early signal, tempering glycaemic-efficacy claims accordingly.

*(Flagged by r1, r3, r4.)*

**Problem.** The interactive LLM chatbot (frequent, responsive contact) is compared against static printed diabetes-education materials, with no stated control contact time.

**Why it matters.** Any benefit may arise from increased attention, engagement, and contact frequency rather than the LLM content specifically. The design therefore cannot isolate the "LLM effect" implied by the title and conclusions — a substantive internal-validity/attribution problem, not merely a description gap.

**Action.** Acknowledge the attention/engagement confound explicitly, reframe claims as "LLM chatbot vs printed materials," report the control's contact time so attention-matching can be judged, and discuss whether a digital or attention-matched comparator would be required to attribute any effect to the LLM itself.

*(Raised distinctly by r1; the attention-matching concern is echoed within the intervention items of r2 and r4.)*

**Problem.** Outcomes are not completely defined. The primary outcome "change in HbA1c" lacks the baseline timepoint, units, and measurement method/assay (central lab vs point-of-care). The secondary outcomes "medication adherence" and "treatment satisfaction" name no measurement instruments (e.g., a validated adherence measure such as MMAS or proportion of days covered; a named satisfaction scale), no scoring, and no assessment timing, and pre-specification is not stated.

**Why it matters.** CONSORT-6a requires completely defined, pre-specified outcome measures. Undefined or non-validated subjective measures — especially with unblinded participants — are vulnerable to measurement and selective-reporting/outcome-switching bias and cannot be interpreted or compared across studies, yet they underpin part of the favourable conclusion.

**Action.** For each outcome specify the exact metric, validated instrument and its scoring, assessment timepoints, and direction of benefit; name the HbA1c assay/lab; identify primary vs secondary; and confirm all outcomes were pre-specified (ideally against a registered protocol).

*(Flagged by all four reviewers; one rated it minor.)*

**Problem.** No trial registration number or registry name appears in the Abstract, Methods, or Declarations.

**Why it matters.** CONSORT-23 and ICMJE/JMIR policy require prospective registration of clinical trials. Its absence prevents readers from verifying that the primary outcome (HbA1c at 8 weeks) and analyses were pre-specified rather than selected after seeing the data, raises outcome-switching concerns, and is commonly grounds for editorial rejection.

**Action.** Add the registry name, registration ID, and registration date (e.g., ClinicalTrials.gov NCT… or another WHO-ICTRP registry) in the Methods and Declarations, and confirm outcomes were registered before enrollment. If registration was retrospective or absent, disclose this explicitly and justify it.

*(Flagged by all four reviewers; one rated it critical.)*

items.section_ref: Declarations — Protocol, AI/code & data access (CONSORT-24; CONSORT-AI-25)
items.content_md:
**Problem.** There is no statement of where the full protocol/SAP can be accessed (CONSORT-24), and no statement of whether or how the AI intervention or its code/prompts/configuration can be accessed, including restrictions (CONSORT-AI-25). Funding is described only as "an institutional research grant" with no grant number, and data availability is limited to "from the corresponding author on reasonable request."

**Why it matters.** Both protocol and AI-access statements are required for transparency and reproducibility; CONSORT-AI-25 is specific to AI trials where access governs replication and external validation. "On reasonable request" is increasingly discouraged by reproducibility policies, and vague funding/vendor disclosure can obscure dependencies or conflicts (e.g., a commercial LLM vendor).

**Action.** Add a protocol/SAP availability statement (supplement, registry record, or repository with a DOI); add an AI-access statement covering model/version, prompts/configuration, code, and any re-use restrictions; strengthen data availability toward a repository with a DOI where feasible; provide the specific funder and grant number; and reconfirm competing interests in light of any commercial model/vendor relationship.

*(Flagged by all four reviewers, all minor.)*

**Problem.** The title ("Efficacy of LLM-assisted counseling in type-2 diabetes") does not identify the study as a randomized controlled trial and does not specify the AI/model type; the word "Efficacy" also pre-asserts the conclusion.

**Why it matters.** CONSORT-1a requires identifying the study as a randomized trial in the title (aiding indexing, retrieval, and the MEDLINE publication type), and CONSORT-AI-1a requires indicating AI/ML involvement and the model type. Their absence reduces discoverability and sets inaccurate reader expectations.

**Action.** Revise to identify both the design and the model, e.g., "A large-language-model counseling chatbot for glycaemic control in type 2 diabetes: a randomized controlled trial," naming the AI/model class (and version) evaluated, and avoid the conclusion-asserting "efficacy."

*(Flagged by all four reviewers; one rated it minor.)*

**Problem.** The structured abstract reports no quantitative results (only "HbA1c improved in the chatbot arm"), omits the trial-design label and the AI/model type, omits trial-registration details, and ends with a definitive efficacy conclusion ("LLM-assisted counseling is effective").

**Why it matters.** Many readers and screeners act on the abstract alone; CONSORT-for-Abstracts and CONSORT-AI expect the design, AI specification, the key numeric result with uncertainty, and a conclusion calibrated to the data. An effect-free, over-stated abstract misrepresents the evidence.

**Action.** Add the primary between-arm HbA1c difference with 95% CI and analysed n; label the design as a randomized trial and name the AI/model; include the registration identifier; and revise the concluding sentence to match what the data show (see the Discussion/Conclusions item). This is the abstract-specific roll-up of the Results, Title, Registration, and Conclusion fixes above.

*(Raised distinctly by r1 and r3; abstract-specific elements also appear across r2/r4's items.)*
